  • FY26 production guidance unchanged vs Capital Markets Day (Dec 2025): Copper 810–870kt, Zinc 700–740kt, Nickel 70–80kt, Steelmaking coal 30–34mt, Energy coal 95–100mt.
  • Own-sourced production (Q1 2026 vs Q1 2025): Copper 199.6kt (+19%); Cobalt 5.8kt (-39%); Zinc 176.9kt (-17%); Lead 41.2kt (-17%); Nickel 17.2kt (-9%); Gold 68koz (-53%); Silver 4,869koz (+15%); Chrome ore 830kt (+3%); Steelmaking coal 6.5mt (-22%); Energy coal 22.9mt (-2%).
  • Copper +31.7kt YoY driven by improved grades at African copper (+27.4kt) and higher throughput/grades at Antamina (+13.5kt); partly offset by Mount Isa copper mine closure impact (-8.9kt).
  • Cobalt -3.7kt YoY due to DRC export quota system introduced late 2025: DRC assets prioritising copper as existing cobalt inventories sufficient for near-term quotas.
  • Zinc -36.7kt YoY mainly from Lady Loretta end of mine life (-22.3kt) and lower Kazzinc contribution (-13.8kt) due to own-sourced feedstock sequencing.
  • Nickel -1.6kt YoY due to 2025 Sudbury furnace disruption impacting matte shipment lead times to Norway.
  • Steelmaking coal -1.8mt YoY due to pit sequencing at EVR, wet weather (Queensland) and planned longwall move at Oaky Creek.
  • H2-weighted guidance drivers: Collahuasi (ore + desalinated water improving), steelmaking coal (Canada pit sequencing + Oaky Creek longwall move), energy coal (Ulan longwall move in H1; lower strip ratios at Bulga/Cerrejón in H2).
  • DRC cobalt update: quota system applies until at least end-2027; excess production stored in-country and sold “as circumstances allow”; may hold cobalt in solution/not reported as production to minimise processing costs.
  • DRC govt extended validity of 2025 quotas to April 2026; Glencore exported most of its 2025 quota in Q1, remainder exported in April 2026; unused Q1 2026 quotas valid until 30 June 2026.
  • Expected cobalt export quota allocation (kt): 2026 (incl. 2025 carryover) 22.8kt (KCC 16.1; Mutanda 6.7) vs 2027 18.8kt (KCC 13.3; Mutanda 5.5).

time_period: AGM update (Wednesday) covering 2025 actions and 2026–2027 merger/disposals timeline
body:
• At AGM, Chair Stuart Chambers described 2025 as “highly consequential” in the context of merger/disposal plans.
  • Merger plan: combine with Teck Resources to form “Anglo Teck”, positioned as “one of the world’s largest copper producers”; completion targeted later in 2026 or early 2027.
  • Governance: new board to be announced; Sheila Murray named as chair of Anglo Teck; Anglo and Teck each to nominate 50% of the non-executive board.
  • Chambers: “Despite the volatility in the world around us, we made great strides in 2025 to ensure that Anglo American is a more agile and resilient business...”
  • Strategic focus reiterated: copper, premium iron ore, and crop nutrients, aligned to “structural trends of decarbonisation, improving living standards, and food security”.
  • Portfolio progress cited: sale of Anglo American Platinum (now Valterra Platinum).
  • Disposals still in progress: steelmaking coal, nickel, and De Beers diamond operations.
  • CEO Duncan Wanblad on failed Peabody coal deal: “While we were very disappointed that Peabody decided not to complete... we expect that we will successfully reach an alternative sales agreement for value in 2026.”
  • Safety: Wanblad called the deaths of two employees in Brazil and Zimbabwe last year “simply unacceptable” and reiterated safety priority.
  • Chambers: “In a year characterised by volatile markets and slow economic recovery in China, and with weaker iron ore prices and cyclically low diamond prices, Anglo American delivered a stable operating and financial performance.”

• Secured UK court approval (Tuesday) for the proposed share capital reduction.
  • Reduction becomes effective once the court order is registered at Companies House (UK); expected in the coming weeks.
  • Plan includes cancellation of the share premium account and cancellation of certain shares in the company’s capital.

• Acquired York Designer Outlet (c.250,000 sq ft, 120 brands) just outside York city centre.
  • Acquired East Midlands Designer Outlet (c.170,000 sq ft, 65 brands) near the M1 motorway.
  • No financial details provided for either purchase.
  • CEO Michael Murray: “These strategic acquisitions reinforce our vision, leveraging strong partnerships with leading global brands to unlock mutual value - supporting their outlet strategies while driving growth,”
  • Murray: “Today, we own over one-fifth of the UK outlet market and have a clear ambition to grow our share further.”

• Adjusted EBITDA (excluding inventory liquidation and associated costs) expected towards the top end of guidance (GBP5.5m–GBP7.5m).
  • Revenue expected GBP200m (bottom end of GBP200m–GBP216m guidance), reflecting a shift to a smaller, more profitable core business.
  • Implied revenue decline of ~20.1% YoY (GBP200.0m vs GBP250.2m prior year).
  • Inventory “continues to improve” and is at its lowest level in 5 years.
  • Net cash +GBP3m YoY to GBP33.4m.
  • CEO Rodrigo Maza: “We go into FY27 with momentum and energised for what lies ahead for Naked,”

• Group revenue £2,920.2m, essentially flat vs £2,921.9m (-0.1%).
  • Revenue by geography: UK & Ireland £2,659.3m (vs £2,691.3m, -1.2%); Germany £260.9m (vs £230.6m, +13.1%).
  • Adjusted operating profit/(loss) £648.9m, up from £629.6m (+3.1%).
  • Profit before tax £298.4m, down from £367.8m (-18.9%), driven by higher adjusting items before tax £184.7m (vs £115.6m, +59.8m / +51.7%).
  • Segment adjusted profit before tax £483.1m, essentially flat vs £483.4m.
  • Capex ramp: property, plant & equipment additions (accruals basis) £707.6m (vs £465.3m, +52.1%); cash-basis PP&E capex £655.7m (vs £466.4m).
  • Impairment/AGP: net impairment charges recognised in operating costs £162.5m (vs £76.5m); includes Accelerating Growth Plan-related net impairment/write-offs/accelerated depreciation £130.5m (vs £43.5m).
  • Adjusting items guidance disclosed: forecast adjusting items before tax £75.0m–£110.0m (AGP-related net impairment/write-offs FY29; strategic F&B programme FY29; strategic IT programme FY27).
  • Net debt increased to £709.3m (from £483.4m, +£225.9m / +46.7%); cash reduced to £233.7m (from £909.0m).
  • Share buybacks: purchased and cancelled 8.8m shares; consideration paid £250.4m (incl fees/stamp duty).
  • Dividends: total equity dividends paid £168.8m (vs £177.8m, -5.1%); proposed final dividend 60.60p per share (£101.3m), subject to AGM approval.
  • Accounting / policy item flagged: intends to apply IASB May 2024 amendments to IFRS 9 retrospectively next year; expects £69.1m increase to 2026 year-end cash at bank and in hand related to committed payment runs (liability derecognition at settlement date).

time_period: Board changes effective 23 July 2026; departure planned end-March 2027
body:
• Andrew Sykes (Senior Independent Director) appointed Independent Non-Executive Director and Chair Designate of 3i Infrastructure plc, effective 23 July 2026.
  • Sykes to stand down from the ICG board end-March 2027, completing a nine-year tenure.
  • Disclosure made in accordance with UK Listing Rule 6.4.9.

• Alessandra Cozzani (Independent Non-Executive Director) appointed Independent Non-Executive Director of Brembo NV (listed on the Italian Stock Exchange), effective 29 April 2026.
  • Disclosure made under UK Listing Rule 6.4.9R(2).

time_period: Three months to March 2026 (Q1 trading update)
body:
• Confirmed full-year guidance: expects adjusted Ebita €435m at constant currency, or €400m after FX headwinds.
  • Q1 adjusted EBITDA +15% YoY (+46% at constant currency).
  • Q1 reported EBIT +25% YoY (+84% at constant currency).
  • Attributed improvement primarily to “sustained cost discipline and the continued benefits of management-led self-help measures,” implemented across 2025–2026.
  • End-market demand: refractory demand weakened in steel markets in Europe, Middle East and Latin America; other regions at expected levels; steel sales volumes broadly in line YoY.
  • Industrial demand: refractory demand decreased slightly in cement and industrial projects.
  • Middle East conflict: no material impact on the quarter overall, but depressed March sales volumes in the region.

• New business profit +10% YoY to $686m (from $625m) at constant exchange rates; new business profit margin 38% vs 36% (+2pp).
  • APE sales +5.2% YoY to $1.82bn (from $1.73bn) at CER.
  • CEO Anil Wadhwani: performance was “broad-based” with higher APE and improved margins, reflecting “our disciplined execution and continued focus on driving high-quality growth.”
  • Bancassurance: reported strong YoY growth in both volumes and margins, with continued traction in key markets.
  • Outlook: “We remain confident in delivering double-digit growth across our key financial metrics in 2026 and achieving our 2027 financial objectives,” (Wadhwani).
  • Regional notes: Hong Kong saw new business profit growth and margin expansion; mainland China JV (CITIC Prudential Life) sustained strong APE momentum from H2 2025; Malaysia saw new business profit growth driven by agency with higher bancassurance margins despite lower volumes; Indonesia modest new business profit growth; Singapore good APE growth.
  • Risk note: some smaller Asian businesses are more exposed to risk of higher inflation from higher energy prices, which “could impact consumer sentiment and buying behaviours in the future.”

• Q1 organic rental growth from rent reviews added £3m of income, representing +3.4% annualised growth (vs +3.2% comparative annualised rate).
  • Annualised contracted rent roll £345m at end-March, up from £342m (+0.9% YoY).
  • Said it continues to see an improving rental growth outlook, especially from rent reviews.
  • Reported “considerable progress” on creating a new vehicle for its private hospital portfolio, targeting a transaction to reduce gearing and provide an alternative capital/growth route; expects to announce a transaction during summer 2026.
  • Transferring a further £103m of assets into the primary care JV is in due diligence and expected to complete before end-July.
  • Cost synergies: achieved £7.8m of £9m target so far; started a rationalisation programme to rightsize the enlarged group.
  • CEO Mark Davies: “We have continued to make excellent progress on the delivery of the post Assura combination objectives… reducing leverage back to our target range of 40% to 50%, net debt/EBITDA below 9.5x, delivering £9 million of annualised cost synergies and integrating the two businesses, all of which we expect to complete ahead of schedule.”

• Net inflows £1.53bn, down from £1.69bn (-9.5% YoY).
  • Gross inflows £5.23bn, up from £5.14bn (+1.7% YoY).
  • Retention rate 95.3%, up from 95.0% (+0.3pp).
  • Funds under management £216.94bn at 31 March: up from £188.59bn (+15.0% YoY) but down from £220.01bn at 31 Dec (-1.4%).
  • Net investment return -£4.60bn vs -£3.1bn (larger negative return YoY).
  • Asset allocation: North American equities remain largest at 38%; UK equities 9% (fifth-largest allocation).
  • CEO Mark FitzPatrick: “While macroeconomic uncertainty continues, periods like this underscore the enduring value of high-quality financial advice,” and “Our advisers provide reassurance and help clients navigate market conditions, ensuring they remain focused on their long-term financial goals.”

• Q1 pretax profit +33% YoY to £2.03bn (from £1.52bn) on net income +9.1% to £4.79bn (from £4.39bn).
  • Said performance reflected “higher total income, controlled costs and benign impairments”.
  • Underlying profit +31% YoY to £2.01bn, ahead of consensus £1.89bn.
  • Underlying net interest income +8.5% YoY to £3.57bn (in line with consensus), with banking NIM 3.17% vs 3.03% (+14bps).
  • Upgraded structural hedge income expectation: now expects to exceed £7bn in 2026 (vs prior guidance of “around” £7bn).
  • Macro/rates: bank assumes no UK rate cut in 2026; expects one 25bp cut by end-2027 and stated: “Increases in energy prices lead to the re-emergence of inflationary pressures, with reductions in UK Bank Rate expected to be delayed until 2027,”.
  • Operating costs -3.1% YoY to £2.47bn; cost:income ratio improved to 51.9% (from 58.1%, -6.2pp).
  • EPS +41% YoY to 2.4p (from 1.7p).
  • Underlying impairment charge £295m (from £309m, -4.5%) and better than consensus £380m; asset quality ratio 25bps.
  • Multiple economic scenarios (MES) update drove a £101m charge, including £151m impact from deterioration in economic outlook due to Middle East conflict, partly offset by release of £50m previously held for tariff/political disruption risk.
  • Confirmed no change to motor finance provision.
  • RoTE improved to 17.0% (from 12.6%, +4.4pp); CET1 ratio 13.4% (from 13.5%, -10bps).
  • Customer loans £486.2bn; customer deposits £495.9bn (slightly lower).
  • Guidance: maintained most targets, but upgraded 2026 underlying net interest income guidance to exceed £14.9bn (vs prior “around” £14.9bn); still expects RoTE >16%, cost:income <50%, asset quality ratio ~25bps.
  • CEO Charlie Nunn: “In the first quarter of 2026, the group delivered sustained strength in financial performance, growing our income, maintaining our cost discipline and delivering strong profitability,” and “We are confident in our delivery for the year ahead,”; also said Lloyds will present a new strategy alongside half-year results.

time_period: First quarter of 2026 (Q1 trading update provided to AGM)
body:
• Said it “made a good start to the year” and kept 2026 guidance unchanged, while monitoring potential aerospace impacts from the Middle East conflict.
  • Q1 revenue +11% YoY: Engines +20%, Airframes +4%.
  • Adjusted operating profit for each division and the group was “well ahead” of the prior year, driven by growth plus improved margins.
  • Net debt and free cash flow were in line with expectations.
  • Middle East conflict: no operations in region and “minimal direct supply chain exposure”, but seeing some freight cost increases; watching for potential reduction in civil flying hours if jet fuel availability tightens.
  • FY2026 guidance reiterated (assuming no material war impact): adjusted operating profit £700m–£750m on revenue £3.75bn–£3.95bn; free cash flow after interest and tax £150m–£200m.
  • Profit and cash expected to be second-half weighted; H1 2026 results due 31 July 2026.

time_period: Buy-back to commence on the London business day immediately after completion of the ongoing £1,000m buy-back (started 11 February 2026) and end no later than 24 October 2026 (subject to regulatory approval remaining in place)
body:
• Will commence a new share buy-back of up to £500m (ordinary shares of 25p each), with shares bought to be cancelled to reduce share capital.
  • Buy-back to be executed by J.P. Morgan Securities plc as riskless principal (on-market purchases on LSE, then on-sold to Barclays).
  • JPM will make trading decisions independently of Barclays, within pre-set parameters and under the shareholder buy-back authority granted at the 7 May 2025 AGM (“2025 Authority”), plus relevant UK Listing Rules / MAR requirements.
  • No repurchases in the US and none in respect of Barclays’ ADRs.
  • Maximum shares that may be repurchased under this £500m programme are capped by remaining 2025 Authority: 1,436,786,392 authorised less completed buy-backs (262,093,958 and 107,319,865) and less shares repurchased under the ongoing £1,000m programme (started 11 Feb 2026) — i.e., 1,067,372,569 minus the ongoing programme’s purchases.

time_period: Event update (High Court confirmation hearing held 28 April 2026)
body:
• High Court of Justice approved Jubilee’s application to reduce the share premium account (capital reduction) at a confirmation hearing on 28 April 2026.
  • The capital reduction was approved by shareholders at the general meeting on 8 April 2026 (Notice of GM published 23 March 2026).
  • The capital reduction becomes effective upon registration of the court order and statement of capital with the Registrar of Companies.

time_period: Leasing update (contracts exchanged; no financial reporting period stated)
body:
• Exchanged contracts to lease 10,022 sq ft of fitted space at The Bower (Old Street).
  • Previously disclosed pipeline reiterated: 21,970 sq ft under offer; if completed, occupancy at The Bower would rise to 96.6%.
  • New letting: 3rd floor of The Tower let to a multinational mobility business, due to take occupation June 2026.
  • CEO Matthew Bonning-Snook: “We are thrilled to welcome another new occupier to our vibrant campus at The Bower where tech and AI businesses see great benefit in clustering to leverage agglomeration effects.

With two further floors under offer and regears agreed on an additional five floors, we look forward to announcing more positive progress over the coming weeks”

  • Advisors: JLL and Compton acted for Helical; Devono acted for the tenant.

• Sylvania Dump Operations (SDO) declared 22,853 4E (29,372 6E) PGM oz vs 24,642 4E (31,380 6E) in Q2 FY2026 (-1,789 4E / -7.3% QoQ); Eastern + Western Ops both exceeded business plan targets.
  • Thaba JV chrome ramp-up: chrome production 19,030 tons, up 80% vs 10,531 tons QoQ; post period-end operational/weather issues now negatively impacting full-year outlook.
  • Financial: SDO net revenue $78.7m, up 44% QoQ (Q2: $54.8m).
  • Financial: adjusted Group EBITDA $47.8m, up 61% QoQ (Q2: $29.8m).
  • Capital returns/liquidity: $2.0m on-market share buyback programme launched during the period; cash balance $63.3m, up 17% QoQ (Q2: $54.0m).
  • Dividend: interim dividend for FY2026 2.00p per share declared Feb 2026 and paid in April 2026 (post period-end).
  • Safety: operations were LTI-free during the quarter; milestones included Doornbosch five years total injury free; Lannex/Lesedi/Millsell five/six/four years LTI-free (as stated in the release).
  • Guidance: expects to achieve or exceed upper end of previously increased PGM production guidance (90,000–93,000 4E oz).
  • Guidance change: chrome concentrate production guidance revised to 50,000–55,000 tons (ROM feed quality and weather-related challenges in recent weeks).
  • CEO Jaco Prinsloo: “I am pleased to report the Company had another strong Quarter, especially at the SDO, with the production of 22,853 4E PGM ounces, which, due to the usual Christmas break, was a slight decrease from the record production achieved in Q2 and Q1 FY2026, but in line with expectations for the Period. Consequently, the Company is likely to achieve or exceed the upper end of the previously increased PGM production guidance of 90,000 to 93,000 4E PGM ounces, announced in the last quarter.

After successfully transitioning into production, the Thaba JV continues to build operational momentum, and the plant throughput and Chrome production for the past quarter were in line with the revised ramp-up plan communicated in February 2026. However, post Period-end, lower ROM feed tonnage and feed grade negatively impacted overall plant performance, while abnormally high rainfall during April 2026 has further impacted mining production volumes and plant throughput due to flooding and material handling challenges with the wet ore. As a result, chrome production guidance for FY2026 has been revised to between 50,000 and 55,000 tons. The focus remains on improving mine planning, scheduling optimisation, and mining standards to improve ROM feed quality, plant feed stability and throughput, and enhancing processing efficiencies as we progress towards steady-state operations.

In April, a tragic incident occurred at the Tweefontein Operation, whereby a Phoenix Security Officer, a contractor for the Company, lost his life during a criminal attack while on duty. The Board of Sylvania sends its heartfelt condolences to the individual's family, friends and colleagues.

We are committed to the safety and security of our employees and contractors and, during the Quarter, recorded zero LTIs across the SDO and Thaba JV Operations. We also achieved several key safety milestones, including Doornbosch reaching five years Total Injury free, Lannex six years LTI-free, Lesedi three years LTI-free and Millsell one-year Total Injury free and four years LTI-free.

From a financial perspective, the average 4E gross basket price increased by 28% in USD and 23% in Rand ("ZAR") terms, and this, together with the increased contribution from attributable Chrome sales of $4.0 million, resulted in an improved net revenue performance of $78.7 million (Q2 FY2026: $54.8 million).

The adjusted Group EBITDA for the Quarter rose to $47.8 million (Q2 FY2026: $29.8 million), which is a significant 61% increase quarter-on-quarter, and similarly to revenue, increased as a result of the increased gross 4E basket price and Chrome sales contribution.”

company: Aston Martin Lagonda Global Holdings PLC
body:
• Delivered Q1 performance in line with guidance; core retail volumes “significantly ahead” of wholesale, plus 102 Valhalla deliveries.
  • Total wholesale volumes 939 vs 950 (-1% YoY); core wholesales (ex-Specials) 836 vs 936 (-11%), reflecting “disciplined” production vs demand balance.
  • Revenue £270.4m, up 16% (driven primarily by higher Specials volumes vs Q1 2025).
  • Total ASP £252k, up 17%; core ASP £179k, down 7% (cited as targeted dealer support to reduce aged stock, as guided).
  • Gross margin 34.7% (mid-30s), up 680 bps YoY; gross profit £93.9m, up 44%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA £23.2m vs £(4.4)m; adjusted EBITDA margin 8.6% vs (1.9%).
  • Adjusted EBIT £(56.9)m vs £(64.5)m (12% improvement); operating loss £(8.9)m vs £(67.3)m; loss before tax £(65.5)m vs £(79.6)m.
  • Adjusted net financing costs £57m vs £15m; increase primarily from non-cash USD debt revaluations.
  • Free cash outflow £116.8m vs £120.3m (slight improvement); capex £61.0m vs £89.8m (lower investment, focused on future product pipeline).
  • End-Q1 liquidity (cash + available facilities) £177.7m (vs £387.2m at Q1 2025; vs £250m at 31 Dec 2025), partly offset by £50m gross proceeds from completed sale of Aston Martin F1 naming rights to AMR GP in Q1.
  • New £50m committed facility agreed with certain members of the Yew Tree Consortium; together with naming-rights proceeds, pro forma liquidity at end-Q1 stated as ~£230m (facility subject to customary closing conditions, incl. Listing Rules compliance).
  • Net debt £1,459.2m vs £1,267.4m (+15% YoY); vs £1,380.3m at 31 Dec 2025 (increase driven by lower cash + slightly higher gross debt/FX effects). Adjusted net leverage 10.8x (vs 5.1x at 31 Mar 2025).
  • Guidance unchanged; key FY26 planks reiterated: wholesale volumes similar to FY25 (5,448) with ~500 Valhalla deliveries, more balanced production cadence from Q2, gross margin into high 30s%, adjusted EBIT margin “materially improve” toward breakeven, capex ~£300m (down from £341m), and free cash outflow materially improve vs FY25 (£410m outflow).
  • Highlighted policy/risk dynamic: UK-to-US tariff quota mechanism (from Q1 2026) — up to 100,000 UK vehicles per calendar year at 10% tariff, above that at 27.5%; 25,000 per quarter available at the lower rate on a “first come first served” basis; Q1 required careful management of US imports at period-end.
  • CEO Adrian Hallmark: “Q1 2026 confirms that we are on track to deliver material financial improvement this year. In line with our full year guidance, Q1 2026 total wholesale volumes were similar to the prior year, while gross margin increased into the mid-30s driven by Valhalla deliveries and the benefits of our transformation programme.

Whilst we remain mindful of the uncertain global macroeconomic and geopolitical context, including the current conflict in the Middle East, we are focused on executing our strategy and achieving our unchanged 2026 full year guidance. Further financial improvement is expected through the rest of the year as we benefit from our expanding core model range, continued Valhalla deliveries following terrific recent five-star driving reviews and ongoing operational discipline.”

• End-market demand: global steel and industrial refractories demand “slightly weaker” vs prior year; regional variation (steel demand weaker in Europe / Middle East / LATAM; other regions “at expected levels”).
  • Group volumes/pricing: steel sales volumes broadly in line with Q1 2025 with moderate pricing benefits; industrial refractory demand decreased slightly (cement + industrial projects).
  • Profitability: Adjusted EBITA +~15% YoY (+46% constant currency); reported EBIT +25% YoY (+84% constant currency), driven by pricing/cost discipline/network optimisation/supply-chain agility self-help.
  • Regional profitability mix: strong North America and LATAM contributions; Europe & CIS underdelivered mainly from volume shifts from Q1 to Q2 (with potential to improve through cost reduction + early industrial recovery signs, esp. non-ferrous).
  • France footprint review (end-Q1): potential closure of one plant and conversion of another into a circular economy site (Network Optimisation Programme Europe).
  • Middle East conflict: no material impact in Q1, but depressed March volumes regionally; shipments redirected via alternative routes; service levels maintained; Q2 order intake remains robust.
  • Inflation pass-through: conflict-related inflation pressures being mitigated via surcharges (energy/freight/raw materials).
  • Balance sheet/work cap: net debt increased vs year-end 2025 due to planned inventory build ahead of anticipated stronger Q2 (expected to unwind through the year; described as seasonal).
  • FY26 guidance reconfirmed: adjusted EBITA ~€435m (constant currency) / ~€400m including FX headwinds.
  • Cash / leverage: expects cash conversion >90% and further deleveraging to ~2.6x by year-end; M&A remains core but no significant 2026 cash outflows anticipated.
  • CEO Stefan Borgas: “Despite a turbulent global economy in Q1, our performance demonstrates continued progress. Demand remains resilient overall, though slightly disappointing in major steel markets and with regional variations as expected. We have restored margins to acceptable levels, reflecting disciplined execution of our self-help measures.

We have also identified further opportunities in digitisation, plant network optimisation and SG&A efficiency, which are expected to support continued improvement and provide momentum over the next 18-24 months.

Our upgraded 4PRO full solutions value propositions to selected customers show encouraging feedback from leading, sophisticated customers in their markets.

We remain highly responsive to the evolving geopolitical backdrop, actively managing our supply chain to optimise costs and pricing. The increasing operational flexibility of our resilient Local-for-Local model, combined with rapidly advancing digital capabilities, further strengthens this adaptability.

Looking ahead, we remain confident that our proactive measures position us to manage ongoing market volatility.”

• Total revenue £83.8m, up 8.0% (Q1 2025: £77.6m); like-for-like revenue £82.8m, up 8.2%.
  • RevPAR £100.0, up 4.9%, driven by average room rate £142.9 (up 4.6%) and occupancy 70.0% (up 20 bps).
  • Like-for-like RevPAR £100.5, up 3.8%, reflecting average room rate £142.2 (up 3.2%) and occupancy 70.7% (up 40 bps).
  • Performance drivers: London portfolio delivered revenue and rate growth with stable occupancy; Netherlands “more challenging” due to higher VAT rate on room accommodation effective January 2026 (anticipated to suppress RevPAR growth); Germany delivered RevPAR growth via occupancy and rate; stronger EUR/GBP was a tailwind.
  • Agreed sale of New York development site for $33.5m, with proceeds intended for redeployment into core geographies; expected to close in the summer.
  • Agreed acquisition of freehold of Park Plaza London Waterloo for £147.9m, funded by a new £136.5m facility with Bank Hapoalim; transaction expected to close “in the coming months”.
  • Noted the hotel had been sold in a 2017 sale-and-leaseback for £161.5m; rationale for re-acquisition: increase freehold exposure, protect against expected material future rental uplifts, and “simplifying and de-risking” the balance sheet.
  • Refinance: new five-year €27.6m facility for art’otel Rome Piazza Sallustio.
  • Like-for-like methodology: excludes 2026 results from art’otel Rome Piazza Sallustio and 2025 results from the terminated leasehold of Park Plaza Wallstreet Berlin Mitte.
  • Outlook: Board “confident” portfolio investments and new openings support further revenue growth in 2026+; FY26 expected in line with market expectations.
  • Company-cited FY26 analyst consensus ranges (as at 29 April 2026): revenue £472.8m–£489.0m; EBITDA £143.8m–£147.5m.
  • Co-CEO Greg Hegarty: “We are pleased to report an encouraging start to 2026. We have made good progress, delivering total revenue and RevPAR growth in what is the quietest quarter of the year.

Our diversified, domestically focused, European portfolio is well placed against the backdrop of an uncertain political and fiscal environment. We are focused on maximising the financial potential of our recent hotel openings and repositioning properties. These factors, alongside the transactions announced in the quarter which have simplified and de-risked the Group's balance sheet, position the Group well for the future.

We remain confident that the Group will further build on its performance in 2025, with a growing contribution from recent investments and new openings and will deliver further growth in revenue in 2026.”

• Gross inflows £5.23bn vs £5.14bn (+£0.09bn / +1.8% YoY).
  • Net inflows £1.53bn vs £1.69bn (-£0.16bn / -9.5% YoY).
  • Closing funds under management (FUM) £216.94bn vs £188.59bn (+£28.35bn / +15.0% YoY); noted that “decline in global markets during the quarter impacted our FUM”.
  • FUM retention rate (annualised) 95.3% vs 95.0% (+0.3pp YoY).
  • Net inflows / opening FUM (annualised) 2.8% vs 3.6% (-0.8pp YoY).
  • CEO Mark FitzPatrick: “I am pleased to report a good first quarter for SJP. Against a backdrop of heightened geopolitical uncertainty and market volatility in the run-up to the tax year end, we attracted gross inflows of £5.2 billion and achieved strong funds under management (FUM) retention of 95.3%. Together, these delivered net inflows of £1.5 billion. The decline in global markets during the quarter impacted our FUM, which closed the period at £216.9 billion.

While macroeconomic uncertainty continues, periods like this underscore the enduring value of high-quality financial advice. Our advisers provide reassurance and help clients navigate market conditions, ensuring they remain focused on their long-term financial goals. The strength and quality of our Partnership, together with the trusted relationships our advisers build with clients, position us well to continue to grow and capture the significant long-term market opportunity.”

• FY26 trading in line with prior guidance; adjusted EBITDA (ex-inventory liquidation) expected towards the top end of the guided range.
  • Revenue expected c.£200m (guided £200m–£216m), reflecting strategy to focus on a smaller, more profitable core.
  • Inventory “continues to improve” and is at its lowest level in 5 years.
  • Net cash (ex-lease liabilities) +£3m to £33.4m: £9m cash generation partially offset by £6m share buybacks.
  • Pricing: price increases had a “meaningful impact” on profitability (notably Q4 FY26) and are expected to have a greater impact in FY27 as they annualise.
  • Cost programme: £25m of annualised savings actioned, exceeding the March 2025 £23m 3–5 year target.
  • Savings mix/timing: includes c.£5m annual G&A savings (impacting FY27 onwards) from Zero-Based Budgeting begun in Q4 FY26; c.£10m of savings actioned late FY26 and will largely impact FY27.
  • Tech platform shift: moving from legacy in-house architecture to a third-party SaaS platform to improve site performance, marketing effectiveness, conversion, and resilience/security; expected to generate significant future cost savings.
  • Transition costs expected to be offset by newly identified c.£5m annual G&A savings; additional up to £5m annualised opex savings expected by late FY29 (not included in the £25m above) → implies up to £10m total annualised savings vs FY27 by FY29/30.
  • FY26 accounts to include c.£2m–£3m non-cash adjusted item relating to previously capitalised development costs; capitalised tech dev spend for FY27–FY30 now expected c.£1m vs up to £7m previously guided.
  • CEO Rodrigo Maza: “We are pleased to have delivered a strong year in FY26, which reflects substantial progress with the new strategy set out last March. The decisions we made during the year will materially improve our profitability over the periods to come, as we continue to build a stronger and more resilient business which will then grow.

“We go into FY27 with momentum and energised for what lies ahead for Naked.”

  • FY27 guidance to be provided with audited FY26 results in the Summer.

• CEO Mark Davies: “The first quarter of 2026 was a strong start to the year, with a continued focus on asset management and risk-controlled development, with organic rental growth from the enlarged portfolio's rent reviews delivering an extra £3 million of income, an increase of c.6% or 3.4% on an annualised basis (up from 3.2% in 2025).

“We have continued to make excellent progress on the delivery of the post Assura combination objectives, since we announced PHP's latest results in March. These include reducing leverage back to our target range of 40% to 50%, net debt/EBITDA below 9.5x, delivering £9 million of annualised cost synergies and integrating the two businesses, all of which we expect to complete ahead of schedule. We have made considerable progress in the period in establishing a new vehicle for our private hospital portfolio."

  • Rent reviews: extra £3m income from 199 completed reviews; total increase c.6% vs prior rent of £54m; equates to 3.4% annualised (2025: 3.2%).
  • Rental uplift by area: Primary Care UK +2.9%; Private Hospitals +3.7%; Ireland +4.4%.
  • Annualised contracted rent roll £345m (2025: £342m).
  • Post-Assura priorities: targeting leverage 40%–50% and net debt/EBITDA <9.5x; integration and £9m annualised cost synergies expected ahead of schedule.
  • Private hospital “vehicle” to reduce gearing: shortlist of counterparties engaged; Board targeting transaction to reduce gearing / provide alternative capital; expects an announcement during summer 2026.
  • JV pipeline: transfer of a further £103m of assets into existing primary care JV (with USS) progressing; expected completion before end-July 2026.
  • Cost synergy delivery: £7.8m annualised delivered (c. 87% of £9m target); rightsizing programme commenced.
  • Development: six schemes on site (2 UK primary care in USS JV, 3 Ireland, 1 private hospital) on track for completion during 2026–2027.
  • Neighbourhood Health Centres: DHSC announced first wave end-March 2026; three are existing PHP assets; programme targets 250 centres by 2035.
  • Dividend: quarterly interim dividend 1.825p (PID 1.325p + normal 0.5p) payable 8 May 2026; annualised 7.3p, +2.8% vs 2025; 30th consecutive year of dividend growth.

• Q1 new business profit (TEV NBP) $686m (+10% CER).
  • Q1 APE sales $1,823m (+6% CER).
  • New business margin 38% (up 2 ppts vs 36%).
  • CEO Anil Wadhwani: “In the first three months of 2026, we once again demonstrated our continued delivery of double-digit new business profit growth. Performance was broad-based across segments, with higher APE sales and improved new business margins, reflecting our disciplined execution and continued focus on driving high-quality growth.

“The quarter reinforced the strength of our multi-channel, multi-market business model, with resilient performance despite ongoing market volatility and geopolitical uncertainty. Similar to the outcome in full-year 2025, bancassurance delivered strong year-on-year growth in both volumes and margins, with continued traction across key markets. We continue to progress our agency transformation programme with a focus on quality recruitment and actions to improve agent productivity, including the rollout of enhanced digital tools. The agency channel continued to grow new business profit in the first quarter.

“Through disciplined value creation, continued strengthening of our distribution and a focus on enhancing customer experience we are well positioned to capture structural growth opportunities across Asia and Africa. We remain confident in delivering double-digit growth across our key financial metrics in 2026 and achieving our 2027 financial objectives."

  • Segment colour: Hong Kong / Mainland China / Malaysia delivered double-digit new business profit growth; CITIC Prudential Life APE momentum continued (with margin moderation due to portfolio rebalance toward participating business).
  • Eastspring: net inflows in the period; FUM $268.9bn at 31 March 2026 (vs $277.7bn at 31 Dec 2025), decline largely from market and FX movements.
  • Capital return: $1.2bn buyback launched in January 2026 (includes $500m recurring returns + $700m net proceeds from the IPO of ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company); Q1 repurchased ~20m shares for $312m.

• Global comparable store sales +6.2%, driven by transactions +3.8% and average ticket +2.3%.
  • North America comparable store sales +7.1% (U.S. +7.1%), driven by transactions +4.4% (U.S. +4.3%) and ticket +2.6% (U.S. +2.7%).
  • International comparable store sales +2.6% (China +0.5%): China transactions +2.1% offset by average ticket -1.6%.
  • Consolidated net revenues +9% to $9.5bn (+8% constant currency).
  • GAAP operating margin +180 bps to 8.7%; Non-GAAP operating margin +120 bps to 9.4%.
  • Effective tax rate 29.8% (vs 23.5% prior year); Non-GAAP effective tax rate +340 bps to 27.1%.
  • EPS: GAAP diluted EPS $0.45 (+32%); Non-GAAP EPS $0.50 (+22%).
  • Store base: +11 net new stores in Q2 to 41,129 total (52% company-operated / 48% licensed); U.S. 16,944 stores, China 7,991.
  • North America segment: net revenues +7% to $6.9bn; operating income -9% to $679.9m; operating margin 9.9% (down from 11.6%), with pressure from labor investments and inflation (tariffs / elevated coffee pricing).
  • International segment: net revenues +10% to $2.1bn; operating income +84% to $398.6m; operating margin 19.4% (from 11.6%), helped by lower store costs after classifying Starbucks China retail ops as held for sale and ceasing related D&A.
  • Channel Development: net revenues +39% to $567.8m; operating margin 40.5% (from 47.3%), citing lower JV income vs revenue growth and mix.
  • CEO Brian Niccol: “Our second quarter marked the turn in our turnaround as our Back to Starbucks plan drove both top and bottom line growth…”
  • CFO Cathy Smith: “We’ve been clear that topline improvement would come first, with earnings growth to follow…”
  • China transaction update: funds managed by Boyu Capital now hold 60% of Starbucks China retail ops; Starbucks retains 40% and continues to own and license the brand/IP; impact expected to begin reporting from Q3.
  • Dividend declared $0.62/share payable 29 May 2026 (record 15 May 2026).
  • FY26 guidance updated: Global and U.S. comp sales growth 5.0%+; consolidated net revenues roughly flat YoY; Non-GAAP operating margin to slightly improve YoY; Non-GAAP EPS $2.25–$2.45; ~600–650 net new coffeehouses (guidance reflects China JV licensee structure in 2H FY26).

• UBS downgrades to ‘neutral’ from ‘buy’ (cutting the ‘buy’ around ~2 months after upgrading).
  • UBS cuts price target to 180p (from 245p).
  • Rationale: “increasing risks to volumes in the travel retail industry linked to the Middle East conflict.”
  • UBS notes its prior upgrade was based on improving cash flow generation (lower capex requirements) and an improving aviation volume backdrop, but adds: “This is supportive for the longer term but in the context of the continued uncertainty linked to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East we see limited scope for the market to turn more positive on the name.”
  • UBS flags forward-looking capacity has fallen vs prior forecasts, implying more challenging near-term growth.
  • Upside scenario: “A quick end to the conflict and a return to fuel prices seen before the outbreak” could quickly improve investor sentiment.
  • Downside scenario: the longer the conflict continues, the “greater the risk of disruption to volumes” and the greater potential impact on the seasonally important summer season.

• CEO Charlie Nunn: “In the first quarter of 2026, the Group delivered sustained strength in financial performance, growing our income, maintaining our cost discipline and delivering strong profitability. Our differentiated business model remains resilient in the context of the current economic uncertainties. We remain focused on supporting UK households and businesses as they look to strengthen their financial positions and achieve their goals.

We are building strategic momentum during the final year of our current plan, providing innovative ways for our customers to manage their financial needs and achieve their financial aspirations. We are confident in our delivery for the year ahead and reiterate our guidance for 2026. We look forward to presenting our new strategy alongside the half-year results.”

  • Statutory profit before tax £2.0bn vs £1.5bn, up £0.5bn (+33%) YoY, “benefitting from higher total income”; return on tangible equity 17.0%.
  • Underlying net interest income £3.6bn, up 8% YoY.
  • Banking net interest margin 3.17%, up 14bp YoY and 7bp vs Q4; driven by “strong structural hedge income” plus “franchise led volume growth”.
  • Average interest-earning banking assets £473.5bn, up 4% YoY.
  • Underlying other income £1.6bn, up 11% YoY, driven by higher customer activity and benefits from strategic initiatives.
  • Operating lease depreciation £389m, up 10% YoY; driven by fleet growth, higher-value vehicle depreciation and lower used car prices, “partially offset by risk mitigation actions”.
  • Operating costs £2.5bn, down 3% YoY; reflects higher cost savings and lower severance, partly offset by growth costs, inflation and Lloyds Wealth (Schroders Personal Wealth).
  • Remediation costs £11m across “a small number of pre-existing rectification programmes”.
  • Underlying impairment charge £295m; asset quality ratio 25bp; includes a £101m net charge from updated multiple economic scenarios.
  • Underlying loans and advances to customers £486.2bn, up £5.1bn (+1%) in the quarter; growth across Retail +£3.5bn and Commercial Banking +£2.8bn.
  • Customer deposits £495.9bn, down £0.6bn in the quarter; Retail -£3.1bn partly offset by Commercial Banking +£2.3bn; fixed-term deposits “fell slightly given Group participation decisions”.
  • Capital generation 41bp in Q1; CET1 ratio 13.4% (after ordinary dividend accrual).
  • Risk-weighted assets £240.8bn, up £5.3bn in Q1, largely from lending growth; “limited planned optimisation”.
  • Tangible net assets per share 57.9p at 31 March 2026, up 0.9p in the quarter (vs 57.0p at 31 December 2025).
  • 2026 guidance reiterated (based on “sustained strength” and current macro assumptions): Underlying NII > £14.9bn.
  • 2026 guidance reiterated: Cost:income ratio < 50% (including operating costs < £9.9bn).
  • 2026 guidance reiterated: Asset quality ratio c.25bp.
  • 2026 guidance reiterated: Return on tangible equity > 16%.
  • 2026 guidance reiterated: Capital generation > 200bp (excluding capital distributions).
  • 2026 guidance reiterated: To pay down to CET1 ratio c.13.0%.

• Group like-for-like (“LfL”) sales grew 4.8% (Retail LfL +4.1%; Autocentres (ex-Avayler) LfL +5.8%).
  • Within Retail: Motoring LfL +2.9%; Cycling LfL +6.4%.
  • FY26 underlying profit before tax (“PBT”) expected around the upper end of the £36.0m–£41.2m consensus range, driven by further gross margin expansion and well managed costs.
  • Balance sheet ended the year in a net cash position; Group “continues to be cash generative”.
  • Management comment on near-term trading/macros: “While the conflict in the Middle East is contributing to an uncertain macroeconomic backdrop, trading in March and April has been in-line with expectations.”
  • Risk management: “The majority of FY27 energy costs and FX requirements are hedged with freight rates largely contracted in advance,” supporting visibility and mitigating near-term volatility.
  • FY27: “we are currently comfortable with consensus expectations” for underlying PBT of £42.0m to £48.6m.
  • Next update: FY26 preliminary results scheduled for 25 June 2026.

time_period: Year ended 31 March 2026 (FY26); FY27 outlook incl. Summer 2026 booking/trading update
body:
• Expects FY26 Operating profit of £435m–£440m (FY25: £446.5m), in line with market expectations (c. -1.5% to -2.6% YoY).
  • FY26 profit guidance includes £11m of promotional + resourcing startup costs for the new London Gatwick base (operations commenced 26 March 2026).
  • Balance sheet: total cash £3.3bn at 31 March 2026; net cash £2.0bn; £500m revolving credit facility undrawn.
  • Capital returns in FY26: £363.0m returned to shareholders.
  • Strategic footprint: entry into London Gatwick (UK’s biggest holiday airport); now 14 UK bases; claims >90% of British public within a 90-minute drive of a Jet2 base.
  • Summer 2026 on-sale capacity: +7.7% vs Summer 2025 to 19.9m seats.
  • Booked-to-date passengers: +6.2% YoY; both package holidays and flight-only showing positive growth.
  • Since the Middle East conflict began, bookings have skewed closer to departure; Q1 (Apr–Jun) average load factor in line YoY, but geopolitical uncertainty is limiting visibility for peak summer and beyond.
  • Continues to invest in load factor and remains committed to “pricing that is attractive and represents real value”.
  • Fuel hedging: 87% of summer requirement hedged; jet fuel swaps at average $707 per metric tonne, providing high cost certainty; maintaining frequent dialogue with suppliers/airport partners on fuel supply.
  • Preliminary Results for FY26 scheduled for 8 July 2026.
  • CEO quote (Steve Heapy):“FY26 was another strong year for Jet2, topped off by the successful launch of operations at London Gatwick which is performing ahead of our initial expectations with over 0.4m passengers booked for the summer season. As ever, our focus on providing the very best Customer First service underpinned our performance in the year, and with that, I would like to thank every one of our Colleagues for their unwavering hard work and support.

Our fully integrated, customer-focused and service-led business model enables growth and resilience, setting the business apart when it comes to earning customer loyalty and repeat bookings. This is supported by our growing fleet of more fuel efficient and quieter A321neo aircraft, with 31 in operation this summer.

Clearly, we continue to monitor the situation in the Middle East but remain focused on our medium-term goals. Jet2 is a business with strong fundamentals, an attractive product offer, and a brand synonymous with VIP customer service. These are the attributes that sit at the heart of our People, Service Profits philosophy and give me confidence for long-term profitable growth as more and more customers rely on Jet2 for their hard-earned holidays.”

time_period: Year ended 31 December 2025 (Annual Report) / 2026 AGM materials
body:
• Confirms Annual Report (FY25), Chairman letter, 2026 AGM notice and proxy have been made available for inspection on the company website, and submitted to Euronext Dublin and the UK National Storage Mechanism.
  • Documents available in ESEF-compliant and PDF formats.
  • Provides inspection locations/portals for Euronext Dublin and the UK National Storage Mechanism.

time_period: FY26 (year ended 31 January 2026) + early FY27 trading (first 3 months)
body:
• Revenue £582.7m (+7.4% YoY).
  • EBITDA £116.8m (-8.4% YoY).
  • Profit before tax £43.9m (-31.5% YoY).
  • Adjusted PBT £56.0m (-15.2% YoY) — delivered in line with revised guidance.
  • Adjusted free cash flow £40.7m (+41.2% YoY) with 98.9% conversion of adjusted earnings (above target range).
  • Basic EPS 9.0p (-35.0% YoY); Adjusted EPS 11.8p (-17.3% YoY).
  • Dividend per share 5.0p (+4.2% YoY); proposed final dividend 3.7p (AGM 25 June 2026; pay date 3 July 2026; record date 29 May 2026).
  • Balance sheet: net debt (ex leases) £67.9m (+15.1% YoY); adjusted leverage (ex leases) 1.0x (from 0.7x; +0.3x), after Funky Pigeon acquisition, dividends and buyback.
  • £15m share buyback to commence in FY27 (subject to AGM authority renewal); company expects it to enhance EPS.
  • CEO Darcy Willson-Rymer quote (strategy + conditions): "Despite a challenging consumer backdrop in FY26, we continued to execute our strategy to transform cardfactory into a global celebrations group..." and "Softer high street footfall in the second half, particularly during our peak trading period, impacted full-year performance..."
  • Store LFL split: H1 +1.3%, H2 -1.7% (footfall-driven), driving full-year outcome.
  • Like-for-like store sales -0.2% (higher basket values from pricing/mix, offset by fewer transactions).
  • Store transactions -3.7%; average basket value +3.5%.
  • Stores: total store sales +1.5%, supported by +27 net new stores; estate 1,117 stores at 31 Jan 2026.
  • Category LFL: cards -0.9% (dragging gifting -1.9%); celebration essentials +1.7%.
  • Wholesale partnerships revenue £47.2m (FY25: £22.2m), reflecting annualisation of Garven and Garlanna; performed in line with expected acquisition economics.
  • Digital sales £20.6m, including £13.5m from Funky Pigeon; YoY decline; Getting Personal closed as focus shifts to profitability.
  • Funky Pigeon acquisition (completed Aug 2025) cash consideration £25.7m (+ transaction costs); targeted synergies £5m from FY28; separation from WH Smith completed; operating model underway toward single platform + optimised fulfilment.
  • Simplify & Scale: delivered £21m of benefits in FY26, including 9% store efficiency improvement; programme positioned to structurally lower costs from FY27.
  • FY26 capex £19.4m (below target range) including investment in new store PoS; FY27 capex expected upper end of £20–25m (digital integration + manufacturing investment), implying FY27 free cash conversion towards lower end of target.
  • Non-underlying FY26 items called out (impacting reported vs adjusted): acquisition-related costs £3.8m, intangible asset write-off £3.2m (digital assets to become obsolete), unrealised derivative losses £4.7m, restructuring/transformation £0.4m.
  • FX/hedging: goods ~half purchased in USD; average delivered USD rate 1.2842 (FY25: 1.2589); FY27 currency requirements 100% hedged and energy 80% hedged.
  • Current trading: first 3 months of FY27 sales in line YoY excluding incremental Funky Pigeon benefit.
  • Middle East risk: company flagged potential impact on container rates, energy and fuel surcharges, and broader inflation/consumer sentiment uncertainty.
  • FY27 outlook: Board expects Adjusted PBT in line with market consensus (company-compiled range £54.8m–£60.5m, average £58.2m, excluding an outlier); PBT delivery expected weighted to H2.
  • Medium-term ambition reiterated: mid-to-high single digit % Adjusted PBT growth per annum over the medium term.

time_period: Ongoing (Middle East war-driven fuel shock; schedule actions cited for May–October and through October)
body:
• Closure of the Strait of Hormuz described as materially tightening oil supply and pushing jet fuel prices sharply higher, prompting airlines to cut capacity.
  • Consumer-facing commentary (Travel Therapy TV host Karen Schaler) quote: "Travel alert: airlines are cutting thousands of flights right now... Book early."
  • Analyst comment (Goodbody’s Dudley Shanley): "It is not unusual for carriers to adjust their schedules at this time of the year," but if jet fuel stays elevated, "there will have to be a little bit more trimming for low-cost airlines".
  • EU Energy Commissioner Dan Jorgensen (Sky News) quote: "Unfortunately, it's very likely that many people's holidays will be affected, either by flight cancellations or very, very expensive tickets."
  • Air Transat cut 6% of its May–October schedule.
  • AirAsia X: earlier said fares up to 40% and about 10% of flights cut so far; further cuts announced without a total figure.
  • Lufthansa: announced 20,000 flights cut through October, and halting regional feeder CityLine (per excerpt).
  • Air France-KLM: trimmed 2% of flights in May and June at Transavia; KLM cancellations held to ~1% of European flights.
  • Ryanair: did not cite fuel directly in Berlin cuts (from October), pointing to high costs/taxes; also cutting 10% of flights from Dublin (airport capacity criticism).
  • Volotea: trimmed ~1% of flights from its summer schedule since the beginning of the month.
  • Wizz Air: CEO Jozsef Varadi quote on not cutting capacity: "We are not taking capacity out, because I think the other guys will take capacity out... You don't have to run faster than the bear, but faster than the guy next to you."

• Underlying revenue growth +3.7% YoY in the 16 weeks (UK +3.5%, International +9.1%).
  • Adjusted for trading days: period had two fewer trading days vs 2025, worth ~GBP11m.
  • Same-depot basis: underlying revenue +2.8% (UK +2.6%, International +7.9%).
  • Seasonality: peak trading in autumn; sales weighted to H2.
  • Outlook: "on track with the outlook for 2026".
  • Supply chain: said “near-sourced” model is not being affected by the US-Israeli war on Iran; quote: "Our near-sourced, vertically integrated business model is resilient across all macro-economic conditions," and supply chain remains “robust”.
  • Half-year results scheduled for 23 July.

• Underlying replacement cost profit USD3.20bn (vs USD1.38bn; >2x YoY) vs consensus USD2.67bn (beat).
  • Segment underlying RC profit: Gas & Low Carbon Energy USD1.34bn (from USD0.997bn); Oil Production & Operations USD1.98bn (from USD2.90bn).
  • Customers & Products underlying RC profit USD3.20bn (from USD0.677bn; ~4.7x YoY) vs consensus USD2.47bn, citing stronger midstream + higher margins.
  • BP quote: "The oil trading contribution for the first quarter was exceptional compared to the average result in the same period last year."
  • Pretax profit USD7.37bn (vs USD3.13bn); profit attributable USD3.84bn (from USD0.687bn).
  • Revenue USD52.26bn (+11% YoY).
  • Operating cash flow USD2.86bn (vs USD2.83bn) after a USD6.0bn adjusted working capital build (rising oil prices + seasonal inventory builds).
  • Net debt USD25.31bn (down vs USD26.97bn YoY, but up vs USD22.18bn at 31 Dec).
  • Capex USD3.29bn (vs USD3.62bn).
  • CEO Meg O’Neill quote: "strong operational and financial delivery" and progress towards 2027 targets; noted high plant reliability/refining availability and increased production in Gulf of America and bpx Energy.
  • Brent average USD81.13/bbl in Q1 (vs USD63.73 in Q4; +27% QoQ, and +7.1% YoY vs USD75.73); BP noted Brent was USD111.34/bbl Tuesday morning.
  • BP on geopolitics: "continues to closely monitor the situation in the Middle East", with full impact dependent on “extent and duration” of market conditions.
  • Dividend declared 8.320 US cents/share (+4.0% YoY; 6.226p vs 6.176p).
  • Reiterated: net debt target USD14–18bn by end-2027; 2026 capex budget USD13.0–13.5bn.
  • Capital structure: plans to reduce perpetual hybrid bond capital to ~USD9bn from USD13.3bn (disposals + balance sheet strengthening).
  • Q2 trading: upstream production expected lower vs Q1; customers volumes seasonally higher but midstream result lower vs Q1; refining throughput to be impacted by more planned turnarounds.
  • 2026: now expects upstream production lower due to Middle East crisis; underlying upstream production expected broadly flat vs 2025.

• Pretax profit GBP2.81bn (+3.3% YoY) vs consensus GBP2.83bn (slightly below).
  • Total income GBP8.16bn (+5.8% YoY) vs consensus GBP8.12bn (in line).
  • Attributable profit GBP1.93bn (+3.8% YoY).
  • Basic EPS 14.1p (+8.5% YoY).
  • Divisional income: Barclays UK +9%; UK Corporate Bank +10%; Investment Bank +4%; US Consumer Bank +14%; Private Bank & Wealth broadly stable.
  • RoTE 13.5% (from 14.0%); Barclays notes all divisions still delivered double-digit returns.
  • Cost-to-income ratio improved to 56% (from 57%); costs GBP4.36bn (+2.3% YoY).
  • Credit impairment charges GBP823m (from GBP643m), including a GBP228m single-name charge in the investment bank.
  • Loan loss rate 74bps (from 61bps).
  • CET1 ratio 14.1% (from 13.9%).
  • Litigation & conduct charges GBP104m driven by GBP105m incremental provision for FCA motor finance redress; total provision now GBP430m (from GBP325m at end-2025).
  • New GBP500m share buyback announced (to start after completion of current GBP1.0bn programme).
  • Guidance/targets reiterated: RoTE >12% in 2026, >14% in 2028; 2026 income ~GBP31bn, cost-to-income high-50s, CET1 within 13–14% range.
  • Capital return ambition reiterated: >GBP15bn to shareholders over 2026–2028 via dividends + buybacks.
  • CEO CS Venkatakrishnan quote: "The breadth and quality of our businesses mean we remain confident in delivering all our financial targets across a range of environments."

• Copper production 170kt (vs 169kt); average realised price 572c/lb (vs 444c/lb; +29% YoY).
  • Diamond production 7.1m carats (vs 6.1m), but consolidated average realised price USD101/carat (vs USD124; -19% YoY).
  • Manganese ore production 759kt (vs 348kt; >2x), reflecting recovery after Australia cyclone-related suspension (March 2024).
  • Steelmaking coal output 1.5mt (vs 2.2mt), impacted by Moranbah North incident (March 2025) and weather at Dawson open cut.
  • Divestment: expects steelmaking coal sale to be agreed in Q2 2026; quote: "As previously announced, Anglo American is committed to divesting its Steelmaking Coal business and the sale process is progressing well..."
  • Nickel output 9.1kt (vs 9.8kt).
  • Iron ore output 15.2mt (vs 15.4mt); average price USD91/wmt (vs USD96).
  • Nickel business disposal: referenced prior definitive agreement to sell to MMG Singapore Resources, with completion subject to antitrust clearance.
  • Kumba: average price USD93/wmt (vs USD98); waste mining 39.2mt (vs 40.5mt); production 8.8mt (vs 9.0mt); sales 9.3mt (vs 9.0mt).
  • Kumba CEO Mpumi Zikalala quote on capex project: "construction of Sishen's ultra high dense media separation project continues, with preparations underway for the main plant tie-in scheduled for the second half of 2026."
  • Zikalala on shipping risk: "Kumba's export sales routes to our markets in Asia and Europe remain open and have not been impacted by shipping disruptions caused by the conflict in the Middle East... we continue to closely monitor developments and manage potential associated risks, including cost inflation."
  • 2026 guidance unchanged (production + unit costs), including: iron ore 55–59mt @ USD41/t (Kumba 31–33mt @ USD45/t, weighted to H1; Minas-Rio 24–26mt @ USD36/t); copper 700–760kt @ 172c/lb; diamonds (exit business) 21–26m carats @ USD80/carat.

• Total revenue -3.1% YoY in Q1, citing reduced construction activity hurting volumes.
  • Like-for-like revenue -1.7%.
  • Network changes contributed -1.4% to revenue.
  • Like-for-like volume -2.8%, partly offset by +1.1% from price and sales mix.
  • Merchanting division like-for-like revenue -2.3%; Staircraft disposal added -1.9% revenue impact.
  • Toolstation UK like-for-like revenue +2.6%.
  • Toolstation Benelux like-for-like revenue -7.1%.
  • Company quote: "Ongoing capital discipline and incremental cash generation opportunities are continuing to enhance the group's financial position," while trading conditions remained “challenging”.

time_period: 4 weeks to 19 April 2026 (plus 12 weeks to 19 April 2026 market share data)
body:
• Take-home grocery sales +0.9% YoY in the 4 weeks to 19 April.
  • Like-for-like grocery inflation fell to 3.8% (lowest since April 2025).
  • Spend on promoted items +7.8% YoY; spend on full-price goods -0.2% YoY.
  • Promotions accounted for 31% of grocery spending; has risen YoY every month since July 2023.
  • Price cuts are driving promo behaviour: ~80% (“four in every five pounds”) of promo spend is on price reductions rather than multi-buys.
  • Fraser McKevitt (Worldpanel) quote: "Concerns about the impact of the Middle East conflict on prices of everyday goods are front of mind for British households. Already feeling the squeeze at the petrol pump, shoppers are responding by turning to special offers in growing numbers when buying groceries."
  • Easter seasonality: fresh lamb joint sales +15% in the 4 weeks to Easter Sunday vs the comparable run-up in 2025; 2.6m households bought a lamb joint in the 7 days to 5 April.
  • Hot cross bun sales +14% in the same run-up period.
  • Spring cleaning tailwind: cleaning product purchases +8% YoY.
  • 12 weeks to 19 April: Lidl hit record share 8.4% (from 8.0%; +0.4pp) with sales +8.8%, adding 500k+ shoppers.
  • Tesco sales +4.3%, share 28.1% (from 27.7%; +0.4pp).
  • Sainsbury’s sales +4.5%, share 15.5% (from 15.3%; +0.2pp).
  • Morrisons sales +1.1%, share 8.4%; Asda share 11.6%.
  • Aldi sales +1.2%, share 10.6%.
  • Ocado Retail fastest-growing grocer: sales +11.3%, share 2.2% (from 2.0%; +0.2pp).
  • Waitrose sales +3.8%, share 4.6%; Iceland sales +2.1%, share 2.3%; Co-op share 5.1%.
  • M&S grocery sales +7.3% (not classified as a “grocer” by Worldpanel due to sales mix).

• CEO Paul Thwaite: “We have started 2026 with positive momentum on which we can build… A new chapter is well underway, one defined not by recovery, but by an ambition to succeed for the long term…”
  • Pointed to NatWest’s recent acquisition of wealth management firm Evelyn Partners as evidence of momentum; reiterated confidence in delivering sustainable returns.
  • Thwaite: “We should be clear: there are near-term challenges. Recent global events mean pressures have elevated for many households and businesses.”
  • Emphasised belief in the UK’s long-term strengths: “strong institutions, deep pools of talent and an enduring capacity to innovate” and framed NatWest’s role in enabling investment that supports future growth.

• Completed acquisition of Semrush Holdings, Inc., positioning Adobe to expand offerings around brand discoverability and conversion as “AI interfaces and agents” reshape discovery.
  • Adobe frames strategy around “customer experience orchestration (CXO)” and highlights Adobe CX Enterprise as an end-to-end “agentic AI system” spanning content supply chain, engagement and brand visibility.
  • Adobe claims its data shows AI traffic to U.S. retail sites up 269% YoY (March 2026) and argues businesses have gaps in “AI-led brand visibility”.
  • Product/market positioning: combining Semrush with Adobe capabilities for SEO, generative engine optimization (GEO) and agentic search optimization (ASO).
  • Planned integration points cited: Adobe Experience Manager, Adobe LLM Optimizer, Adobe Commerce, Adobe Experience Platform (AEP) and AEP-powered apps, plus Adobe Brand Concierge.
  • Adobe’s Anil Chakravarthy: “The rules of brand discovery and commerce are being rewritten in real time…” and said the combined offering will give “the full picture of how their brands show up… from discoverability in search engines and LLMs to content creation, customer engagement and conversion…”
  • Semrush CEO Bill Wagner: “By joining Adobe, we see an incredible opportunity to build the definitive platform for brand visibility in an AI-driven world…”
  • Adobe says Semrush customers should expect “continued investment” and an expanded roadmap as integration progresses.

• Pretax profit up 3.3% to GBP2.81bn (vs GBP2.72bn).
  • Total income up 5.8% to GBP8.16bn (vs GBP7.71bn).
  • Attributable profit up 3.8% to GBP1.93bn (vs GBP1.86bn); basic EPS up 8.5% to 14.1p (vs 13.0p).
  • Costs up 2.3% to GBP4.36bn (vs GBP4.26bn); cost-to-income ratio improved to 56% (from 57%).
  • Motor finance: GBP105m top-up to the FCA motor finance redress provision, lifting total provision to GBP430m (from GBP325m at end-2025).
  • Credit quality: total credit impairment charges GBP823m (vs GBP643m), including a GBP228m single-name charge in the investment bank; loan loss rate 74bps (from 61bps).
  • Capital: CET1 ratio 14.1% (vs 13.9% a year ago).
  • Returns: RoTE 13.5% (vs 14.0% YoY); Barclays said all divisions delivered double-digit returns.
  • Shareholder returns: announced GBP500m new share buyback (to begin after completion of the current GBP1.0bn programme).
  • Guidance/targets reiterated: 2026 total income ~GBP31bn, high-50s cost-to-income ratio, CET1 within 13%–14%; RoTE >12% in 2026 and >14% in 2028; plans to return >GBP15bn of capital to shareholders 2026–2028.
  • CEO CS Venkatakrishnan: “solid” quarter and “The breadth and quality of our businesses mean we remain confident in delivering all our financial targets across a range of environments.”

• Executive Chair Robert Perrins bought 7,000 shares at GBP32.45 (total GBP227,160).
  • Noted he bought 14,000 shares earlier this month after the share fall linked to Berkeley’s decision to halt land buying amid a UK housing market downturn attributed to the conflict in the Middle East.

• CEO Eric Lipar: “We are pleased with our first quarter results, which met or exceeded our expectations across nearly every metric.”
  • Delivered 916 total closings (including 35 currently/previously leased homes); 881 home closings contributed to $319.7m home sales revenue; profits from 35 leased-home sales recognised in other income.
  • Average sales price up 2.9% to $362,924, citing targeted price discounts and financing strategies to support affordability.
  • Gross margin (home sales revenue basis) 18.7%; gross margin excl. impairment 20.2%; adjusted gross margin 23.4% (above prior guidance range).
  • Backlog: ended quarter with 1,699 homes, up 63.4% YoY and up 21.9% sequentially.
  • Profitability: net income before tax $4.3m; net income $2.2m ($0.09 basic/diluted EPS); adjusted net income $5.6m ($0.24 adjusted basic/diluted EPS).
  • Liquidity: total liquidity $355.0m (cash $60.9m + revolver availability $294.2m); net debt to capital ratio 44.0%.
  • Guidance change: raising full-year gross margin guidance to 18.5%–20.5% and adjusted gross margin to 22.0%–24.0% (other FY2026 guidance reiterated).
  • FY2026 outlook reiterated: closings 4,600–5,400; active communities year-end 150–160; ASP $355k–$365k; SG&A 15.0%–16.0%; effective tax rate ~26.5%.
  • Lipar: “based on first quarter margins exceeding our previous guidance range, we are raising full year gross margin guidance…” and highlighted “structural advantages of our self-developed land pipeline” and disciplined pricing/inventory management.

time_period: FY ended January 2026 (plus first 3 months of FY2027 trading comment)
body:
• Pretax profit down 32% to GBP43.9m (vs GBP64.1m).
  • Adjusted pretax profit down 15% to GBP56.0m (vs GBP66.0m), delivered in line with revised guidance (Dec guidance: GBP55m–GBP60m, cut from ~GBP70m).
  • CEO Darcy Willson-Rymer: “Softer high street footfall in the second half, particularly during our peak trading period, impacted full-year performance, with adjusted pretax profit being delivered in line with our revised guidance.”
  • Revenue up 7.4% to GBP582.7m (vs GBP542.5m), supported by acquired businesses.
  • Total store sales up 1.5%; included 27 net new stores YoY.
  • Like-for-like store sales down 0.2%: higher average basket values offset by fewer transactions amid low consumer confidence.
  • Category LFL: cards down 0.9%; gifts down 1.9%; celebration essentials up 1.7%.
  • EPS: basic down 35% to 9.0p (vs 13.8p); adjusted down 17% to 11.8p (vs 14.3p).
  • FY2027 outlook: expects adjusted pretax profit in line with market consensus GBP58.2m (slightly above FY just ended); external environment remains “uncertain”.
  • Early FY2027 trading: total sales (excluding Funky Pigeon) in the first three months are in line with the same period last year.
  • Capital returns: dividend up 4.2% to 5.0p (vs 4.8p); GBP15m share buyback to start immediately, running to end-January next year.
  • Estate: expects FY2027 new store openings at a similar rate.

• Gold production down 4.1% QoQ to 82,723 oz (vs 86,273 oz).
  • Copper production up 17% QoQ to 4,128 t (vs 3,528 t).
  • Gold sales up 35% QoQ to 97,800 oz (vs 72,212 oz).
  • Copper sales up 40% QoQ to 4,620 t (vs 3,301 t).
  • Weighted average realised prices: AUD6,773/oz gold and AUD15,803/t copper.
  • Net revenue up 46% QoQ to AUD742m (c. USD532.2m) (vs AUD507m).
  • Cash flow from operations AUD543m; record cash build AUD260m; closing cash balance AUD1.21bn (vs AUD948m at end-Dec).
  • Hedging/pricing: full exposure to gold price, with partial downside protection via gold put options.
  • MD Shaun Day: “We are pleased to have delivered another strong operational result which delivered a record cash build of AUD260 million for the March quarter,” and “A key highlight of the March quarter was the delivery of a significant resource upgrade at Telfer which increased Greatland's total gold resources to nearly 15 million ounces… The enlarged resource base has the potential to underpin a multi-decade, world class gold-copper mining hub.”
  • Outlook/costs: expects FY all-in sustaining cost to trend to the lower end of AUD2,400–AUD2,800/oz guidance; YTD cost AUD2,136/oz.
  • Risk watch: “closely monitoring” potential supply-chain and cost impacts from the war in the Middle East.

• March quarter delivered record cash build of $260m, closing cash $1,208m (vs $948m at 31 Dec 2025); debt free.
  • Quarterly production: 82,723oz Au and 4,128t Cu at AISC $2,056/oz Au; FY26 YTD: 249,887oz Au and 11,022t Cu at AISC $2,136/oz Au.
  • Operations: maintained high gold recoveries at 88.4% (in line with H1 FY26); “record” open pit material movements and ore mined.
  • Safety: zero lost time injuries; 12-month moving average LTIFR 0.2; TRIFR 4.6.
  • Sales: 97,800oz Au and 4,620t Cu at weighted average realised prices of $6,773/oz Au and $15,803/t Cu, generating net revenue $742m and operating cash flow $453m.
  • Guidance tone: “we currently expect full-year production to be around, or slightly above, the upper end of guidance (260,000–310,000oz Au)” and full-year AISC “to trend towards the lower end” of $2,400–$2,800/oz Au.
  • Price risk management: “Full upside exposure to the gold price” with partial downside protection via gold put options struck at $4,200/oz (CY26), $5,000/oz (Q1 CY27) and $5,200/oz (Q2 CY27).
  • Growth spend: $42m Telfer growth capex (tailings expansion, pre-strip, underground development, fleet renewal).
  • Drilling: record 240,000m annual Telfer drilling program on track; 78,302m completed in the quarter (vs 54,204m prior quarter), with 10 rigs operating.
  • Resource update (announced 30 March 2026): Telfer MRE grew +4.8Moz (+150%) to 8.0Moz Au (plus 370kt Cu) at 31 Dec 2025; Measured & Indicated increased +2.4Moz (+163%) to 3.8Moz Au (plus 249kt Cu); Group MRE stated at 14.9Moz Au (Havieron unchanged).
  • O’Callaghans MRE: 70Mt @ 0.35% WO3 / 0.30% Cu / 0.57% Zn / 0.28% Pb (contained 246kt WO3 / 207kt Cu / 371kt Zn / 182kt Pb), described as “one of the largest high-grade tungsten deposits globally”.

• Underlying Group revenue +3.7% YoY; +2.8% on a same-depot basis.
  • UK underlying revenue +3.5%; +2.6% same-depot.
  • International underlying revenue +9.1%; +7.9% same-depot (against “very strong” comparators).
  • Underlying figures adjusted for two fewer trading days vs 2025 (impact c.£11m).
  • Implemented price increases across all geographies at the start of the year; balancing margin vs volume.
  • Supply chain described as robust despite Middle East instability; fuel hedged through year-end and “very good” stock availability maintained.
  • 2026 investment plan reiterated: around £30m in strategic initiatives; open ~25 UK depots and 5 in Republic of Ireland; refurbish ~45 older UK depots.
  • Product/supply chain initiatives: 24 new kitchens in 2026; Runcorn manufacturing expansion “progressing as planned”.
  • Outlook: trading “well” early year; mindful sales are weighted to H2 (Autumn peak); on track with 2026 outlook.

• Group like-for-like revenue -1.7% in “challenging trading conditions”.
  • Merchanting LFL revenue -2.3% as construction activity remains subdued; price/mix +1.0% vs volume -3.3% (LFL).
  • Merchanting: progress passing through manufacturer price increases and delivering procurement benefits, while “maintaining market share in the General Merchant”.
  • Toolstation UK LFL revenue +2.6%; cited estate maturity benefits and further margin growth from range/offer evolution.
  • Toolstation Benelux LFL revenue -7.1% (volume -8.8%, price/mix +1.7%).
  • Total revenue growth (incl. network): Group -3.1%; network change -1.4% (including -1.9% in Merchanting) reflecting the 2025 disposal of Staircraft.
  • Focus remains on overhead control, operational efficiencies, capital discipline and incremental cash generation to improve financial position.

time_period: N/A (director dealing; references recent interim/guidance update)
body:
• Executive Chair Leo Quinn bought 17,764 shares at £5.58 and £5.60, total consideration £99,389 (trade reported for Friday 24 April 2026).
  • Shares fell >10% on Thursday 23 April 2026 after WH Smith suspended dividend payments following reduced interim profit and lowered guidance, citing Middle East-related travel disruption.

time_period: Trading update (UK performance YTD to 26 April 2026; FY 2026 outlook)
body:
• CEO Jennie Daly: “Sales in the year to date have been steady…” but notes ongoing affordability challenges and an increasingly uncertain macro backdrop.
  • Net private sales rate YTD: 0.74 per outlet per week (2025: 0.77; -3.9%).
  • Net private sales rate excluding bulk: 0.72 (2025: 0.76; -5.3%).
  • Cancellation rate: 14% (2025: 16%; -2pp).
  • Order book (26 Apr 2026): £2,229m (2025: £2,335m; -4.5%) representing 7,689 homes (2025: 8,153; -5.7%).
  • Order book pricing: ~1% lower YoY, with greatest impact in more affordability-stretched South of England, and where capital is being recycled as Greater London apartment schemes are phased out.
  • Outlets: average YTD 219 (2025: 208; +5.3%); current 218 (2025: 201; +8.5%). Company expects more outlet openings in 2026 than 2025 and average outlets to increase YoY.
  • Landbank: short-term landbank ~76k plots (end-Mar 2025: ~78k; -2.6%); strategic pipeline ~133k plots (2025: ~136k; -2.2%).
  • Land approvals YTD: ~1k plots (2025: ~1.7k; -41%), citing a more selective approach.
  • Shareholder returns: intends to pay 2025 final dividend 2.95p on 15 May 2026 (2024 final: 4.66p; -36.7%), subject to AGM approval; reiterates updated policy to return ~5% of net assets via ordinary dividend plus a further 2.5% via dividend or buyback.
  • Buyback progress: purchased 39.0m shares for £34.9m of the planned £52m (expects completion in H1).
  • Cost outlook: due to rising energy costs, build cost inflation now expected low-to-mid single digit in 2026, with supply-chain surcharges emerging; focus on controlling land and WIP spend and mitigating costs.

time_period: Funding / going concern update impacting FY 2025 audited accounts (year ended 31 December 2025)
body:
• Board says an equity fundraise of at least $100m is currently the only viable near-term solution to meet obligations and provide working capital while operating at a reduced level for the next ~18 months and to continue as a going concern.
  • Company has received indicative, non-binding institutional EOIs > $100m in aggregate, but conditional on terms the company says cannot be achieved within the required timeframe.
  • Largest shareholder Fevamotinico (49.32%): supports the fundraise and will provide an irrevocable undertaking to vote in favour of required resolutions, provided it can participate pro rata where the fundraise is no greater than $100m.
  • No certainty that any funding solution will be executed; further announcement promised if/when terms are finalised.
  • Delay to audited FY 2025 results: will not publish audited financial statements by 30 April 2026 (DTR deadline), because finalisation depends on completing the bookbuild to conclude accounts on a going concern basis.
  • Expected suspension: shares expected to be suspended from listing and trading from 07:30 (UK) on 1 May 2026 until audit completion and publication of the FY 2025 annual report and accounts (which itself requires an appropriate funding solution).

time_period: Corporate action update (treasury share cancellation / share capital reduction)
body:
• Completed share capital reduction by cancelling 115.5 million treasury shares.
  • Capital reduction deed registered with the Madrid Commercial Register “Wednesday last week”.
  • Expects cancelled shares to be excluded from the relevant stock exchanges “in the forthcoming days”.
  • Post-cancellation share capital: €461.2m, representing 4.61bn shares.

• Appointed CFO Peter Graham and COO Kerri Palmer as Co-Presidents, reporting to CEO Jon Witter; intended to support alignment and execution following the retirement of the Chief Commercial Officer.
  • Graham (CFO since 2023) will, as Co-President, additionally oversee strategic partnerships and emerging lines of business.
  • Palmer (COO and President of Sallie Mae Bank since 2023; formerly Chief Risk Officer / Chief Risk & Compliance Officer) will, as Co-President and Head of Financial Services, lead the core private education loan business plus credit and operations.
  • CEO Jon Witter quote: “As CEO, I remain fully focused on leading the company, and these appointments will further strengthen our ability to execute our strategy with depth and continuity…”

• Robert Bourrier appointed Chief Sales Officer.
  • Mandate: lead and scale the national sales organisation, targeting sustained revenue growth, market share expansion, and disciplined financial performance; translate enterprise strategy into measurable execution across all markets.
  • Scope: sales strategy execution, operating rhythms/performance management, sales talent & leadership development, and go-to-market execution to deliver consistent dealer/consumer experience.
  • CEO Vinayak Hegde quote: “Robert strengthens our ability to turn our customer-centric strategy into consistent execution… discipline in how we operate is foundational… His expertise in building the teams, systems, and operating rhythms we need will be instrumental…”
  • Background: 25+ years sales/commercial leadership; most recently led corporate marketplace efforts at Wheels Up; prior senior roles overseeing global corporate agreements at Delta Air Lines and Air Canada.
  • Robert Bourrier quote: “Credit Acceptance has a powerful mission to change lives… strengthen our strategy to drive growth and stronger customer outcomes.”

• CEO Duncan Wanblad said: "We've delivered a strong start to the year across both Copper and Premium Iron Ore, tracking well to our mine plans. In Copper, the reopening of the second plant at Los Bronces has provided incremental profitable production, Collahuasi continues to progress towards higher grade ore later this year and Quellaveco's recoveries improved, helping to partially offset the expected lower grades through the first half. In Premium Iron Ore, Kumba and Minas-Rio once again delivered stable operational performances.
