While the conflict in the Middle East is creating considerable volatility in the broader market, our resilient supply chain is currently supporting business continuity, and we are actively managing the situation to address potential adverse effects, including cost inflation.

"We are continuing to execute our portfolio optimisation. We have resumed normal operations at Moranbah North and the sale process for Steelmaking Coal is progressing well, with expectations for a sale to be agreed in the second quarter of 2026. We are progressing the sale process for De Beers and continue to assess further cost and capital preservation measures to minimise the impact from challenging diamond markets. In Nickel, we are working through the European Commission's anti-trust approval process.

"Our merger with Teck, to form a copper-focused global critical minerals champion, is on track for an expected September 2026 to March 2027 close. We were pleased to receive regulatory approval from South Korea in the quarter, with anti-trust approval from China now the final outstanding regulatory milestone, alongside other customary closing conditions. Although we both continue to operate separately until closing, the integration planning is progressing well, ensuring that once the transaction closes, we will be well positioned to begin delivering the exceptional value and expected synergies that we have identified."

  • Group “simplified portfolio” production: Copper 170.4kt (+1% YoY); Premium iron ore 15.2Mt (-2% YoY); Manganese ore (40% share) 759.1kt (+118% YoY).
  • “Exiting businesses” production: Diamonds 7.1Mct (+17% YoY); Steelmaking coal 1.5Mt (-31% YoY); Nickel 9.1kt (-7% YoY).
  • 2026 production and unit cost guidance unchanged for continuing businesses: Copper 700–760kt (unit cost ~172c/lb; Chile ~230c/lb, Peru ~100c/lb); Premium iron ore 55–59Mt (unit cost ~$41/t; Kumba ~$45/t, Minas-Rio ~$36/t).
  • Diamonds guidance unchanged (exiting business): 21–26Mct at unit cost ~$80/ct; De Beers output to be aligned to demand amid weak rough trading conditions.
  • Realised prices vs Q1 2025: Copper 572c/lb (+29%); Premium iron ore (FOB) $91/wmt (-5%); Diamonds $101/ct (-19%) and De Beers price index 68 (-17%); Hard coking coal $199/t (+16%); Nickel $6.71/lb (+7%).
  • Copper: Chile 97.0kt (+9% YoY) driven by throughput (Los Bronces +12% to 48.5kt after second plant restart; Collahuasi attributable +10% to 38.8kt on higher throughput and recoveries 71.8% vs 66.2%, despite lower grades 0.77% vs 0.86%).
  • Collahuasi: processing lower-grade stockpile ore continues until access to higher-grade ROM in Rosario pit “towards the end of the year”; desalination plant commissioning underway and “on track” to ramp up and be fully operational by mid-2026.
  • Peru (Quellaveco): 73.4kt (-8% YoY) on expected lower grades (0.68% vs 0.80%) partly offset by higher recoveries (85.5% vs 80.2%); production still weighted to H2 2026.
  • Copper sales volumes: 166.5kt (-4% YoY) and -5% vs Q4 2025.
  • Premium iron ore: Kumba production 8.8Mt (-2% YoY) with Sishen +5% (to 6.3Mt) offset by Kolomela -15% (to 2.6Mt) due to planned stock drawdown ahead of Q2 rail maintenance.
  • Kumba: sales 9.1Mt (+2% YoY) on improved port performance; finished stock 7.3Mt (vs 7.5Mt in Q4 2025) with mine stock down (4.7Mt from 5.7Mt) and port stock up (2.6Mt from 1.8Mt).
  • Kumba pricing: realised export basket $93/wmt (wet) stated as 8% above Fastmarkets 62% Fe benchmark; Fe content 63.7% (vs 64.2%), lump:fines 65:35 (vs 68:32).
  • Minas-Rio production 6.4Mt (broadly flat / -1% YoY) supported by higher plant utilisation in wet season; realised price $89/wmt stated as 5% above Fastmarkets 65% Fe benchmark.
  • Manganese ore (40% share): +118% YoY driven by easier comps after Cyclone Megan disruption in March 2024; current quarter still impacted by adverse weather incl. Cyclone Narelle.
  • De Beers: rough production 7.13Mct (+17% YoY), with Canada +163% (planned ore release at Gahcho Kué) and Venetia underground +53%; Namibia -12% (vessel maintenance + decommissioning impact).
  • De Beers trading: Q1 rough sales 7.7Mct (consolidated 6.4Mct) from two Sights; consolidated rough sales value $648m (+25% YoY), but average realised price $101/ct (-19%) on weaker price index and lower-value mix; “challenged” trading conditions cited (industry, geopolitical and tariff headwinds).
  • Steelmaking coal: production 1.545Mt (-31% YoY, -25% QoQ) due to Moranbah North post-incident and significant weather impacts at Dawson.
  • Moranbah North: regulator lifted final directives in Feb 2026; site transitioned to normal longwall operations and is ramping up.
  • Steelmaking coal pricing: realised hard coking coal $199/t vs benchmark $235/t; price realisation 85% (vs 93% in Q1 2025) due to lower premium HCC volumes from Moranbah North.
  • Grosvenor: re-entry/rectification “final stages”; longwall restart targeted late 2027 (subject to investment approval) after inspections confirmed limited infrastructure damage and undamaged next-panel longwall equipment.
  • Steelmaking Coal divestment: sale process “progressing well” with expectation for a sale to be agreed in Q2 2026.
  • Nickel: production 9.1kt (-7% YoY) on maintenance at Barro Alto and Codemin; output expected to increase gradually from Q2; sale to MMG Singapore Resources progressing through European Commission anti-trust approval.
  • Portfolio actions referenced during/around the period: delisting from SIX Swiss Exchange (23 Mar 2026); Woodsmith investment agreement with Mitsubishi (20 Feb 2026); FY25 results and final dividend notice (20 Feb 2026).

time_period: Year ended 31 December 2025 (with Q1 2026 update and 2026 guidance)
body:
• CEO Ian Perks: “delivered against a clear set of strategic priorities… [including] significant cost reductions… positioned the company strongly for the successful refinancing, which completed earlier this month… provides… financial foundation and flexibility required to deliver value for stakeholders.”
  • CEO Ian Perks: “A key milestone has been the agreement to purchase the TEN FPSO, a value-accretive acquisition that… eliminates lease costs… [and] capture operating cost savings… extending the Jubilee and TEN petroleum agreements to 2040, and higher oil prices have further strengthened our platform…”
  • Group WI production averaged 40.4 kboepd (2024: 51.5; -21.6%), reflecting portfolio change post-Gabon disposal.
  • Revenue $847m (2024: $1,287m; -34.2%), including hedge impact of $19m (2024: $74m).
  • Adjusted EBITDAX $586m (2024: $1,008m; -41.9%); gross profit $247m (2024: $635m; -61.1%).
  • Continuing operations loss after tax $129m (2024: $55m loss); total profit after tax $7m (2024: $55m; -87%), driven by discontinued ops profit $136m.
  • Free cash flow $99m (2024: $156m; -36.5%); management flags lower realised revenue late-year + delayed Kenya proceeds + delayed Ghana cash calls/gas payments as key drags.
  • Net debt $1,353m (2024: $1,452m; -6.8%); net debt/EBITDAX 2.3x (2024: 1.4x) on lower EBITDAX.
  • YE liquidity headroom $322m (2024: $715m; -54.9%); post-refinancing liquidity headroom stated as “in excess of $200m” (free cash + undrawn facilities).
  • Disposals: Gabon sale completed July for $307m net cash; Kenya sale completed September with $80m received (two tranches) and $40m contingent due no later than 30 June 2033 (oil-price-linked schedule). 2025 proceeds disclosed as $347m.
  • 2025 capex $166m (ex-Gabon; 2024: $179m ex-Gabon) + decommissioning cash/provisioning $17m (2024: $60m).
  • Q1 2026 production averaged 43.4 kboepd, supporting delivery at the higher end of prior 34–42 kboepd guidance (incl. ~6 kboepd gas).
  • Ghana terms strengthened: Jubilee & TEN Petroleum Agreement extensions to 2040 ratified Feb 2026; revised Jubilee gas price terms to end-2040 at an escalating $2.50/mmbtu + heads of terms for potential TEN gas supply + agreed gas payment security mechanism.
  • TEN FPSO acquisition: SPA signed 19 Feb 2026 to buy TEN FPSO for $205m gross / $125.6m net to Tullow, payable on completion end-Q1 2027; intended to remove lease cost and drive Jubilee/TEN operating synergies.
  • 2026 guidance uplift: pre-financing cash flow guidance increased to ~$260–365m at $70–100/bbl (citing YTD production outperformance, higher realised prices, and a higher oil-price assumption); FCF guidance $70–175m at $70–100/bbl.
  • Oil-price leverage disclosed: cash flow +~$40m moving from $70 → $80/bbl, then +~$30m per additional $10/bbl, non-linear due to hedges.
  • FCF guidance includes recovery of 2025 cash call receivables + ~$40m gas revenues from 2026 gas, but excludes $110m historical gas receivables and $50m TEN development debt receivable; GoG receivables at end-2025 stated as ~$225m net to Tullow (split: $65m cash calls, $110m gas, $50m TEN dev debt).
  • Refinancing completed 27 April 2026: Senior Secured Notes extended to 15 Nov 2028 (after $100m paydown; $1.185bn new notes issued + $25m fungible notes to Glencore) and Glencore facility extended to 15 May 2030 (with $21m accrued interest + $2m PIK fees added); new $100m cargo prepayment facility to 15 Nov 2028 (undrawn), with no maintenance covenants.
  • Refinancing structure includes a forward-looking cash sweep tied to maintaining rolling 15-month projected liquidity ≥ $100m; and a conditional maturity “springing” to 15 May 2028 if an M&A process (to commence before end-2026) does not reach a binding SPA within nine months.
  • Going concern assessment period stated to 30 April 2027; reverse stress test implies average oil price of ~$32/bbl over the period would be required to reduce headroom to zero.
  • Operations: average FPSO uptime 97% (Jubilee & TEN) in 2025; Jubilee J72-P onstream July 2025 (currently ~8 kbopd); J74-P onstream Jan 2026; J75-P onstream Mar 2026; total six Jubilee wells expected onstream in 2026 (5 producers + 1 injector), with next producers targeted June/July and injector September.
  • Reserves: audited 2P reserves 100.4 mmboe (2024: 164.5; -39.0%) driven by 2025 production (14.7 mmboe), Gabon disposal (36.0 mmboe) and Jubilee revision (11.8 mmboe); 2P NPV10 ~$1.27bn (price deck starts $62.29/bbl in 2026 rising to $66.24/bbl in 2030, then 2% inflation).
  • Disputes: Kenya Revenue Authority tax assessment ~$170m (VAT/CGT alleged on Kenya disposal) — company states assessment is “wholly without merit”, will contest, no expected cash outflow for objections/appeal and no provision recorded.
  • Governance: four new INEDs announced 8 April 2026 to satisfy refinancing requirements; Henry Steel effective immediately (also SID); Garrett Soden, Euan Shirlaw, James Peterkin effective 1 May 2026 (subject to AGM election in June).
  • Sustainability: routine flaring reduced 22% following FPSO process improvements/modifications in Ghana; reiterates Net Zero by 2030 commitment.

• Q126 RoTE 13.5% (Q125: 14.0%); profit before tax £2.8bn (+3% YoY); attributable profit £1.9bn (+4% YoY); EPS 14.1p (+8% YoY).
  • CEO C. S. Venkatakrishnan: "Barclays delivered another solid quarter with a 13.5% RoTE in Q126, and double-digit returns in all our businesses. This was despite a one-off charge and impairments in the quarter. Top line income grew 6% year-on-year, driven by broad based divisional performance including in the Investment Bank, where we generated over £4bn quarterly income for the first time. The cost: income ratio improved to 56% and earnings per share (EPS) grew by 8% to 14.1p. Our capital position remains robust with a 14.1% common equity tier 1 (CET1) ratio and we are announcing a £500m buyback today. The breadth and quality of our businesses mean we remain confident in delivering all our financial targets across a range of environments. This includes greater than 12% RoTE in 2026 and greater than 14% RoTE in 2028."
  • Announced intention to initiate a share buyback of up to £500m, to start after completion of the ongoing £1.0bn buyback announced at FY25 results.
  • Reiterated all 2026 and 2028 targets (incl. >12% RoTE in 2026 and >14% RoTE in 2028; CET1 target range 13–14%; 2024–26 capital returns ≥£10bn with quarterly buybacks; planned £2bn dividend for 2026; 2025–28 income >5% CAGR; cost:income high-50s in 2026 / low-50s in 2028 with c.£2bn gross efficiency savings in 2026–28).
  • Group income £8.2bn (+6% YoY); investment bank income £4.0bn (+4% YoY) with over £4bn quarterly income for the first time.
  • Group cost:income ratio improved to 56% (Q125: 57%); delivered c.£150m gross cost efficiency savings in Q126; total operating expenses £4.5bn (+4% YoY).
  • Credit impairment charges £0.8bn (Q125: £0.6bn); loan loss rate 74bps (Q125: 61bps) including a £0.2bn single-name impairment in the Investment Bank (c. 20bps impact on Group LLR).
  • FY26 impairment guidance: Group LLR expected around the top end of the 50–60bps through-the-cycle range (explicitly linked to the Q126 single-name charge).
  • Capital: CET1 ratio 14.1% (Dec-25: 14.3%); RWAs £364.5bn (Dec-25: £356.8bn). Pro-forma for the new £500m buyback, CET1 would be 13.9% (top end of 13–14% target range).
  • CET1 bridge highlights: +53bps from attributable profit; -41bps from shareholder distributions (incl. FY25 £1.0bn buyback + FY26 dividend accrual); RWAs +£7.7bn (incl. £5.5bn ex-FX, mainly UK lending growth + higher Global Markets activity).
  • UK leverage ratio 4.8% (Dec-25: 5.1%) as leverage exposure rose £74.0bn to £1,321.3bn, mainly higher Global Markets activity.
  • Liquidity: liquidity pool £326.1bn (down £11.7bn vs Dec-25); average LCR 165.4% (Dec-25: 170.0%); average NSFR 135.4% (Dec-25: 135.2%); deposits £587.6bn (slightly up vs Dec-25) driven by IB International Corporate Bank deposit growth.
  • MREL: issued £3.0bn equivalent of MREL instruments in Q126; MREL ratio 35.4% vs requirement 30.5% (ex-PRA buffer).
  • Barclays UK: RoTE 19.7% (Q125: 17.4%); income £2.26bn (+9%); NII £2.0bn (+9%); NIM 3.72% (+17bps); PBT £863m (+14%). Loans £217.8bn (+£1.3bn vs Dec-25, mortgage-led); deposits £243.9bn (-£0.7bn, seasonal).
  • UK Corporate Bank: RoTE 19.9% (Q125: 17.1%); income £530m (+10%); NII £394m (+15%); PBT £273m (+32%); impairments £3m (Q125: £19m).
  • Private Bank & Wealth Mgmt: RoTE 25.5% (Q125: 34.5%); income £347m (flat); costs +9%; PBT £92m (-25%); net new assets under management £1.5bn (Q125: £1.0bn); client assets & liabilities £223.8bn (-£3.8bn vs Dec-25, negative markets partly offset by inflows/FX).
  • Investment Bank: RoTE 15.0% (Q125: 16.2%); income £4.03bn (+4%); Global Markets £2.83bn (+6%) driven by Equities +16%; Banking fees & underwriting £754m (+17%) with Advisory +78% and ECM +31% (DCM -6%). PBT £1.62bn (-5%) as impairments rose to £279m (incl. £228m single-name).
  • US Consumer Bank: RoTE 18.8% (Q125: 4.5%); income £983m (+14%); NII £823m (+21%) and NIM 12.76% (Q125: 10.53%); PBT £236m (Q125: £55m). Impairments £367m (down vs £399m); card arrears ticked up (30/90 day 3.1%/1.7%).
  • UK motor finance redress (CFSL): FCA final rules published March 2026; Barclays increased provision by £105m in Q126 to £430m (Dec-25: £325m). Barclays: "has decided not to challenge the FCA's final rules in the interests of enabling a swift resolution for customers. However, Barclays strongly disagrees with aspects which require financial redress even where customers suffered no demonstrable financial harm."
  • USCB portfolio changes (Q226): exited American Airlines co-branded partnership on 24 April 2026, releasing c.$5bn RWAs and estimated gain on sale c.$300m; expects to complete Best Egg acquisition around 1 May 2026 for $800m (expected c.$500m goodwill & intangibles). Estimated net impact of both transactions expected to marginally increase Group CET1 in Q226.
  • FX impact: 7% YoY appreciation of average GBP vs USD reduced income/profits (and reduced reported opex/impairments), cited as a material headwind across Group/IB/USCB.
  • Structural hedge: gross structural hedge contribution £1,660m (Q125: £1,335m); structural hedge notional £241.8bn (Dec-25: £236.1bn); average structural hedge duration c.3.5 years.

• Announced successful completion of refinancing supported by >99% of bondholders and Glencore.
  • CEO Ian Perks: refinancing extends maturities and reduces cash interest payments, strengthening the financial base to execute the plan.
  • Redeemed $100m of existing notes at par; released remaining existing notes/obligations.
  • Issued ~$1.185bn senior secured notes due 2028.
  • Released obligations under existing $400m secured notes facility.
  • Issued ~$423m junior secured notes and entered a $100m new super senior cargo prepayment facility.
  • Issued $25m of additional new notes to Glencore via private placement; transaction effective time occurred 27 April 2026 (lock-up agreement terminated).

• Expects to complete move from AIM to the Main Market on 1 May 2026 (Admission + AIM cancellation at 8:00 a.m.; last AIM trading day expected 30 April 2026).
  • Admission is by introduction of existing shares only (no offer / no new issuance); shares retain ISIN GB00BQSBH502 and ticker MAB1.
  • Trading: mortgage applications in the first 16 weeks of 2026 were +19% YoY.
  • Company statement: “Notwithstanding macroeconomic uncertainty relating to the situation in the Middle East, the company continues to trade in line with the board's expectations, with refinancing activity expected to remain robust through the remainder of 2026 and the business well placed to deliver further profitable growth.”

• Announced $16.4bn cash-and-shares acquisition of ARC Resources Ltd (equity value ~$13.6bn plus assumption of ~$2.8bn net debt & leases).
  • Consideration: CAD 8.20 cash + 0.40247 Shell ordinary shares per ARC share.
  • Funding mix for the equity consideration: $3.4bn cash + $10.2bn Shell shares.
  • Deal framing: expected to generate double-digit returns, bolster long-term cash flows, and be accretive to free cash flow per share from 2027 onwards.
  • Capex: incremental organic cash capex absorbed within existing cash capex post-2026; 2027–2028 cash capex range unchanged at $20–22bn.
  • Synergies: expects ~$250m annualised synergies within a year of closing.
  • Shareholder returns: distribution policy unchanged.
  • Adds ~370 kboe/d immediately across liquids and gas; implies ~4% production CAGR through to 2030 vs 2025.
  • Strategic rationale: increases exposure to “long-duration, low-cost and top quartile low carbon intensity shale gas and liquids production in Canada's Montney basin”.
  • CEO Wael Sawan: acquisition “complements our existing footprint in Canada and strengthens our resource base for decades to come”.

• Published an FCA-approved prospectus for Admission to the Main Market (Equity Shares (Commercial Companies) category) and cancellation from AIM.
  • Expected timetable: Admission + AIM cancellation at 8:00 a.m. on 1 May 2026; last day of AIM trading expected 30 April 2026.
  • Admission is by introduction of existing shares only — no offer / no issuance of new shares or other securities.
  • Shares retain existing ISIN GB00BQSBH502 and continue trading under ticker MAB1.
  • Trading update: mortgage applications in the first 16 weeks of 2026 were +19% YoY.
  • Board view unchanged: trading in line with expectations despite macro uncertainty linked to the Middle East; refinancing expected to remain robust through 2026, supporting “further profitable growth”.

time_period: Planning milestone for Skelton Grange; transaction phases from June 2024 through Plot 2 completion
body:
• Leeds local planning committee passed a resolution to approve a planning application from Harworth and MSFT MCIO Ltd (a Microsoft subsidiary) at Skelton Grange.
  • Application covers a data centre campus (with associated infrastructure) plus outline planning for a warehouse building.
  • Proposed development: 500,000 sq ft across three data centre halls and auxiliary buildings.
  • Additional scope includes 160,000 sq ft of industrial & logistics space and EV charging on Harworth’s adjoining 16-acre plot.
  • Phase 1 of the Microsoft transaction completed in June 2024: GBP51.2m for 27 acres; Harworth continues remediation and enabling works for Microsoft.
  • Phase 2 covers 21 acres; Harworth expects a further GBP53.2m on completion of the sale to Microsoft.
  • CEO Lynda Shillaw said: “Obtaining the resolution to grant planning consent is demonstrable progress towards the completion of the Plot 2 sale and reflects our close and effective collaboration with Microsoft,”
  • Shillaw added: “This transaction has driven group level value growth and facilitates investment in the region, catalysing economic growth, with Skelton Grange estimated to deliver GBP4 billion of inward investment when the development completes.”
• Leigh Thomas (Managing Director, Property) sold 160,000 shares at GBP2.11, for total proceeds of GBP338,368 (Thursday last week).

time_period: Broker reaction to FY results; outlook/forecast changes covering FY2026–FY2028
body:
• Shares fell 3.0% to 334.10p on Monday morning after two broker downgrades.
  • Goldman Sachs double-downgraded the stock to ‘sell’ from ‘buy’; Citigroup downgraded to ‘neutral’ from ‘buy’.
  • Goldman said FY2026 Ebit and free cash flow were in line with its expectations, but: “the outlook from here is likely to be more challenging”.
  • Goldman’s macro rationale: UK household consumption growth expected at 0.6% YoY in 2026 (its lowest since 2009 excluding Covid) and April’s GfK survey showing higher intentions to save rather than spend.
  • Goldman now forecasts another year of flat retail Ebit, assuming still-elevated price inflation and grocery Ebit growth offset by FY2027 Argos like-for-like sales -2% and an Ebit decline amid a “demanding macro outlook, and growing competitive intensity”.
  • Goldman flagged the recently launched Chinese marketplace Joybuy as a competitive threat, noting 300,000 active users in March.
  • Goldman cut FY2027/FY2028 Ebit forecasts by 4% and 6%, and lowered its price target to 335p (from 390p).
  • Goldman rationale for the rating shift: “With our new [target price] implying 3% downside versus sector average 14% upside, we move to a sell rating on the shares (from buy),” the bank said.
  • Citigroup cut FY2027/FY2028 EPS forecasts by 10% and 12% to reflect weaker guidance for the current financial year.
  • Citi reduced FY2027 Ebit forecast by 7% to GBP1.05bn, citing “continued headwinds from wage inflation and price investment amid a competitive market backdrop.”
  • Citi kept FY2027 grocery sales growth forecast at 4.3% (vs consensus 4.5%), assuming ongoing volume momentum, but expects Sainsbury to “under-inflate the market” via price investment to defend improved relative price positioning.
  • Citi lowered its price target to 335p (from 377p), maintaining valuation at ~13x FY2027 P/E (in line with long-run sector average).

time_period: Open-period buyback arrangement from 27 April 2026 until the next closed period begins
body:
• Entered a share buyback arrangement with Deutsche Bank AG (London branch).
  • Arrangement sits within AGM authority granted 24 September 2025, which allows repurchases up to 67,502,373 ordinary shares.
  • Deutsche Bank can buy up to 10,000,000 shares during open periods between now and the start of the next closed period; buys executed on Frasers’ behalf and aligned with UK Listing Rules.
  • Aggregate purchase price under the arrangement capped at £70.0m.
  • Shares purchased will be held in treasury; stated sole purpose is to reduce share capital.

time_period: Quarter ended 31 March 2026 (plus post-period updates to 24 April 2026)
body:
• Uranium spot market volatility during the quarter: near-term trading range from mid-US$80s/lb to just above US$100/lb, with activity driven in part by financial buying.
  • Term market strengthened: reported term prices US$90–93/lb; increased contracting activity expected to influence spot purchasing patterns and may add upward pressure.
  • U3O8 holdings at 31 March 2026: 23,114,230 lb (stored in Canada and France).
  • Holdings increased by 1,431,912 lb (+6.6%) vs 31 Dec 2025 (21,682,318 lb), reflecting: (i) 1,331,912 lb delivery from Kazatomprom (16 March 2026) and (ii) 100,000 lb spot purchase delivered 20 March 2026 (Orano, France).
  • Equity raise: completed an oversubscribed placing of ~13m shares on 17 Feb 2026, raising gross proceeds of ~£80.6m (≈US$110m).
  • 2026 Kazatomprom option: elected to purchase 1,160,766 lb at US$86.15/lb (aggregate US$100.0m); delivery expected H2 2026.
  • 2025 Kazatomprom option (settled in-quarter): option exercised Sept 2025 to buy 1,331,912 lb at US$75.08/lb (aggregate US$100.0m); delivered 16 March 2026; funded by Sept 2025 placing (gross ~£129.6m / ~US$175m).
  • Post quarter-end: bought an additional 100,000 lb spot, delivered 20 April 2026.
  • Pro-forma holdings (after H2 Kazatomprom delivery + April spot purchase): expected to rise to 24,374,996 lb.
  • Uranium holdings value increased 9.7% over the quarter to US$1,940.4m (from US$1,768.2m), driven by higher lbs held and spot price moving from US$81.55/lb (31 Dec 2025) to US$83.95/lb (31 Mar 2026) (+2.9%).
  • Estimated NAV per share increased 5.0% to £6.33 (from £6.03), aided by higher uranium value and GBP depreciation vs USD.
  • Estimated pro-forma NAV on 24 April 2026: £6.34/share (US$ 2,167.9m), assuming 24.37m lb valued at US$86.45/lb, and adjusting for the US$100.0m Kazatomprom commitment plus US$8.5m paid for the post-period 100,000 lb spot purchase.
  • CEO Andre Liebenberg said: **"The first quarter of 2026 marked a transition from policy ambition to large-scale implementation, accentuated by the conflict in the Middle East once again highlighting the importance of energy security. With 39 nations now committed to tripling nuclear capacity, the World Nuclear Association projects global capacity could reach 1,446 GWe by 2050, surpassing the targets launched at COP28 in 2023. This momentum is evidenced by China elevating its 2030 target to 110 GWe, near double its current capacity, and the U.S. 'UPRISE' initiative, which fast-tracks reactor uprates to power the AI and data centre revolution.

"While we welcome the progress of long-dated supply projects like NexGen Energy's Rook I and Denison's Phoenix, the market remains structurally tight today. We see utilities increasingly prioritising security of supply, driving term prices higher as they compete for limited available material.

"Against this backdrop, Yellow Cake continues to deliver tangible value for shareholders. Our oversubscribed US$110 million placing and the exercise of our Kazatomprom option have grown our holdings to 23.1 million pounds, with committed purchases expected to increase holdings to approximately 24.4 million pounds."**

time_period: Planning / transaction update as at 27 April 2026
body:
• Leeds local planning committee has passed a resolution to approve Microsoft’s planning application at Skelton Grange (Leeds).
  • Application seeks full planning permission for a hyperscale data centre campus (c. 500,000 sq ft across three data centre halls + auxiliary buildings) on Plots 1 & 2, plus outline permission for a warehouse building.
  • Also includes c. 160,000 sq ft of I&L space plus EV charging on Harworth’s 16-acre adjoining plot.
  • Transaction phasing: Plot 1 (27 acres) completed June 2024 for £51.2m gross; Plot 2 is 21 acres—Harworth delivering enabling works and expects £53.2m gross on completion of sale to Microsoft.
  • CEO Lynda Shillaw said: "Obtaining the resolution to grant planning consent is demonstrable progress towards the completion of the Plot 2 sale and reflects our close and effective collaboration with Microsoft. This transaction has driven group level value growth and facilitates investment in the region, catalysing economic growth, with Skelton Grange estimated to deliver c. £4bn of inward investment when the development completes."
  • CEO Lynda Shillaw added: "At Skelton, the 16 acres of adjoining land and our adjacent Gateway 45 site provide capacity for further development. Our powered land portfolio has a further 0.8GW of power connections, on top of our Skelton Grange site, and is well positioned to capture demand from data centre and other power-intensive sectors, providing the potential for further upside opportunities, delivering strong group level value gains and attractive shareholder returns."

• Unconditional exchange agreed to sell The Brix (Essen), a 21,797 sqm office building, for €60.0m, in line with the 31 Dec 2025 valuation; completion expected Q2 2026. ()
  • Asset repositioning: signed a new 30-year lease with the City of Essen; comprehensive refurbishment completed; tenant in occupation since October 2025.
  • CLS acquired the asset in April 2021 with 28% vacancy; leasing + refurbishment framed as successful “active asset management” to create long-term secure income.
  • Sale proceeds to be used to pay down debt.
  • CEO Fredrik Widlund said: "Following completion of the lease and refurbishment, we consider this the right time to crystallise the value of our investment through a disposal, improving the geographical focus of our German portfolio and reducing debt. The transformation of the building demonstrates our commitment to active asset management and to delivering high-quality, sustainable offices that meet the needs of long-term occupiers."

time_period: Five-year plan update expected with results (week following 25 April 2026 report)
body:
• Times reports Whitbread intends to sell a "swathe of Premier Inn hotels" to support a potential shareholder return of around £1.5bn.
  • Reported strategic shift: freehold ownership of Premier Inn properties expected to fall from ~50% to 40% under changes to the five-year plan (per “City sources” cited by The Times).
  • Times suggests Whitbread may sell or lease back one in five freehold hotel properties, potentially becoming a majority leasehold business for the first time since 1987 (founding year of Premier Inn cited).
  • Company comment: a Whitbread spokesperson declined to comment (per the report).

time_period: UK FCA motor finance redress scheme (covering 2007–2024 mis-selling period)
body:
• Close Brothers says it will not pursue a legal challenge to the FCA’s motor finance compensation/redress scheme.
  • Company statement (to Sky News): "Close Brothers does not intend to challenge the FCA's motor finance redress scheme. While there are elements of the scheme that we disagree with, after careful consideration we believe that the existing scheme offers a quick, clear and certain route to resolving this matter for all relevant parties."
  • Report notes Barclays and Lloyds have also decided against challenging; other lenders reportedly still preparing legal action ahead of a Monday deadline to launch a challenge.
  • FCA scheme cost estimates cited: expected firm payouts around £7.5bn (down from £8.2bn), rising to £9.1bn including scheme running costs (Sky report); mis-selling period referenced as 2007–2024.

time_period: CFO transition effective 7 May 2026 (AGM) + 4 months advisory overlap
body:
• Confirms Myles Westcott will commence as Group CFO following the AGM on 7 May 2026 (as previously announced on 24 February 2026).
  • Current CFO Philip Harrison will step down from the Board on 7 May 2026, ending >10 years as CFO.
  • Harrison will remain in an advisory capacity for four months to support transition.
  • AGM housekeeping: Resolution 9 (re-elect Philip Harrison as Director) is withdrawn; no impact on other resolutions, proxies, or numbering of remaining resolutions.

time_period: Board/management update (week of 22–24 April 2026); FY runs to end of January
body:
• Financial Times reported Chair Andrew Higginson stepped down after pushing for CEO Regis Schultz to be replaced, but failing to secure unanimous board backing; majority shareholder Pentland (55%) indicated continued support for Schultz.
  • Company statement to Alliance News reiterates board support for CEO: "It was mutually agreed between Andy and the board that this is the right time for a change of chair; there has been no disagreement about the board’s continued support for the CEO. The board is grateful for the valuable role that Andy has played during his tenure at the business."
  • JD Sports previously guided (January) that FY2026 profit before tax and adjusting items would be in line with market expectations at £849m (implying -8.0% vs FY2025 £923m; FY2025 was -4.0% vs FY2024 £961m).
  • Shares referenced as down sharply vs 2021 levels: around 70p on Friday (vs >230p in 2021); full-year results due 7 May.

• Confirms Emma Harris commenced employment at M&B on Monday 20 April 2026, following the previously announced CFO succession plan.
  • Confirms outgoing CFO Tim Jones will retire as CFO and Executive Director at close of business on Monday 6 July 2026 (post-handover).
  • Prior announcement reference: succession and planned handover were originally communicated on 23 October 2025.
  • Reported share price context in the accompanying note: 257.50p, down 1.3% on Friday morning in London.

time_period: 52 weeks ended 28 February 2026; buyback programme to FY end 27 February 2027
body:
• Commenced a share buyback programme of up to £300m, to be completed by 27 February 2027.
  • First tranche is up to £200m (ordinary shares), running from 24 April 2026 to on/before 11 September 2026.
  • Sole purpose is to reduce share capital; intends to cancel all repurchased ordinary shares.
  • Shore Capital Stockbrokers Limited appointed under non-discretionary instructions to execute the programme and make trading decisions independently.
  • Buyback constrained by AGM authority (3 July 2025): max remaining capacity (net of shares already repurchased since then) of 205,181,434 shares.
  • In annual results (52 weeks to 28 Feb 2026): pretax profit up 2.0% to £619m (FY25: £607m).
  • Revenue (ex-VAT, incl. fuel) up 2.7% to £33.65bn (FY25: £32.77bn).
  • Management commentary: Sainsbury said it is in a "strong competitive position" after another year of good progress.

• Total revenue $420.0m, +6.3% YoY (vs $395.2m).
  • Net income $239.4m (vs $170.4m); diluted EPS $0.82 (vs $0.60).
  • AFFO $297.1m, +9.2% YoY (vs $272.0m); AFFO per share $1.02 (vs $0.96).
  • Adjusted EBITDA $393.0m (vs $360.1m).
  • Dividend: declared $0.78/share for Q1 (paid 27 March 2026); annualised dividend $3.12/share.
  • Raised 2026 AFFO/share guidance to $4.08–$4.12 (prior $4.06–$4.11); total 2026 AFFO now guided $1.212bn–$1.223bn.
  • Completed two transactions in Q1 totaling $727m: Bally’s Lincoln real estate assets acquired for $700m (8.0% cap rate) and Live! Casino & Hotel Virginia land acquired for $27m (first step in $467m total commitment; 8.0% cap rate; draws expected to start H2 2026; targeted opening late 2027).
  • Development pipeline: ~$1.8bn commitments remaining to fund; 2026 development funding assumed $590m–$640m (total 2026 development spend $750m–$800m).
  • Balance sheet/leverage: net debt to adjusted EBITDA 5.0x at 31 March 2026 (low end of 5.0x–5.5x target range).
  • CEO quote: “we are raising our AFFO per share guidance for 2026” and highlighted record first quarter results plus long-term tenant stability with “vast majority” of rent coverage >1.8x.

• Revenue $13.6bn, +7% YoY (vs $12.7bn).
  • GAAP gross margin 39.4% (up 2.5ppts); non-GAAP gross margin 41.0% (up 1.8ppts).
  • GAAP net income (loss) $(3.7)bn; GAAP EPS $(0.73); non-GAAP net income $1.5bn (+156%); non-GAAP EPS $0.29 (+123%).
  • Cash from operations $1.1bn in Q1.
  • Segment revenue: Client Computing Group $7.7bn (+1%); Data Center & AI $5.1bn (+22%); Intel Products total $12.8bn (+9%); Intel Foundry $5.4bn (+16%); All other $0.6bn (-33%).
  • Noted comparability impact: Altera deconsolidated effective 12 September 2025 after sale of 51% stake.
  • Q2 2026 guidance: revenue $13.8bn–$14.8bn; GAAP EPS $0.08; non-GAAP EPS $0.20; GAAP gross margin 37.5%; non-GAAP gross margin 39.0%.
  • CEO quote: “The next wave of AI will bring intelligence closer to the end user… significantly increasing the need for Intel’s CPUs and wafer and advanced packaging offerings,” and said the operating reset drove a “sixth consecutive quarter of revenue above our expectations.”
  • CFO quote: “We delivered robust Q1 results… and our disciplined execution to expand available supply.”

• Pretax loss narrowed to GBP137.9m (from GBP241.5m).
  • Revenue fell 13.8% to GBP1.12bn (from GBP1.30bn).
  • Gross merchandise value (GMV) fell 8.6% to GBP1.17bn (from GBP1.28bn).
  • Maintained full-year guidance, including adjusted EBITDA of GBP150m–GBP180m.
  • Customer trend: new customer growth +9% YoY in March (noted as the first increase since 2021 in the article).
  • Mix/merchandising: expanded assortment with ~60 new brands in H1; increased exclusivity agreements; accelerated “test and react” (especially in womenswear).
  • Supply chain/product cycle: lead times cut by ~30 days; higher full-price sell-through supporting gross margin momentum (per commentary in the article).
  • Headwinds flagged in the article: inflationary pressures and supply chain disruption linked to Middle East geopolitical tensions.

• Portugal has asked Air France-KLM and Lufthansa to submit binding offers for a controlling stake in TAP as it progresses privatisation.
  • Government is seeking a buyer for most of the 49.9% stake it plans to sell; binding bids requested within three months.
  • Government indicated a final decision could be made in August or early September.
  • IAG (British Airways/Iberia parent) had expressed interest previously but did not submit a bid in this process (per the article).
  • Rationale highlighted: TAP’s network strength on routes to Brazil and Portuguese-speaking Africa.

time_period: Quarter / Q1 2026 trading update (exact dates not stated)
body:
• Update ahead of AGM: Q1 trading described as an “encouraging start” to the year.
  • Total system sales grew 5.8% in the quarter.
  • Like-for-like sales grew 4.5%.
  • Total orders rose 2.3%.
  • Like-for-like orders rose 0.9%.
  • New product/format: launched “Chick ’N’ Dip” (chicken concept); initial trading met expectations.
  • Cost outlook: does not foresee supply-related issues; costs hedged “well into 2027”.
  • Outlook: expects to meet full-year earnings expectations.
  • CEO Nicola Frampton statement: "We have carried the positive momentum seen at the end of 2025 into 2026, with trading performing in line with our expectations" and "As we move through 2026, we remain firmly focused on growing the core business and improving our operational execution for current and future years,".

• Revenue fell 10.2% to GBP39.6m (from GBP44.1m).
  • Lettings division revenue rose 4.7%.
  • Sales division revenue fell 35%; Foxtons notes the prior-year comparator benefited from elevated volumes ahead of the stamp duty deadline.
  • New buyer activity was lower than initially expected, impacted by the Middle East crisis, subsequent mortgage rate increases, and lower mortgage product availability.
  • Management statement: "To reflect these headwinds, the group is taking action to reposition the Sales business to current market conditions."
  • Cost action: targeting at least GBP3m annualised savings, on top of GBP1.5m already delivered via relocation (effective from January).
  • Headcount to be moved from Sales into higher growth lettings activities.

• Revenue -14% to £1.12bn (from £1.30bn).
  • Gross merchandise value (GMV) -9% to £1.17bn (from £1.28bn).
  • Pretax loss narrowed to £137.9m (from £241.5m).
  • Adjusted EBITDA +51% to £64.0m (from £42.5m).
  • Adjusted loss before tax narrowed to £52.4m (from £69.5m).
  • Adjusted gross margin +330bps to 48.5% (from 45.2%) — 8th consecutive quarter of YoY improvement.
  • Management flagged “significant progress and momentum”, with improving trends in UK trading, womenswear, and new customer growth.
  • New customer growth in top four markets returned to positive on a six-month rolling basis.
  • UK GMV decline -5% YoY, outperforming group GMV decline.
  • CEO Jose Calamonte: “The first half of 2026 has seen significant progress and momentum for ASOS.”
  • CEO Jose Calamonte: “Together, we are taking decisive steps towards re-establishing ASOS as a leading online fashion destination.”
  • Middle East conflict: “We have taken proactive actions to help mitigate inflationary impacts and supply chain issues arising from the conflict in the Middle East. We continue to monitor developments closely and are continuously reviewing a range of levers to protect profitability whilst ensuring seasonally relevant product arrives to meet customer demand,”
  • Began process to pursue refunds related to ~£7m of tariffs paid in H1.
  • Trading update: current trading in line with expectations; GMV trends showing “further sequential improvement” in Q3-to-date.
  • New customer growth was +9% in March, first monthly increase since September 2021.
  • FY26 guidance unchanged: adjusted EBITDA £150m–£180m, gross margin improvement ≥100bps, and broadly neutral free cash flow.

• Reported net outflows of $1.6bn in Q1 vs JPMorgan-cited market consensus for +$1.8bn net inflows.
  • Outflows included a $6.1bn redemption by a single client in long-only systematic equity.
  • Long-only equity: -$2.8bn net outflows overall.
  • Long-only credit: +$2.2bn net inflows.
  • Assets under management (AuM) +0.5% to $228.7bn at 31 March (from $227.6bn at end-2025), but below $231.1bn market consensus.
  • AuM uplift driven by $3.1bn positive investment performance.
  • JPMorgan note referenced: first quarter of negative net flows since Q3 2024.

time_period: Q2 FY26 (quarter to March 2026); interim results due 21 May 2026 (six months to March)
body:
• Customer numbers rose by 50,000 in the quarter to 723,000.
  • Customer growth: +22% YoY and +7% QoQ.
  • Assets under administration (AUA) +20% YoY to £108.7bn.
  • AUA +0.6% QoQ.
  • Gross inflows +40% to £5.6bn.
  • Net inflows +42% to £2.7bn.
  • AUA impacted by market volatility into quarter end; market levels improved post quarter end.
  • CEO Michael Summersgill: “Whilst recent market volatility impacted asset values at the end of the quarter, customer appetite to invest remained strong in the run-up to the tax year-end,”
  • Interim results for the six months ended March expected 21 May 2026.

time_period: Share buyback programme announced 17 September 2025 (now completed)
body:
• Issued a headline alteration: the 22 April 2026 RNS headline should read “Completion of Share Buyback Programme” (text unchanged).
  • Completed its third share buyback programme (announced 17 September 2025).
  • Repurchased and cancelled 1,957,703 ordinary shares.
  • Average repurchase price approx. £5.11 per share.
  • Total cost £10m.

• Reported an “encouraging start” to 2026 and backed full-year outlook (expects to meet FY earnings expectations).
  • Total system sales +5.8% in Q1.
  • Like-for-like system sales +4.5% in Q1.
  • Total orders +2.3%; like-for-like orders +0.9%.
  • “Chick 'N' Dip” chicken concept: initial trading performance met expectations.
  • Supply/cost outlook: does not foresee supply issues; costs hedged “well into 2027”.
  • CEO Nicola Frampton: “We have carried the positive momentum seen at the end of 2025 into 2026, with trading performing in line with our expectations”.
  • CEO Nicola Frampton: “As we move through 2026, we remain firmly focused on growing the core business and improving our operational execution for current and future years,”
  • Leadership update: Frampton became permanent CEO at end-March, after serving as interim since November.

time_period: Half-year to February 2026 (interim) + first 7 weeks of H2 FY26
body:
• Suspended dividend to “reduce debt and strengthen the group's financial position.”
  • Cut FY guidance for pretax profit (before non-underlying items) to £90m–£105m (from £100m–£115m).
  • Cited a more cautious outlook due to the Middle East crisis, reflecting likely lower passenger numbers and weaker consumer confidence.
  • Pretax profit before non-underlying items fell to £3m (from £21m LY).
  • Revenue +4.5% to £748m (from £716m).
  • Company guidance caveat: “Much will depend on the peak summer trading period and the group assumes no immediate improvement in consumer confidence and assumes that jet fuel supplies can be maintained,”
  • Like-for-like revenue +2% in the first seven weeks of H2 FY26.
  • Executive Chair Leo Quinn: “The immediate focus is to restore confidence and ensure the right foundations are in place to support profitable growth and long-term value creation.”
  • Executive Chair Leo Quinn: “As a first step, the board has taken the prudent decision to suspend the dividend,” adding a focus on cash, cost discipline and balance sheet strengthening.
  • Noted backdrop: shares down sharply on the day, and the stock remains pressured after an earlier ~£30m overstatement of expected North America headline trading profit that led to the departure of the prior CEO.

• Appointed Elizabeth G. Chambers (“Libby”) as independent non-executive director, effective 1 May 2026.
  • Libby to become Chair of the Remuneration Committee, subject to regulatory approval.
  • Succession: Libby to succeed Margaret Hassall (leaving after five years on the Board).
  • Career highlights: Morgan Stanley; Harvard Business School MBA; ~10 years at McKinsey (associate → partner); C-level product/marketing/strategy roles at Barclays/Barclaycard, Western Union, Bank of America.
  • Current roles: NED at Kape Technologies, Wise plc, TSB Bank; also Chair of the Remuneration Committee at Wise and TSB.
  • Prior boards include Evelyn Partners (Tilney Smith & Williamson), 7IM, Vanquis Banking Group, Hastings Group (also chaired RemCo).
  • Chair Fiona Clutterbuck: “I am delighted to welcome Libby to the AJ Bell Board… Her extensive experience in financial services businesses and her background in marketing, organisational leadership and strategy will bring an invaluable contribution to the Board.”
  • Libby Chambers: “I'm excited to join AJ Bell… Building great organisations and brands is a passion for me, and I look forward to supporting the company and the Board in these and many other areas.”

• Contracted £23m of new headline rent in Q1 (vs £13m LY), up 43%.
  • New headline rent split: £11m from the existing portfolio; £12m from development lettings.
  • Rent reviews / renewals / regears delivered +38% uplift in the UK; +19% at group level; +4% in Europe.
  • Management: continues to “capture embedded mark-to-market rent potential in the portfolio”.
  • Customer retention 83% (vs 92% LY).
  • Occupancy 94.0% (vs 94.8% LY).
  • Middle East conflict: “no discernible impact” on leasing momentum; customer base health “remains strong”, but company is watchful for potential real estate market impacts.
  • CEO David Sleath: “Segro has had a strong start to 2026, building on the momentum seen in the second half of last year... Structural trends continue to drive occupier demand... at a time when speculative space under construction across our markets remains low.”
  • CEO David Sleath: “We expect these favourable supply-demand dynamics to support further market rental growth and create new development opportunities on Segro's exceptional land bank... Segro is well-placed to deliver further compound growth in earnings and dividends.”

• Issued $500m senior unsecured 4.625% Notes due 2031 via wholly-owned subsidiary Rentokil Terminix Funding plc, guaranteed by Rentokil Initial plc.
  • Distribution format: offered in the US to QIBs under Rule 144A and outside the US under Regulation S.
  • Use of proceeds: general corporate purposes, including debt repayment.
  • Applied to list the Notes on Euronext Dublin (Official List; trade on the Global Exchange Market).
• Director declaration: Chair Kate Swann did not seek re-election to the board of Beijer Ref AB at its AGM and will formally retire from that board at the conclusion of the AGM (today).

time_period: Q2 trading update (3 months ended 31 March 2026)
body:
• Net new customers +50,000 in the quarter to 723,000 (+22% YoY, +7% QoQ) — record quarterly growth.
  • Channel split: advised customers 189,000 (+7% YoY, +2% QoQ); D2C customers 534,000 (+28% YoY, +9% QoQ).
  • Assets under administration (AUA) £108.7bn (+20% YoY, +1% QoQ); market movements reduced AUA growth by ~2% in the quarter (management notes market levels improved post quarter-end).
  • Platform flows: gross inflows £5.6bn (+40%); net inflows £2.7bn (+42%) (both record vs prior year quarter).
  • Investment business AUM £9.8bn (+31% YoY, +3% QoQ); net inflows £0.3bn (prior year: £0.4bn).
  • CEO Michael Summersgill said:"I am delighted to report an excellent quarter of growth for our dual-channel platform, as we delivered a record 50,000 net new customers and £2.7 billion of net inflows. This performance reflects the early benefits of our previously announced increased investment in our brand and propositions."Our D2C platform delivered its strongest quarter to date, achieving record customer growth - over 50% higher than the same quarter last year - alongside record net inflows. This significant increase in new customers reflects the value placed on our low-cost propositions, ease of use and trusted brand. These factors also contributed to heightened inbound account transfers from other platforms within the market. Our Advised platform delivered record-equalling gross inflows, with strong growth partially offset by anticipated outflows associated with ongoing adviser consolidation."We operate a digital-first platform, while recognising that at key moments in the investment journey customers and advisers may want to speak to us directly. The tax year-end is the busiest time of the year for customer activity and throughout this period we maintained excellent service standards, with customers and advisers waiting an average of just eight seconds to reach our customer services team. This focus on service quality is reflected in our market-leading 4.9-star Trustpilot rating and our Which? Recommended Provider status for an eighth consecutive year."We have a proven track record of growing across different market conditions. Whilst recent market volatility impacted asset values at the end of the quarter, customer appetite to invest remained strong in the run-up to the tax year-end. The UK platform market continues to offer significant structural growth opportunities, and our diversified revenue model and highly scalable business model position us well to continue to invest for long-term, sustainable growth."
  • Interim results scheduled: 21 May 2026 (six months ended 31 March 2026).

• GMV £1,170.1m (-9%); adjusted group revenue £1,113.5m (-14%) (mix shift to commission-only flexible fulfilment models cited as structural driver).
  • Adjusted gross margin 48.5% (H1 FY25: 45.2%; +330bps); reported gross margin 48.6% (+350bps).
  • Adjusted cost to serve 43.0% (H1 FY25: 42.1%; +90bps) — includes ~200bps revenue deleverage and ~60bps structural effect from AFS growth; underlying efficiencies used partly to fund marketing investment (noted +50bps as % of sales).
  • Profitability improvement: Adjusted EBITDA £64.0m (+51%); adjusted EBIT £(18.3)m (H1 FY25: £(39.6)m); adjusted loss before tax £(52.4)m (H1 FY25: £(69.5)m).
  • Statutory metrics improved but still loss-making: operating loss £(100.9)m (H1 FY25: £(210.1)m); loss before tax £(137.9)m (H1 FY25: £(241.5)m).
  • Adjusting items in H1 included £67.3m non-cash technology asset impairment (ERP replatform to Microsoft Dynamics referenced) and £7.7m refinancing fees.
  • US tariffs/duties: ~£7m IEEPA-related tariffs recognised in H1; company says mitigations enacted and has initiated process to pursue refund claims for the £7m.
  • Free cash outflow £(92.6)m (H1 FY25: £(84.1)m; outflow £8.5m higher / ~10% worse), cited as in line with expectations given H1 seasonality and higher interest (incl 2028 convertible coupon timing).
  • Net debt (ex leases) £(294.9)m (H1 FY25: £(275.8)m; £19.1m higher / ~6.9%); cash £209.5m.
  • Financing/liquidity actions: refinanced in Nov 2025 (new £150m term loan + £87.5m delayed draw term loan to Nov 2030, undrawn at period-end); post period-end repaid 2026 convertible bonds for £73.6m cash.
  • Customer/traffic “green shoots”: new customers +9% for the Group in March (first month of growth since Sept 2021); UK GMV -5% (outperformed Group), and Womenswear growth rate improved by ~10ppts vs H2 FY25.
  • FY26 guidance unchanged: gross margin improvement ≥100bps to 48–50%; adjusted EBITDA £150m–£180m; broadly neutral free cash flow; GMV trajectory improving through the year.

• Total revenue £39.6m (-10%) (Q1 2025: £44.1m).
  • Lettings revenue £26.4m (+5%) (Q1 2025: £25.2m), comprising £0.6m organic growth + £0.9m acquisition contribution, partly offset by £0.2m lower interest on client monies.
  • Sales revenue £10.7m (-35%) (Q1 2025: £16.4m), driven by tough comparator (pre-31 March 2025 stamp duty deadline) and weaker market; vs Q1 2024, like-for-like Sales revenue +£0.2m (Q1 2024: £9.5m).
  • Financial Services revenue £2.6m (+3%) (Q1 2025: £2.5m), helped by refinance activity/ancillaries offsetting weaker purchase volumes.
  • Completed two acquisitions (Milton Keynes and Birmingham) as previously announced; expects revenue + cost synergies and supports further bolt-ons (pipeline referenced).
  • Cost actions: new programme targeting ≥£3m annualised savings underway, on top of £1.5m annualised savings from HQ relocation effective Jan 2026.
  • Trading in line with expectations; full-year guidance unchanged.
  • Guy Gittins (CEO) quote:"Our strategic focus on recurring revenues has ensured that Foxtons has delivered a resilient performance despite recent market headwinds. In the quarter, we acquired Lettings businesses in the high-growth, complementary markets of Birmingham and Milton Keynes. This, combined with organic growth and increasing take up of our property management services, meant that Lettings revenues increased 5% in the period."The implementation of the Renters' Rights Act on 1 May 2026 is expected to create growth opportunities for Foxtons. Higher regulatory requirements further underline the importance of working with a trusted, professional agent, and Foxtons' scale, expertise and compliance capabilities position the business to protect landlords' investments and capture market share."The Sales market remains subdued and has been further affected by recent events in the Middle East, which have tempered buyer sentiment and impacted mortgage rates and availability. As ever, Foxtons is focused on what we can control by managing costs, increasing efficiencies and repositioning our Sales business to mitigate the impact of the market."We remain confident that the resilience of our Lettings and Financial Services businesses, which represents more than two thirds of revenues, alongside work to reposition the Sales business, can continue to deliver market-leading results for customers, growth opportunities for our people and long-term value creation for shareholders."

• Total Group revenue £748m (+5%) (HY25: £716m); by division: UK +2%, North America +10% (constant currency), ROW/Other +8% (constant currency).
  • Like-for-like (LFL) revenue +2% for HY; in the first 7 weeks of H2, Group LFL +2% (UK flat; North America LFL +2% with core Travel Essentials LFL +6%; ROW LFL +5%).
  • Headline trading profit (pre-IFRS 16) £32m (HY25: £47m; -31.9%); headline PBT before non-underlying items £3m (HY25: £21m; -85.7%).
  • Headline diluted EPS before non-underlying items (0.8)p (HY25: 11.5p) — swing to loss.
  • Dividend suspended “to reduce debt and strengthen the Group’s financial position”.
  • FY26 guidance introduced/confirmed in the context of Middle East uncertainty: expects Headline Group PBT (before non-underlying items) £90m–£105m.
  • UK H1 impacted by (i) disruption from refurbishing multiple large airport stores and (ii) inflation headwinds; global flagship stores opened at Heathrow T3/T4/T5 in March/April.
  • North America: Travel Essentials total revenue +22% (constant currency) in H1; decisive actions in Resorts (controlled exit of loss-making fashion/specialty stores) and InMotion under review (future openings only within strategically important tenders; store count expected to decline ~20%–30% over time to <100 medium term).
  • Balance sheet/leverage: Headline net debt £496m (Aug 2025: £390m); leverage 2.9x (Aug 2025: 2.1x). Net debt incl IFRS 16 leases £1,010m.
  • Liquidity/near-term maturity: £327m convertible bond matures 7 May 2026; term loan expected to be drawn ahead of repayment.
  • Cash flow: Headline EBITDA £48m (HY25: £62m); capex £50m (HY25: £38m); free cash outflow £61m (HY25: £72m). FY26 capex expected ~£90m; FY26 headline net debt expected ~£420m.
  • Regulatory: continues to cooperate with the FCA re investigation into compliance with Listing Principles/Rules and DTRs linked to the North America accounting issue (previously announced 19 Nov 2025).
  • Leo Quinn (Executive Chair) quote:"The immediate focus is to restore confidence and ensure the right foundations are in place to support profitable growth and long-term value creation."Moving forward, the Board and management team will have a relentless focus on driving cash, cost discipline and strengthening the balance sheet. As a first step, the Board has taken the prudent decision to suspend the dividend."This is a business with a strong brand and proposition in high-footfall travel markets and the new flagship stores opened across Heathrow airport are raising the global standard for travel essentials retail."None of this is achievable without our people. Making sure our colleagues are empowered is a key priority, as engaged teams execute better, serve customers better and drive higher performance over time."While the near-term outlook is uncertain, I am confident that, with the right focus and discipline, the business can deliver superior returns for the benefit of our colleagues, partners and shareholders over the longer-term."

• Simon Roberts (CEO) said:"More and more customers are choosing Sainsbury's for more of their shopping, trusting us to deliver great value day in day out. The conflict in the Middle East means customers are even more focused on the cost of living and we are absolutely committed to making sure everyone gets the best possible value when they shop with us."By staying relentlessly focused on the things that matter most - value, quality, availability and service - we have outperformed the market for the sixth year in a row. Rather than pass through the full extent of cost inflation, we invested to sustain the strength of our competitive position while also refreshing stores, improving digital experiences and increasing colleague pay by five per cent."We offer the biggest Aldi Price Match in the market, with great prices on everyday essentials and even more value through Nectar Prices and personalised Your Nectar Prices. Alongside our standout fresh food offer and the growing strength of Taste the Difference, we are well placed to be first choice for more customers."Long term partnerships with thousands of farmers and suppliers are key to delivering good food at great value. We have committed to invest more than £5 billion in British and Irish farming over the coming years, making our supply base more resilient at a time of increased challenges."Our balanced choices reflect a consistent long-term approach to creating value for shareholders: strengthening our relationships with customers, colleagues and suppliers and building a stronger Sainsbury's for the future."We will do everything we can to support our customers and colleagues over the coming months, with absolute focus on keeping prices low. We have made a positive start to the new financial year, with continued strong Grocery momentum."I would like to thank all our colleagues, farmers and suppliers for their brilliant commitment and hard work - it's their dedication that makes such a difference for our customers every time they shop with us."
  • Group revenue (ex VAT, inc fuel) £33.647bn, up 2.7% (FY25: £32.772bn).
  • Retail sales (ex VAT, ex fuel) £29.992bn, up 4.3% (FY25: £28.754bn).
  • Like-for-like sales (ex fuel) +3.9% (FY25: +3.4%); like-for-like (inc fuel) +2.5% (FY25: +1.8%).
  • FY sales (ex fuel): Sainsbury’s £25.9bn (+4.9%), Argos £4.1bn (+0.7%), Fuel £3.6bn (-8.2%).
  • Grocery sales +5.2% (strong volume growth + market share gains); Fresh food sales +8%; Taste the Difference sales ahead of £2bn target (Fresh food TTD +16%).
  • Retail underlying operating profit £1,025m (-1.1%); retail underlying operating margin 3.06% (FY25: 3.17%, -11bps). Retail underlying EBITDA margin 6.59% (FY25: 6.72%, -13bps).
  • Total underlying operating profit £1,025m (+1.1%) (FY25: £1,014m) driven by Financial Services moving from £(22)m to £0m (break-even).
  • Underlying PBT £718m (+1.3%); underlying EPS 22.3p (+3.2%).
  • Statutory profit after tax £393m (+55.3%); non-underlying items £(115)m (FY25: £(251)m).
  • Retail free cash flow £574m (+8.1%) (FY25: £531m); cash capex £843m (FY25: £825m; +2.2%).
  • Net debt (incl leases) £5.743bn (broadly flat vs £5.758bn); non-lease net debt £203m (FY25: £264m; £61m lower).
  • Proposed full-year dividend 13.7p (+0.7%); final dividend 9.6p (payable 10 July 2026, record date 5 June 2026).
  • Cash returns: ordinary dividends £316m + core buyback £200m completed; bank disposal proceeds returned via £250m special dividend + £50m buyback; FY26/27 buyback planned £300m (core £200m + additional £100m bank proceeds).
  • 2026/27 guidance (explicitly framed by Middle East uncertainty): Total underlying operating profit £975m–£1,075m; Retail free cash flow >£500m.
  • Stores/space: opened 10 supermarkets and 33 convenience stores; plan to open ~10 supermarkets and ≥20 convenience stores in 2026/27; expected net space growth impact ~0.5% to sales in 2026/27.
  • Online: Groceries Online sales +13%; OnDemand sales +69% to >£700m, now covering ~70% of UK population.
  • Cost programme: delivered ~£680m cost savings since Feb 2024; target £1bn over three years to 2026/27; delivered a further £330m structural cost savings in FY26.
  • Financial Services: exit from core banking materially completed; new partnership with NatWest announced April 2026 (loans, savings, NatWest Nectar credit card expected H2 2026); expects to surrender banking licence by July 2026; Financial Services no longer to be reported as a separate operating segment.
  • Pension: IAS 19 defined benefit surplus £525m (down £206m YoY); 2024 triennial valuation completed 20 March 2026 (surplus £171m incl ABC; £15m excl); escrow established (no funds deposited as at 28 Feb 2026).
  • Management framing on performance/cash returns: "We delivered a resilient operating profit performance despite significant cost inflation, whilst generating strong free cash flow and returning more than £800 million to shareholders."

• Reported Q1 2026 revenue $1.9bn and operating income $1.3bn; net income $1.2bn; diluted EPS $3.18.
  • Adjusted results: operating income $1.4bn; adjusted net income $1.2bn; adjusted diluted EPS $3.36 (excluding specified items per reconciliation).
  • CEO Terry Duffy quote: “In a world in which risk has become the new normal, 2026 is off to a record-breaking start…” and “Robust demand for our products drove Q1 average daily volume up 22% to a record 36.2 million contracts, including records in all six asset classes.”
  • Duffy also said: “This exceptional market participation translated directly into record financial performance, with revenue rising 14% and adjusted net income and diluted EPS increasing 20%.”
  • Duffy: “over $85 billion in average daily margin savings” and “extend our FICC cross-margining agreement to end-user clients later this month.”
  • Q1 ADV 36.2m (+22% YoY); non-U.S. ADV 11.4m (+30% YoY). Regional records: APAC +33%, EMEA +29%.
  • Clearing & transaction fees revenue $1.5bn (record); average rate per contract $0.652.
  • Market data revenue $224m (record).
  • Balance sheet (31 Mar 2026): $2.6bn cash (includes $200m deposited with FICC) and $3.4bn debt.
  • Capital returns in Q1: ~$2.7bn dividends paid and $536m share repurchases.

• Appointed Rachel Kentleton as a non-executive director.
  • Ruth Anderson to retire at the conclusion of the AGM on 14 May 2026 (NED since 2023).
  • Kentleton background: former CFO of St Modwen Properties; previously Finance Director at PayPoint PLC.

• Q1 sales GBP431m, down 2.5% YoY (vs GBP442m), but +1% at constant exchange rates (CER).
  • Segment performance: Consumer Care GBP255m (flat YoY); Life Sciences GBP126m (-6.0%); Industrial Specialties GBP50m (-5.7%).
  • Management flagged a strong Q1 2025 comparator; growth in Beauty Actives and Fragrances & Flavours offset weaker Crop Protection, which “not benefiting from the customer inventory rebuild that positively impacted our performance in 2025”.
  • Regional: all regions except North America grew; Latin America +4% reported / +11% CER (ag momentum). North America -13% reported / -7% CER as Crop Protection demand normalised, plus weather and consumer cost pressures.
  • Middle East conflict: “did not have a material effect” in Q1; region ~5% of total sales, mainly Fragrances & Flavours.
  • Pricing: “We are actively and responsibly managing the impact of the conflict, increasing prices to fully recover input cost inflation.”
  • Medium-term framework reaffirmed: “we are confident of delivering an improving performance over the next three years” (more consistent sales growth, higher profitability, stronger cashflows, improved returns).
  • Maintained 2026 outlook: organic sales growth 3% to 6%, adjusted operating margin growth, and adjusted operating profit in line with market expectations at constant currency.
  • Transformation programme: “Implementation of our transformation programme remains on track” and “a significant proportion of future margin improvement is in our control and not dependent on market recovery.”

time_period: Q1 2026 (quarter ended 31 March 2026; AUMA update also as at 17 April 2026)
body:
• AUMA GBP547.7bn at 31 Mar 2026, down 1.5% QoQ (vs GBP556.0bn at 31 Dec 2025) and up 9.5% YoY (vs GBP500.1bn).
  • Q1 movement reflected sale of the financial planning business, lower markets, and net outflows of GBP2.9bn.
  • Net outflows improved vs prior-year quarter: GBP2.9bn vs GBP5.2bn (improvement of GBP2.3bn, ~44% lower outflows YoY).
  • Outflows driven by “anticipated redemptions in the Investments unit” reflecting “geopolitical uncertainty”.
  • Estimated AUMA as at 17 Apr: GBP573bn (management highlighting ongoing market volatility).
  • Described overall performance as “robust” “despite market headwinds”, with “particular strength in interactive investor”.
  • interactive investor delivered a record quarter; net flows GBP3.0bn (vs GBP1.6bn prior year) and customer numbers +14% YoY, supported by strong growth in SIPP customers.
  • Adviser arm: stable net outflows of GBP600m YoY.
  • Reiterated commitment to 2026 targets: adjusted operating profit ≥ GBP300m (vs GBP264m in 2025; +~13.6%) and net capital generation GBP300m (vs GBP239m; +~25.5%).

• Andrew Higginson will step down as Chair at the AGM on 21 July 2026 (chair since 2022).
  • Darren Shapland (NED) to become interim Chair after the AGM.
  • Kath Smith (Senior Independent Director) to lead the search for a permanent successor.
  • Higginson quote: “We have focused the business back to sports fashion, bought out minority interests in subsidiaries, and accelerated our expansion outside JD's UK market, particularly in the US,” adding it was “a tough period in the sportswear market”.
  • JD Sports notes ~40% of the business is now in North America.
  • Regis Shultz remains CEO.
  • Current stock price (as stated): 7.

time_period: Q1 2026 (three months ended 31 March 2026) and FY 2026 guidance update
body:
• Q1 highlights: Total revenue $3.985bn; rental revenue $3.419bn (both record Q1).
  • Profitability: net income $531m (margin 13.3%); GAAP diluted EPS $8.43; adjusted EPS $9.71.
  • Cash flow/capex: operating cash flow $1.514bn; free cash flow $1.054bn; gross rental capex $874m (incl. $767m gross payments for rental equipment purchases).
  • Shareholder returns: $500m returned in Q1 ($375m buybacks, $125m dividends).
  • Leverage/liquidity: net leverage ratio 1.9x; total liquidity $3.377bn at 31 March 2026 (incl. $156m cash).
  • Operating drivers: fleet productivity +2.3% YoY; average OEC +5.7% YoY supporting rental revenue growth.
  • Segment detail: General rentals rental revenue +6.2% YoY to $2.229bn; rental gross margin +150 bps to 33.8% (lower depreciation, labour/benefits, delivery costs as % of revenue).
  • Segment detail: Specialty rentals rental revenue +13.8% YoY to $1.190bn; rental gross margin -170 bps to 41.4% (higher depreciation, delivery costs, mix shift to lower-margin ancillary revenues).
  • Restructuring: $45m restructuring charges in Q1 (branch consolidations and other cost-reduction measures under program initiated in Q4 2025); restructuring-related payments $13m in Q1.
  • Guidance change: raises FY 2026 outlook — revenue to $16.9bn–$17.4bn (from $16.8bn–$17.3bn); adjusted EBITDA to $7.625bn–$7.875bn (from $7.575bn–$7.825bn); net rental capex after gross purchases to $2.95bn–$3.35bn (from $2.85bn–$3.25bn) with gross purchases $4.4bn–$4.8bn (from $4.3bn–$4.7bn); operating cash flow to $5.4bn–$6.2bn (from $5.3bn–$6.1bn).
  • Buybacks/dividend: completed prior $2.0bn repurchase program in Q1; commenced new $5.0bn program; expects $1.5bn repurchases in 2026 (incl. $1.15bn under the $5.0bn program). Declared quarterly dividend $1.97/share, payable 27 May 2026 (record date 13 May 2026).
  • CEO quote: “I am very pleased with our strong start to 2026… We reported first-quarter records in EPS, adjusted EBITDA and revenue…”
  • CEO quote: “The increases to our full-year guidance are supported by the momentum we are carrying into our busy season… Longer-term, the flexibility and resiliency of our business model… positions us to deliver industry-leading growth, profitability and cash generation…”

time_period: Leadership changes effective immediately (announcement date not stated)
body:
• Appoints John Martin as Chief Financial Officer, effective immediately.
  • Promotes Mark Jones, Jr. to President and Chief Operating Officer; he continues reporting to CEO Mark Miller.
  • John Martin background (as stated): most recently CFO at a private equity-backed e-commerce platform; prior roles at Highbridge Capital Management, Providence Equity Partners, and Morgan Stanley (IBD); Duke University (summa cum laude).
  • Mark Jones, Jr. quote: “John brings strong financial and strategic experience across operating companies, capital markets and e-commerce platforms… We are excited to welcome John to Goosehead and believe he will be a strong addition to our leadership team.”
  • John Martin quote: “I am honored to join Goosehead at such an exciting time for the business… I look forward to working alongside the talented leadership team to continue scaling the organization and driving long-term growth.”
  • Mark Miller quote on Jones: “Mark has been instrumental in building the foundation of our business, and I look forward to working closely with him as he focuses on operational execution and continuing to drive the company forward.”

time_period: FY ended 28 February (year not stated); includes references to Q3 and expectations for Q4
body:
• Consensus expectation: retail underlying operating profit GBP1.03bn (vs GBP1.04bn, -0.6% YoY).
  • Consensus expectation: underlying pre-tax profit GBP730m (vs GBP709m, +3.0% YoY).
  • Q3 trading snapshot (as previously reported): Grocery sales +5.4%; General Merchandise & Clothing -1.1%; Argos sales -1.0%.
  • Hargreaves Lansdown (Aarin Chiekrie) on mix: “Sainsbury's grocery sales continued to impress in the third quarter, rising 5.4% as it gained market share… Taste the Difference range grow by an impressive 15%. However, its general merchandise and Argos divisions were a small drag on performance… the picture could get tougher in the coming year if oil prices remain elevated, squeezing consumers' budgets,” Chiekrie said.
  • Deutsche Bank expectation for Q4: like-for-like sales +3.1%, plus +0.6% from new space.
  • Deutsche Bank on mix and inflation: “Within the mix, we expect Grocery sales +4.9%, GM and Clothing -1.1.% and Argos -2.2%… Recent data points to softer grocery inflation in January before a pick-up in February and March,” Deutsche analysts said.
  • Deutsche Bank profit view: retail underlying operating profit forecast GBP1.05bn (vs GBP1.03bn consensus).
  • “Key debate” flagged for results day: commentary on cost pressures tied to Middle East conflict, potential food inflation implications, any demand impact so far, and profit guidance range sensitivity.
  • Shareholder returns expectations (preview): dividend expected 13.9p (vs 13.6p); AJ Bell notes hopes for a buyback, tempered by uncertainty.
  • Argos strategic context: recap that Sainsbury’s terminated Argos sale discussions after sought to proceed on a “materially revised set of terms and commitments” not deemed in stakeholders’ best interests.
  • Shore Capital on Argos process: “Sainsbury… walked away from a scenario that did not, in the end, work for all of its stakeholders… Argos is a business that has the basis to flourish in a more synergistic home… Sainsbury is also correct to seek to engineer an ongoing improvement… through its ‘More Argos, more often' approach,” Shore analysts commented.

• Reports “further strong progress” across the business over 1 Oct–21 Apr, building on “significant momentum” delivered in H1.
  • Flags significant letting progress, with increased enquiries in the Old Street sub-market now translating into tangible letting activity (no numbers disclosed in the text provided).

time_period: FY ending 3 October (guidance update); trading commentary references first three months of 2025 and cash at end-March 2026
body:
• Cuts FY guidance to an adjusted pre-tax loss of GBP1.0m–GBP2.0m, versus prior expectation of ~GBP1.0m profit (and GBP2.4m profit last year), implying a swing of ~GBP2m–GBP3m vs prior view.
  • Attributes weaker trading to challenging conditions in the first three months of 2025, citing the UK government’s last two budgets and Middle East geopolitical issues weakening consumer confidence.
  • Management quote: “These macroeconomic factors have increased customer caution, leading to lower footfall, less discretionary spend and additional costs such as container prices and transportation costs, with a resultant reduction in revenue and profit,” it says.
  • Expects H2 trading and costs will also be impacted (no quantification given).
  • Balance sheet/liquidity: “We remain debt free and confident in our cash management, with cash levels at the end of March 2026 higher than the year end position for FY25,” the firm adds.
  • Interim results due early May.

• Q1 trading: total system sales +5.8% YoY.
  • Like-for-like sales +4.5% YoY.
  • Total orders +2.3% YoY.
  • Like-for-like orders +0.9% YoY.
  • Product news: launched CHICK ’N’ DIP; initial performance met expectations with positive customer feedback/advocacy.
  • Cost / supply: costs hedged for FY26, with some hedged into 2027; Board does not currently foresee supply-related issues.
  • Outlook: Board maintains full-year earnings expectations.
  • CEO Nicola Frampton: "We have carried the positive momentum seen at the end of 2025 into 2026, with trading performing in line with our expectations… We are pleased with the early performance of CHICK 'N' DIP and excited for our Italianos range… As we move through 2026, we remain firmly focused on growing the core business and improving our operational execution…"

• Contracted £23m of new headline rent in Q1, including £12m from development lettings (and £11m from the existing portfolio).
  • Rent capture: uplift on rent reviews/renewals/regears UK +38% (Group +19%, Continental Europe +4%).
  • Operating metrics: customer retention 83%; occupancy 94.8% (vacancy reduced in London; Germany urban speculative completions with multiple lease negotiations ongoing).
  • Development completions: 40,000 sq m completed with £4m of headline rent; 37% leased (lower than usual due to mix skewed to urban speculative completions).
  • Development pipeline: projects under construction/advanced negotiations equate to £73m potential rent; 67% pre-let; target development yield 7.6%.
  • Reiterated 2026 development capex guidance: £450–550m.
  • Data centres: signed a 30,000 sq m powered shell pre-let on Slough Trading Estate; secured planning for first 56MW fully fitted data centre in West London; progressing major power upgrades in Slough.
  • Capital recycling: £106m disposals completed above book value (with £5m associated rent); further £138m exchanged to complete later in 2026 (c. 75% completed/exchanged since start of March).
  • Balance sheet/liquidity: LTV 31%; £1.5bn cash and undrawn committed facilities; repaid March 2026 bond maturity using existing committed facilities.
  • SELP JV financing: issued €500m senior unsecured notes at 3.875% coupon to refinance existing debt and for general corporate purposes.
  • CEO David Sleath: "SEGRO has had a strong start to 2026, building on the momentum seen in the second half of last year… The Middle East conflict has, so far, had no discernible effect on our leasing momentum… but we remain watchful… We expect these favourable supply-demand dynamics to support further market rental growth… SEGRO is well-placed to deliver further compound growth in earnings and dividends."

time_period: CEO succession update (effective 1 September 2026, subject to regulatory approval)
body:
• Subject to regulatory approval, Enrique Alvarez Labiano will take up the role of CEO on 1 September 2026.
  • Enrique will be appointed as an executive director of OSB Group plc, OneSavings Bank plc, and Charter Court Financial Services Limited with effect from 1 September 2026.
  • Enrique succeeds Andy Golding, who will retire on 31 August 2026 (retirement intention announced 13 November 2025).
  • Company states: “There is no further information to be disclosed pursuant to Listing Rule 6.4.8R.”

time_period: Quarter ended 31 March 2026 (AUM as at 31 March 2026)
body:
• Total AUM $228.7bn at 31 Mar 2026 vs $227.6bn at 31 Dec 2025 (+$1.1bn / +0.5%).
  • Q1 AUM bridge: net flows -$1.6bn, investment performance +$3.1bn, other -$0.4bn.
  • Net flows included a $6.1bn redemption from a single client in long-only equity (systematic).
  • Alternative AUM $106.9bn (from $103.9bn): net flows -$1.0bn, performance +$3.0bn, other **+$1.0bn.
  • Long-only AUM $121.8bn (from $123.7bn): net flows -$0.6bn, performance +$0.1bn, other **-$1.4bn.
  • Segment moves:• Liquid alternative $89.8bn (from $86.8bn).• Private market $17.1bn (flat).• Long-only equity $86.4bn (from $90.0bn).• Long-only credit $35.4bn (from $33.7bn).
  • Selected fund performance (net of fees) over 3 months to 31 Mar 2026 highlights: AHL Alpha +5.7%, Man Alternative Risk Premia +4.7%, Man Japan CoreAlpha Equity +5.0%; weak spots include Man Continental European Growth -9.7%, Numeric Global Core -3.3%, Man Undervalued Assets -3.3%.
  • Provided both current and legacy reporting AUM bridges and product/category tables (showing comparatives across 31 Mar 2025, 30 Jun 2025, 30 Sep 2025, 31 Dec 2025, 31 Mar 2026).
