time_period: Leasing agreements at Tritax Park Newark (new 20-year letting + 10-year extension)
body:
• Completed contracts with Currys on two leases at Tritax Park Newark, securing £9.5m annual rent (pre rent reviews) for 20 years across 1.2m sq ft.
  • New letting: 0.4m sq ft recently completed speculative development now let to Currys on a 20-year lease.
  • New building specs: BREEAM Excellent, EPC A, 557KWp rooftop solar; portfolio solar capacity increased to 29.0MWp.
  • Development economics: letting delivers a development yield on cost in line with guidance of 6–8%.
  • Existing facility: 10-year lease extension on Currys’ 0.8m sq ft building, taking remaining lease term to 20 years.
  • Phase 2 pipeline: plans being progressed for up to 970,000 sq ft design-and-build opportunities plus a 40-acre country park.
  • Tritax (Charlie Withers): "We are delighted to both let a brand new development and extend the lease on our existing building with Currys… The lettings evidence Tritax Big Box's strategic advantage of being both a long-term investor and developer… Combined, the leases are a strong endorsement of the gathering momentum of Tritax Big Box's lettings pipeline…"
  • Currys (Lindsay Haselhurst): "Newark is the centre of our UK supply chain operations, supporting over £5 billion of annual sales… Extending our operations… will enable continued growth of our omnichannel model and support further market share gains."
  • Simons Developments (Philip Hodgkinson): "We're delighted to have completed this high-quality building and to see it fully let to Currys… a clear demonstration of what our partnership with Tritax Big Box can deliver…"

time_period: Board / governance changes (effective immediately and from 30 June 2026)
body:
• Colette Burke (Non-Executive Director) will step down from the Board on 30 June 2026 (served three years).
  • Senior Independent Director Simon Emeny: "I would like to thank Colette for her contribution to the Board. She has provided thoughtful challenge and valuable perspective during a period of significant change for the Group."
  • Situl Jobanputra becomes Chair of the Remuneration Committee with immediate effect.

time_period: Annual Report year ended 31 December 2025 (with comparatives at 31 December 2024 and 31 December 2023)
body:
• Corrected ESG-integrated AUM (as should have been shown in the FY25 Annual Report, page 21): $103.9bn (31 Dec 2025); $60.6bn (31 Dec 2024); $41.8bn (31 Dec 2023).
  • Correction arose after an internal process review and reclassification of a non-statutory metric.
  • No impact on previously reported total AUM, individual fund-level classifications, or prior remuneration outcomes.
  • AGM reminder: proxy voting deadline is 4.00pm (UK) Tuesday 5 May 2026.

time_period: Board change effective AGM on 14 May 2026
body:
• Appoints Rachel Kentleton as non-executive director.
  • Kentleton to become chair of the audit committee and member of nomination and remuneration committees.
  • Ruth Anderson to retire from the board at the conclusion of the 14 May AGM (served since 2023).
  • Kentleton previously CFO of St Modwen Properties Ltd and finance director at PayPoint PLC.

• Q1 attributable production:

– 1.6m oz silver (vs 1.8m oz Q4 2025; 1.8m oz YoY).

– 55,252 oz gold (vs 57,410 oz Q4; 58,020 oz YoY).

– Gold equivalent production 75,599 oz (vs 81,330 Q4).

  • Full-year 2026 production guidance unchanged at 300,000–328,000 gold equivalent ounces.
  • Maintained 2026 all-in sustaining cost guidance of USD2,157–USD2,320/oz gold equivalent.
  • Average realised prices sharply higher YoY:

– Gold USD4,471/oz (vs USD2,708).

– Silver USD89.08/oz (vs USD33.2).

  • Net cash position of USD95m at 31 March, versus USD23m net debt at end-2025 (material balance sheet improvement).
  • CEO Eduardo Landin: “We have made a solid start to the year… we are on track to meet our full-year production and cost guidance.”
  • Targeting Q3 2026 for project approval and construction start at Monte Do Carmo (Brazil).

• Revenue GBP431m, down 2.5% reported (GBP442m), but +1% at constant exchange rates (CER).
  • Consumer Care revenue GBP255m (flat YoY).
  • Life Sciences revenue GBP126m, down 6.0% (GBP134m).
  • Industrial Specialties revenue GBP50m, down 5.7% (GBP53m).
  • Growth in Beauty Actives and Fragrances & Flavours offset weaker Crop Protection, which did not benefit from prior-year customer inventory rebuild.
  • Regional performance:

– Latin America +4% reported, +11% CER.

– North America -13% reported, -7% CER, reflecting normalising Crop Protection demand and weather/cost pressures.

  • Middle East conflict had no material Q1 impact (~5% of sales exposure, mainly Fragrances & Flavours).
  • “We are actively and responsibly managing the impact of the conflict, increasing prices to fully recover input cost inflation.”
  • 2026 guidance maintained:

– Organic sales growth 3%–6%.

– Adjusted operating margin growth.

– Adjusted operating profit in line with market expectations at CER.

  • Transformation programme on track, with “a significant proportion of future margin improvement… in our control and not dependent on market recovery.”

• Assets under management & administration (AUMA) of GBP547.7bn, down 1.5% QoQ (GBP556.0bn) but up 9.5% YoY (GBP500.1bn).
  • Quarterly movement reflects sale of financial planning business, weaker markets, and net outflows of GBP2.9bn (improved from GBP5.2bn outflows YoY).
  • Outflows driven by “anticipated redemptions in the Investments unit” amid “geopolitical uncertainty”.
  • AUMA estimated at GBP573bn as at 17 April, indicating recovery post quarter-end.
  • interactive investor delivered a record quarter:

– Net inflows of GBP3.0bn, up from GBP1.6bn YoY.

– Customer numbers up 14% YoY, supported by strong SIPP growth.

  • Adviser arm reported stable net outflows of GBP600m YoY.
  • Reiterates commitment to 2026 targets:

– Adjusted operating profit ≥GBP300m (vs GBP264m in 2025).

– Net capital generation GBP300m (vs GBP239m in 2025).

  • Described performance as “robust” despite market headwinds, with particular strength in interactive investor.

• Heavy mineral concentrate (HMC) production 217,200t (-30% YoY), “primarily due to the ongoing debottlenecking of Wet Concentrator Plant A.”
  • Ilmenite production 125,900t (-38% YoY); total shipments 277,900t (-10% YoY), stated as in line with run-rate for annual guidance (>1.1Mt).
  • Notes progress in debottlenecking and zircon price recovery, but softer titanium feedstock market.
  • In talks with the Mozambican government to renew Kenmare's licence / agreement at Moma.
  • Progressing “recovery of value” from shipments made in Q3 2025 to a customer in financial distress.
  • Stockpiles: has drawn down 99,900t of finished product inventories by end-Q1, consistent with “value over volume” approach.
  • Outlook: production expected to strengthen from Q2 as WCP A increasingly operates at nameplate capacity; warns geopolitical volatility and liquidity prioritisation reduce flexibility and may limit responsiveness.

time_period: Refinancing / consent solicitation results (Expiration 21 April 2026; redemption 23 April 2026; settlement expected 24 April 2026)
body:
• Update relates to $1,285,245,000 10.25% Senior Secured Notes due 2026 and the proposed Refinancing Transaction.
  • Consent Solicitation expired 11:59pm New York time on 21 April 2026; Tullow received valid, unrevoked consents representing >99% of outstanding principal.
  • Settlement Date expected 24 April 2026; consents can’t be revoked as the Revocation Deadline has passed.
  • On 10 April 2026, Tullow and relevant parties executed:• Second Supplemental Indenture (operative immediately): permits pro rata redemption, reduces minimum denominations, and implements the Waiver.
  • Third Supplemental Indenture: reflects other proposed amendments, becoming operative on Settlement Date subject to conditions.
  • Mechanics for all holders (whether or not they participated):• 23 April 2026: portion of existing Notes redeemed at par, pro rata, plus accrued/unpaid interest on redeemed amount.
  • 24 April 2026: holders receive New Notes, accrued/unpaid interest on remaining existing Notes, and a 1.00% fee calculated on principal outstanding at the Expiration Time.

time_period: Director declaration (effective at Shaftesbury Capital AGM 14 May 2026)
body:
• Trustpilot confirms (per UK Listing Rule 6.4.9R(2)) that Rachel Kentleton (independent NED of Trustpilot) has been appointed independent NED of Shaftesbury Capital, effective at the conclusion of Shaftesbury Capital’s AGM on 14 May 2026.
  • At Shaftesbury Capital, Kentleton will be Chair of the Audit Committee and a member of the Nomination and Remuneration committees.
  • Shaftesbury Capital listings noted: LSE (primary); JSE and A2X (secondary).

• Confirms it “has been in discussions regarding a possible offer” for Deltic Energy plc.
  • Explicitly a Rule 2.4 Code announcement: “does not constitute an announcement of a firm intention to make an offer” (no certainty an offer will be made, nor terms).
  • Advises shareholders to take no action at this stage.
  • States a further announcement will be made “if and when appropriate.”
  • Notes (per the accompanying summary) Capricorn has itself been subject to recent interest, including an approach from Alamadiyaf al-Masiyyah for Trading LLC.

• Trustpilot NED Rachel Kentleton appointed independent non-executive director of Shaftesbury Capital PLC, effective from conclusion of Shaftesbury Capital’s AGM on 14 May 2026.
  • On joining Shaftesbury Capital’s board, she will be chair of the audit committee and a member of the nomination and remuneration committees.
  • Notes Shaftesbury Capital listings: LSE (primary), JSE (secondary), and A2X (secondary).

JD Sports Fashion PLC

Publish Date 23 April 2026

Time Period Board change effective AGM on 21 July 2026

  • Chair Andrew Higginson to step down at the AGM on 21 July, having served since 2022.
  • Non-Executive Director Darren Shapland to become interim chair following the AGM.
  • Senior Independent Director Kath Smith to lead the search for a permanent successor.
  • Higginson said his tenure covered “a tough period in the sportswear market”, adding: “We have focused the business back to sports fashion, bought out minority interests in subsidiaries, and accelerated our expansion outside JD's UK market, particularly in the US.”
  • JD Sports notes ~40% of group business now in North America, underlining geographic shift.
  • Regis Shultz remains chief executive officer (no change to executive leadership).

time_period: Consent Solicitation results to 21 April 2026 (Settlement expected 24 April 2026)
body:
• Consent Solicitation expired 11:59pm New York City time, 21 April 2026.
  • Received valid, unrevoked consents representing >99% of outstanding principal of the $1.285bn 10.25% senior secured notes due 2026.
  • Settlement Date expected 24 April 2026; revocation is no longer possible (revocation deadline passed).
  • On 10 April 2026, executed: • Second Supplemental Indenture (operative immediately) to permit pro rata redemption at par, reduce minimum denominations, and implement the Waiver. • Third Supplemental Indenture to reflect other Proposed Indenture Amendments (operative only on Settlement Date subject to conditions).
  • Mechanics (subject to conditions): all holders (whether they consented or not) will: • On 23 April 2026: have a portion redeemed at par, pro rata, and receive accrued and unpaid interest on the redeemed amount. • On 24 April 2026: receive New Notes, accrued and unpaid interest on remaining existing notes, and a 1.00% fee calculated on the aggregate principal outstanding at expiration.

time_period: FY ending 3 October (guidance update; trading/cash commentary up to end-March 2026)
body:
• Guidance cut: now expects adjusted PBT loss of GBP1.0m–GBP2.0m (vs prior expectation of GBP1.0m profit).
  • Compares to prior year: GBP2.4m adjusted PBT profit last year (implying a deterioration of GBP3.4m–GBP4.4m YoY).
  • Attributes weak early-year trading to UK government’s last two budgets and Middle East geopolitical issues hurting confidence; says this has driven lower footfall, lower discretionary spend, and higher costs (incl. container and transport).
  • Shoe Zone: “These macroeconomic factors have increased customer caution, leading to lower footfall, less discretionary spend and additional costs such as container prices and transportation costs, with a resultant reduction in revenue and profit,” it says.
  • Expects H2 trading and costs “will also be impacted.”
  • Liquidity statement: “We remain debt free and confident in our cash management, with cash levels at the end of March 2026 higher than the year end position for FY25,” the firm adds.
  • Interim results due early May.

• Reports “further strong progress” across the business over the period, building on “significant momentum” delivered in H1.
  • Highlights “significant letting progress” with a rise in enquiries in the Old Street sub-market now translating into “tangible letting activity”.

time_period: 3 months to 31 March 2026 (with AUM update to 20 April 2026)
body:
• Net outflows of GBP836m, down ~35% vs GBP1.28bn in the prior-year quarter.
  • AUM at 31 March 2026: GBP19.55bn, down ~13% vs GBP22.59bn a year ago.
  • AUM at 20 April 2026: GBP20.75bn, up ~6% vs 31 March.
  • Liontrust: “The extreme volatility and dislocations we have seen in markets over the past couple of weeks are demonstrating the importance of, and opportunities for, active management and stock picking,” Liontrust says.
  • CEO John Ions: “It feels that over the past few years, the only certainty has been uncertainty. The current day-to-day unpredictability and fluctuations in markets reinforces our belief in active management and the long-term power of robust and repeatable investment processes.”

• Appointed Rachel Kentleton as independent Non-Executive Director and Chair of the Audit Committee, effective at conclusion of AGM on 14 May 2026.
  • Ruth Anderson to retire as NED and Audit Committee Chair at conclusion of the 2026 AGM; resolution to re-elect her will be withdrawn.
  • Kentleton background highlighted: former CFO St Modwen Properties (2021–2023); former Group FD PayPoint; prior senior roles at easyJet (strategy/implementation).
  • Current external roles: Senior Independent Director + Audit & Risk Chair at JET2, Audit & Risk Chair at Trustpilot, Audit Committee Chair at Travelodge.
  • Chair Jonathan Nicholls: thanked Anderson for contribution and said Kentleton’s “extensive financial expertise and experience as an audit committee chair” will complement the Board.

• Chair Andrew Higginson to step down at conclusion of JD’s AGM on 21 July 2026 (Chair since July 2022).
  • Succession process initiated, led by Kath Smith (Senior Independent Director).
  • Darren Shapland to become Interim Chair following the 2026 AGM until a permanent Chair is appointed (current Independent NED since June 2023; chairs ESG Committee).
  • Higginson quote: “We have focused the business back to sports fashion, bought out minority interests in subsidiaries, and accelerated our expansion outside JD's UK market, particularly in the US. … our focus on business discipline and cashflow leaves JD well placed…”
  • Kath Smith quote: “On behalf of the Board, I would like to express our gratitude to Andy for his extensive contributions…”
  • CEO Regis Schultz quote: “I'm grateful to Andy for his support and counsel through a critical and transformational period for the Group…”

• Announced commencement of share buyback programme of up to £750m by April 2027.
  • Entered arrangement with Citigroup Global Markets Limited to repurchase shares on Tesco’s behalf (riskless principal) on LSE and/or Cboe Europe (BXE/CXE).
  • First tranche (“Initial Programme”) set at up to £250m; any further tranches to be announced later.
  • Citi must operate independently of Tesco (including purchases during closed periods).
  • Buyback constraints referenced: pricing capped at the higher of (i) 105% of 5-day average middle market closing price and (ii) higher of last independent trade and highest current independent bid on venue.
  • Stated purpose: reduce the Company’s share capital.

time_period: Third quarter ended 31 March 2026 (Q3 FY2026) and nine months to 31 March 2026
body:
• Copper: Q3 production 476,800t (-7.1% YoY); sales 118,700t (-11% YoY).
  • Iron ore: Q3 production 62.8Mt (+1.7% YoY); sales 60.2Mt (-0.7% YoY).
  • Steelmaking coal: Q3 production 3.8Mt (-2.6% YoY); sales 3.9Mt (+1.6% YoY).
  • Energy coal: Q3 production 4.0Mt (+12% YoY); sales 4.0Mt (+25% YoY).
  • 9M FY2026 production: copper 1.46Mt (-2.6%); iron ore 196.6Mt (+2.1%); steelmaking coal 13.0Mt (+0.8%); energy coal 12.2Mt (+11%).
  • Guidance maintained; now expects copper towards the upper end / upper half of 1.9–2.0Mt FY range; iron ore guidance unchanged at 258–269Mt.
  • Steelmaking coal expected in lower half of 18–20Mt guidance; energy coal expected in upper half of 14–16Mt guidance.
  • CEO quote (Mike Henry): “BHP has delivered strong performance over the past nine months, including record material mined and concentrator throughput at Escondida and record production at [Western Australia iron ore]. These results reflect the consistency of our operations and the strength of our high margin diversified portfolio in an evolving operating environment...We continue to make steady progress across our copper growth program, consistent with our focus on long-life, high-quality copper supply and disciplined capital allocation.”
  • Reported conclusion of 2026 iron ore sales contract negotiations with China’s China Mineral Resources Group (CMRG), ending the dispute over supply terms that had persisted since September 2025. ()

• Chair Vijay Thakrar, CEO Pamela Bingham, and CFO Simon Dray each bought 25,000 shares at 240p (transaction notified 20 April 2026). ()
  • Resulting shareholdings stated as 0.21% (Thakrar), 0.07% (Bingham) and 0.36% (Dray).

• Announced grand opening of “Citrus Place” in Babson Park (Polk County), Central Florida; homes starting in the high-$200s.
  • Location positioning: southern Polk County, minutes from Highway 17 and <10 minutes to Downtown Lake Wales (access to shopping/dining/employment centres).
  • Product: homes on oversized 75’ and 80’ lots, with many featuring covered back patios.
  • Standard inclusions: CompleteHome™ / CompleteHomePlus™ packages with designer finishes and modern features “at no additional cost.”
  • Floorplan range: 3-bed/2-bath/2-car up to 5-bed/3-bath/3-car, up to 2,414 sq ft.
  • Quote (John Gammon, VP Sales): “Citrus Place is designed to offer something for every buyer, with a wide range of thoughtfully designed floor plans from comfortable three-bedroom, two-bath homes with two-car garages to spacious five-bedroom, three-bath homes with three-car garages and up to 2,414 square feet of living space…”
  • Amenities: planned community park and playground; marketed around nearby outdoor recreation (parks/lakes/nature areas).

• CEO Matthew Bonning-Snook: “The Period has seen further strong progress across our business, building on the significant momentum delivered in the first half of the year. At The Bower, EC1, the resurgence of the tech occupier market that we highlighted at our Half Year Results has translated into a c.20,000 sq ft letting of two floors to an AI platform, three further floors totalling c.32,000 sq ft going under offer, as well as regears with existing occupiers being agreed on a further five floors.

Having acquired, financed and signed the main construction contract at our 240,000 sq ft development at Paddington, W2, we now have four office schemes, totalling over 700,000 sq ft, currently under construction. 100 New Bridge Street, EC4 is on track to achieve practical completion later this month, triggering completion of the forward sale of the building to State Street Corporation and a return of equity to the business. This will be followed by the completion of Brettenham House, WC2 and 10 King William Street, EC4 later in the year, with both best-in-class assets well positioned to take advantage of the strong rental growth being driven by a market with very tight supply.

This pipeline is supplemented by our equity-light 429 bed PBSA scheme at Southwark, SE1, where, in February 2026, the joint venture agreed a forward funding with Places for London. With no further equity required to be injected, this has significantly de-risked the project and should generate a very strong return on investment. Finally, yesterday, our joint venture with Places for London successfully secured a planning consent for a new 55,000 sq ft office development in Farringdon, further bolstering our exciting pipeline.”

  • 100 New Bridge Street, EC4: practical completion expected April 2026; handover to State Street to complete £333m forward sale (Helical share £166.5m); delivered in 24 months and in line with original budget.
  • Brettenham House, WC2 (128,000 sq ft): completion due Q3 2026; two floors reached sectional completion; scaffolding removed; awarded 5★ NABERS Design for Performance rating.
  • 10 King William Street, EC4 (142,000 sq ft): practical completion due December 2026; topped out January 2026; façade installation complete; good letting interest cited amid limited best-in-class City supply.
  • Southwark, SE1 (PBSA 429 beds): forward funding agreed with Places for London (Feb 2026); affordable residential forward sale exchanged with Southwark Borough Council; no further equity required; targeted >3.0x return; enabling works due to start imminently; delivery targeted for 2029/30 academic year.
  • Paddington, W2 (240,000 sq ft): JV site acquisition completed Feb 2026 for £55m (Helical share £28.1m); main construction contract signed with Mace (Mar 2026); enabling works by Keltbray; practical completion targeted Q3 2028.
  • Paddington sustainability: achieved BREEAM Outstanding (97.4% score) at design stage (noted as second-highest UK new-build office score); targeting WELL Platinum, EPC A, NABERS 5.5★.
  • 63 Charterhouse Street, EC1M / Farringdon: 22 April resolution to grant planning permission for c.55,000 sq ft office adjacent to the new London Museum; heads of terms agreed to acquire site upon s106.
  • The Bower, EC1 lettings: exchanged to lease 5th/6th floors (19,592 sq ft fitted) to ; heads of terms agreed for 3rd floor (10,022 sq ft) and 12th floor (9,572 sq ft); expansion + lease extensions agreed with existing tenant at The Warehouse (7th floor 12,398 sq ft) plus regears on three further floors at The Tower.
  • The Bower occupancy: upon completion of agreed terms, occupancy expected to rise to 96.6%; rents “in line with current ERVs” with exchanges anticipated in May.
  • Financing: entered £220m development financing facility with PIMCO Prime Real Estate (4.5-year, pari passu) for Paddington; reimburses 54.5% of equity invested to date and funds 54.5% of remaining costs, with margin step-downs and one-year extension option.

• Group AUMA £547.7bn at 31 Mar 2026 (31 Dec 2025: £556.0bn; -£8.3bn / -1.5%), reflecting divestment of financial planning business, lower markets, and net outflows.
  • Q1 net flows £(2.9)bn (Q1 2025: £(5.2)bn) — improved by £2.3bn (~44%) YoY.
  • interactive investor: AUA £95.3bn (vs £97.5bn); record quarterly net inflows £3.0bn (Q1 2025: £1.6bn; +88% YoY).
  • interactive investor customers 513k (+14% YoY); excluding Jarvis acquisition, customer growth c.9%.
  • SIPP momentum: SIPP customers 116k (+32% YoY; +10% QoQ); record SIPP transfers highlighted.
  • Engagement: DARTs 35k (c. +21% QoQ vs Q4 2025); trading uplift attributed to market volatility and FX repricing to improve competitiveness.
  • interactive investor cash balances £8.7bn (31 Dec 2025: £8.0bn; +~9%), reflecting growth and customer behaviour in volatile markets.
  • Adviser: net outflows £(0.6)bn (flat YoY); gross inflows £1.9bn (from £1.7bn) offset by higher redemptions; AUMA £78.6bn (vs £80.4bn).
  • Adviser leadership: Rich Denning appointed as new Adviser CEO (joining May) to drive return to growth; bringing key service teams back in-house; client engagement hub NPS >+50.
  • Investments: AUM £383.4bn (vs £390.4bn); net outflows £(5.4)bn (Q1 2025: £(6.4)bn), driven by previously announced c.£4bn lower-margin equities withdrawals plus adverse markets, partly offset by fixed income inflows.
  • Insurance Partners flows improved to £0.0bn (Q1 2025: £(2.3)bn), reflecting asset allocation changes and DC workplace pension-related business from Standard Life.
  • Forward look on flows: Q2 expected to include c.£1.2bn real assets advisory mandate win and c.£1bn credit win; GEM equity income flows expected later in year.
  • FY26 targets reiterated: adjusted operating profit ≥£300m and net capital generation c.£300m.
  • Market recovery note: estimated Group AUMA at market close 17 April 2026: c.£573bn, “having recovered strongly”.

• CEO Eduardo Landin: “We have made a solid start to the year, with a strong operational performance at our flagship Inmaculada mine in Peru whilst the turnaround programme at Mara Rosa in Brazil is progressing well, with continued improvements in the plant and filtering operations. Overall, we have delivered another quarter of strong cash generation and we are on track to meet our full-year production and cost guidance. In parallel, we have continued to advance the engineering work at our Monte Do Carmo gold project in Brazil, where we are targeting the third quarter for project approval and the commencement of construction. Finally, the Environmental Impact Study for the Royropata silver project in Peru is progressing according to plan.

With Inmaculada performing well, an improved Mara Rosa, guidance maintained and an exciting portfolio of future growth projects, we see a strong future for Hochschild and look forward to updating the market on our progress”

  • Q1 attributable production (in line with expectations): 55,252oz gold, 1.6Moz silver, 75,599 gold equivalent oz.
  • Guidance reiterated: 300,000–328,000 gold equivalent oz in 2026; AISC $2,157–$2,320/Geoz.
  • Cash: total cash, cash equivalents & short-term investments ~$412m at 31 Mar 2026 (vs $317.0m at 31 Dec 2025; +$95m / ~+30%).
  • Balance sheet swing: net cash ~ $95m at 31 Mar 2026 (vs net debt $22.7m at 31 Dec 2025).
  • Group attributable production vs prior: gold equivalent 75.60koz (Q1 2025: 81.65koz); silver equivalent 5,821koz (Q1 2025: 6,287koz).
  • Inmaculada (Peru): Q1 production 32,561oz gold and 1.21Moz silver (48,281 Geoz); moderately higher tonnage offset by lower grades (cut-off grade effect from higher prices); reverse osmosis plant completed and started up; tailings lift advanced.
  • San Jose (Argentina, 51%): Q1 production 0.699Moz silver and 17.92koz gold (26,991 Geoz); tonnage up vs Q1 2025; silver grades slightly lower; “on track” for annual target.
  • Mara Rosa (Brazil): turnaround continued (greater plant stability, maintenance reliability, higher run rates); peak quarter throughput noted (crushing 8.4kt; milling 6.9kt; filter plant 7.0kt); contractor replacement mobilisation 75% by end-March; new tailings thickener commissioning expected end-April with operations by end-May 2026; Q1 production 13,552 Geoz (below expectations due to equipment availability, weaker waste development, rainy season).
  • Prices: average realisable prices $4,471/oz gold and $89.8/oz silver (Q1 2025: $2,708/oz and $33.2/oz).
  • Monte Do Carmo (Brazil): engineering/permitting/procurement work progressed; board approval targeted in Q3 2026 (and start of construction thereafter).
  • Royropata (Peru): Environmental Impact Study progressing “according to plan”.
  • Exploration: 2026 brownfield drilling commenced at all sites; first results due from Q2 2026.
  • ESG: LTIFR 0.55 (FY 2025: 0.97); fresh water used 0.17 m³/t (FY 2025: 0.26); women in workforce 11.2% (FY 2025: 10.6%); ECO score 5.90/6 (FY 2025: 5.61).
  • Cash location/disclosure: cash includes $230m held in Argentina (with $84m in instruments to mitigate inflation/devaluation risk) and $35m held in Tiernan Gold for Volcan development support.

• Group sales £431m, +1% constant currency (reported -2% vs £442m), in line with expectations against a strong Q1 2025 comparator (+9% constant currency).
  • Mix: growth in Beauty Actives and Fragrances & Flavours (F&F) offset lower sales in Crop Protection (no longer benefiting from the 2025 customer inventory rebuild).
  • No change to expectations for FY 2026; transformation programme on track and framed as a material driver of margin improvement “in our control”.
  • Divisional sales (reported): Consumer Care £255m (0% YoY; +4% constant currency); Life Sciences £126m (-6% YoY; -3% constant currency); Industrial Specialties £50m (-5% YoY; -2% constant currency).
  • Consumer Care detail (constant currency): Beauty Actives +12%, F&F +10%, Home Care +6%, Beauty Care -4% (prior period benefited from some pre-tariff buying in North America and Asia).
  • Life Sciences detail (constant currency): Crop Protection -8% (strong restocking comparator); Seed Enhancement +2% (services-led model); Pharma -2% (Pharma Solutions down vs strong comparator; Pharma Ingredients up as resources reallocated to accelerate growth).
  • Regional performance (constant currency): Latin America +11% (strongest; ag momentum + robust Consumer Care); EMEA +2%; Asia +2%; North America -7% (normalisation in Crop Protection + weather impacts + consumer pressure).
  • Middle East conflict: stated no material effect in Q1; Middle East was ~5% of 2025 Group sales (mostly F&F); Croda increasing prices to fully recover input cost inflation.
  • FX sensitivity: constant currency framework uses 2025 average rates (US$1.32, €1.17); USD+EUR ~65% of translation exposure; estimated impact on adjusted operating profit £1m per 1¢ move in USD and £1m per 1¢ move in EUR (per annum).
  • If FX from Apr–Dec 2026 matched Mar 2026 closing rates: expected ~£4m negative impact on reported FY26 operating profit (including hyperinflation accounting).

• Managing Director Tom Hickey: “We are pleased to have returned to a strong safety performance in Q1 2026, with no Lost Time Injuries incurred. Shipments were in line with the run rate of our annual guidance, and we have already drawn down almost 100,000 tonnes of inventory.

While our product markets remained soft in Q1, global supply curtailments and unplanned production disruptions are starting to reduce the oversupply of ilmenite, supporting a tighter market outlook. Encouragingly, the zircon market continues to show signs of recovery, with higher agreed prices in Q2, and we shipped more of our new product, ZrTi, in Q1 than we did in all of 2025. Although current geopolitical uncertainty has increased freight rates and affected access to some customers’ port facilities, altering our shipping schedule in Q1, the overall impact of the war has been limited to date.

There continues to be constructive and regular engagement with the Government of Mozambique regarding the renewal of Moma’s Implementation Agreement and we are focused on securing a near-term resolution.”

  • Safety: 0 LTIs in Q1; rolling 12-month LTIFR 0.07 per 200,000 hours (31 Mar 2025: 0.06).
  • Guidance: on track to achieve 2026 guidance on all metrics; finished product stockpiles drawn down 99,900t in Q1 (value-over-volume).
  • Production headwinds: HMC 217,200t (-30% YoY) driven by ongoing WCP A debottlenecking; excavated ore 8.005Mt (-14% YoY) and grade 3.26% (-23% YoY) as WCP A reaches end of Namalope mine path.
  • Ilmenite: 125,900t (-38% YoY), impacted by lower HMC and lower recoveries (spillage/inconsistent feed + retreatment campaigns); retained material to be retreated in Q2.
  • Zircon & rutile: primary zircon 8,900t (-36% YoY); rutile 1,000t (-60% YoY) (same recovery issues; partly offset by intermediate stockpile processing for zircon).
  • Concentrates: 45,200t (+397% YoY) supported by new by-product ZrTi production 35,200t.
  • Shipments: 277,900t (-10% YoY) but in line with run-rate for >1.1Mt FY guidance; includes 46,400t ZrTi; shipment mix: 207,300t ilmenite, 10,900t primary zircon, 100t rutile, 59,500t concentrates.
  • Inventory: closing HMC stock 41,000t (vs 29,200t at FY25); finished product stock 244,100t (vs 344,000t at FY25), reflecting the 99,900t drawdown.
  • WCP A upgrade: major construction/installation complete; capex rate decreased substantially in Q1; low-cost fixes implemented (dredge winch brake software/cooling; tailings pump gearbox upgrades; stable densification paddock/TSF operations; long-term rectifications after walkway turning over).
  • Early Q2 ops: improved dredge feed delivery achieved on one dredge and to be replicated; plant flowrate debottlenecking to complete “over the coming weeks”, with expectation that nameplate capacity will be substantially achieved thereafter.
  • Market: titanium feedstocks remained soft; Kenmare’s average ilmenite price declined significantly vs H2 2025 (as expected); supply curtailments/disruptions tightening outlook; strong order book for Q2.
  • Zircon market: demand exceeds Kenmare supply; higher agreed prices for Q2 2026.
  • Geopolitics/logistics: higher freight rates and port-access constraints altered shipping schedule in Q1; “overall impact of the war has been limited to date”.
  • Credit recovery: “significant progress” recovering value from Q3 2025 shipments to a distressed customer; Spanish ops new owner settled outstanding invoice in full; discussions ongoing with proposed buyer of Malaysian ops to recover value.
  • ZrTi accounting: ZrTi recognised at estimated net realisable value (c. ~$130/t in 2025 after handling/export taxes), reducing ilmenite production cost.
  • Implementation Agreement (IA): meeting with Government 19 Feb 2026 targeted renewal terms by 20 Mar 2026 (not achieved); engagement continues; Kenmare reserves right to safeguard entitlements “up to and including arbitration” if needed; mining ops are under separate framework not impacted by IA process.

time_period: Update as at 22 April 2026 (includes liquidity position to 17 April 2026) and summary unaudited results for FY 2025 (year ended 31 December 2025)
body:
• Board says an equity capital raise is “currently the only viable solution” in the required timeframe; intended to raise minimum US$100m via a conditional placing to existing/new institutional investors to support working capital and operate at reduced level for the next ~18 months (the “Intended Fundraise”).
  • Intended Fundraise launch subject to Board approval and market conditions; completion conditional on shareholder Resolutions at a General Meeting convened ASAP after launch; company does not intend to publish a prospectus.
  • Group reiterates it has sufficient net accessible cash until ~end-August 2026, assuming no material adverse changes (iron ore price/opex incl. energy, operating conditions incl. energy supply, no restrictive measures by FPM insolvency manager, no negative legal outcomes); assumes receipt of proceeds from sale of vessel “Iron Destiny”.
  • Agreed sale of owned transhipping vessel “Iron Destiny” for US$7.7m net cash; completion expected later April 2026.
  • Critical timetable: if fundraise proceeds, it is expected to be launched and completed on/before 30 April 2026 to enable publication of audited FY2025 results.
  • If the bookbuild is not completed by 30 April 2026, shares expected to be suspended from 07:30 (UK) on 1 May 2026 until audit completed and FY2025 Annual Report published; no certainty on timing of any lifting of suspension, “if at all”.
  • Company states there is no certainty the Intended Fundraise (or any alternative funding) can be executed in the timeframe, or at all; non-completion could lead to insolvency filings and shareholders potentially losing all or a substantial portion of their investment.
  • Engagement ongoing with largest shareholder Fevamotinico S.a.r.l. (holds 49.32% of shares in issue ex treasury) on support for the Resolutions and potential participation; discussions focus on dilution and implications of association with Kostiantyn Zhevago (beneficiary of The Minco Trust which wholly owns Fevamotinico).
  • Liquidity deterioration highlighted: net cash ~US$20m (net accessible cash ~US$17m, excluding funds held at MBaer Merchant Bank after its banking licence revocation in Feb 2026) as at 17 April 2026 vs US$25m (31 Mar 2026), US$30m (27 Feb 2026), US$47m (31 Dec 2025), US$50m (30 Jun 2025), US$101m (31 Dec 2024).
  • Key driver of liquidity stress: ongoing suspension of VAT refunds since March 2025; net VAT receivable balance US$90.3m at 31 March 2026; US$78.9m claimed for refund (Jan 2025–Mar 2026), with US$69.4m refused (Jan–Dec 2025) cited as due to association with Mr Zhevago and his personal sanctions in Ukraine.
  • Operations: production resumed in 2026 but remains ~25% of sustained pre-war capacity; currently running 1 of 4 pellet lines and selling primarily to European customers.
  • Q1 2026 production: 593kt total (pellets 525kt, Fe 67% concentrate 68kt).
  • Notes constraints from war impacts (energy grid attacks, costly electricity imports, logistics, conscription) and legal/regulatory actions in Ukraine; production was temporarily suspended 20 Jan 2026 due to electricity disruption, with limited resumption late Feb 2026.
  • States that aside from lease liabilities (mostly long-term over ~10 years), the Group has no outstanding interest-bearing loans/borrowings.
  • Longer-term outlook framed as multi-phase recovery dependent on war evolution, restoration/collection of VAT refunds, legal outcomes, and additional capital beyond this raise; target max capacity ~12mtpa, with an expected start of recovery to pre-war levels in 2028; highlights Belanovo greenfield optionality (1.7bn tonnes JORC resource) suitable for DR pellets.
  • Use of proceeds: primarily working capital (power, labour, consumables, logistics, maintenance) and preserving financial flexibility amid uncertainty (power, logistics, legal proceedings, VAT refund suspension).
  • Funding alternatives reviewed (debt, supplier/customer terms, trade finance, care & maintenance, asset disposals, strategic investment) but Board concludes none can raise sufficient cash in time; cites constraints from Ukrainian sanctions/KYC concerns, asset freeze orders, FPM bankruptcy process limiting disposals/security, and inability to assure stable export income to service debt.
  • Legal / regulatory milestones flagged: Supreme Court of Ukraine hearing on contested sureties claim scheduled 1 May 2026; FPM appeal hearing against opening of bankruptcy proceedings scheduled 30 April 2026.
  • FY2025 unaudited (going concern basis contingent on Intended Fundraise) summary: sales volume 6.6mt (2024: 6.8mt, -4%); Fe 65% fines price US$115/t (2024: US$123/t, -7%); revenue US$787m (2024: US$933m, -16%).
  • FY2025 production 6.1mt (2024: 6.8mt, -9%); C1 cash costs US$76.3/t (2024: US$77.5/t, -2%); write-offs/impairments US$161m (2024: US$72m, +124%).
  • FY2025 profitability: operating result loss US$197m (2024: profit US$18m; swing -US$215m); loss for period US$211m (2024: loss US$50m; loss widened by US$161m / +322%); diluted EPS -35.8c (2024: -8.5c).

• Worldwide procedures (da Vinci + Ion) grew ~17% YoY; da Vinci +16%, Ion +39%.
  • System placements: 431 da Vinci (vs 367; +17.4%), including 232 da Vinci 5 (vs 147; +57.8%); 52 Ion systems (vs 49; +6.1%).
  • Installed base (as of 31 March 2026): 11,395 da Vinci systems (vs 10,189; +11.8%); 1,041 Ion systems (vs 853; +22.0%).
  • Revenue $2.77bn (vs $2.25bn; +23.1% YoY), driven by procedure growth, higher system placements, and installed base expansion.
  • Instruments & accessories revenue $1.69bn (vs $1.37bn; +23% YoY), tied to da Vinci procedure growth (~16%) and Ion procedure growth (~39%), plus buying patterns.
  • Systems revenue $651m (vs $523m; +24.5% YoY); placement mix included 243 da Vinci systems under operating leases (vs 198), of which 118 were usage-based operating leases (vs 107).
  • Profitability: GAAP income from operations $855m (vs $578m; +47.9% YoY); non-GAAP income from operations $1.08bn (vs $0.77bn).
  • Earnings: GAAP net income $822m / $2.28 per diluted share (vs $698m / $1.92; net income +17.8% YoY); non-GAAP net income $901m / $2.50 per diluted share (vs $662m / $1.81; net income +36.1% YoY).
  • Share repurchases: bought back 2.3m shares for $1.1bn in Q1.
  • Liquidity: ended Q1 with $7.98bn cash/cash equivalents/investments (down $1.05bn QoQ), primarily due to buybacks and an acquisition (not detailed), partly offset by operating cash flow.
  • Dave Rosa (CEO) said: “We are pleased with company performance this quarter, which was marked by expanded adoption of our da Vinci, Ion, and digital platforms,” and “We remain deeply committed to supporting customers globally in their pursuit of the quintuple aim: achieving better patient outcomes, improving patient and care team experiences, lowering the total cost to treat, and expanding access to high quality, minimally invasive care.”
  • FY 2026 outlook: worldwide da Vinci procedure growth ~13.5%–15.5%; non-GAAP gross margin 67.5%–68.5% (includes estimated tariff impact ~1.0% of revenue); non-GAAP operating expense growth 11%–14%.

• Board approved a new stock repurchase authorisation of up to $25bn in common stock through 30 April 2030.
  • Repurchase rationale stated: return value to shareholders, minimise dilution from stock issuance, and reduce share count over time.
  • Execution flexibility: may repurchase in the open market and via structured repurchase agreements with third parties.
  • Dan Durn (EVP & CFO) said: "Our new $25 billion share repurchase authorization is a direct expression of confidence in our robust cash flow and the long-term value we are delivering to investors. Returning meaningful capital to stockholders while continuing to invest aggressively in innovation speaks to the durability of Adobe's business model and strategy to leverage AI to amplify creativity, scale reach and deliver impactful experiences,”

• Diluted EPS $0.59 (vs $0.48; +22.9% YoY); adjusted EPS $0.60 (vs $0.47; +27.7% YoY).
  • Net revenues $1.67bn (vs $1.43bn; +16.8% YoY); adjusted net revenues $1.68bn (vs $1.40bn; +20.0% YoY).
  • Income before tax $1.29bn (vs $1.06bn; +21.7% YoY); adjusted $1.30bn (vs $1.02bn).
  • Commission revenue +19% to $613m on higher trading volumes; customer volumes: stocks +25%, futures +20%, options +16%.
  • Net interest income +17% to $904m, driven by higher average margin loans and customer credit balances.
  • Execution/clearing/distribution fees -12% to $106m, citing (i) SEC Section 31 transaction fee rate reduced to zero (14 May 2025) and (ii) greater capture of liquidity rebates amid higher stock/options volumes.
  • Pretax margin 77% (reported and adjusted) vs 74% reported / 73% adjusted in the prior-year quarter.
  • Balance sheet: total equity $21.3bn.
  • Dividend: quarterly cash dividend raised $0.08 → $0.0875 (+9.4%); payable 12 June 2026 to holders of record 1 June 2026.
  • Activity / client metrics: customer accounts +31% to 4.75m; customer equity +38% to $789.4bn; total DARTs +24% to 4.37m; customer credits +35% to $168.8bn; margin loans +35% to $86.0bn.
  • Currency diversification strategy: reduced comprehensive earnings by $53m as USD value of the GLOBALs basket decreased ~0.30%; reported as Other Income +$26m and Other Comprehensive Income -$79m.

• Building business appointed to Sovereign Network Group’s £750 million affordable homes framework.
  • Framework intended to support Sovereign’s target of delivering >2,000 homes per year across southern England.
  • Award scope/bands: appointed to mid and high-value bands across London & East and South regions; high-value band for West region.
  • Bill Hocking (CEO) said: "We are delighted to have been selected by Sovereign for this major new framework, and look forward to working with them across their regions of operation to deliver much-needed high-quality homes for their customers. This framework builds on the momentum generated by our recent appointments to the Hyde Group framework, and first standalone contract for Clarion Housing Group at the City Place scheme in Chester."

time_period: Board appointment (effective close of 19 May 2026 AGM)
body:
• Appointed John-Paul Savant (departing CEO of Auction Technology Group plc) as independent non-executive director.
  • Savant to join Trustpilot board at the close of its AGM on 19 May 2026.
  • Auction Technology Group: Savant to stand down after a “short orderly handover period”; he has served as CEO for 10 years.

• Appointed Will Wilkins as new Chief Financial Officer, joining from Mpac Group plc (CFO since 2018; 14 years at Mpac).
  • Wilkins to start at Ibstock on 1 August 2026.
  • Simon Bedford (Financial Controller) has been interim CFO since Chris McLeish departed in October (year not stated).
  • Mpac: Duncan Tyler (Corporate Development Director) to act as interim CFO.

time_period: Board / governance update (effective 12 May 2026; chair transition after 22 July 2026 AGM)
body:
• Adam Crozier (BT Group Chair) to join Experian plc’s Board as Independent Non-Executive Director and Chair designate from 12 May 2026.
  • Adam Crozier to become Chair of Experian following conclusion of Experian’s AGM on 22 July 2026.
  • Will join Experian’s Remuneration and Nomination and Corporate Governance Committees; to chair the Nomination and Corporate Governance Committee following the AGM.

• Q1 2026 total revenue £393.1m, +4.6% YoY reported; +7.0% YoY continuing constant currency (CCY) — best Q1 growth since 2021.
  • Segment revenue (Q1 2026): THG Beauty £233.3m (+2.4% reported, +5.8% continuing CCY); THG Nutrition £159.8m (+8.1% reported, +8.8% continuing CCY).
  • Beauty acceleration: +5.8% CCY in Q1 vs +5.4% in H2 2025.
  • Middle East disruption impacted growth by c.30bps; management notes underlying growth accelerated on Q4 exit trends.
  • Disposals/discontinued activities reduced Group Q1 revenue growth by 150bps; currency headwinds “most pronounced” in the US.
  • FY 2026 guidance reiterated; confidence supported by market share gains and “pricing and product optimisation strategies”.
  • Cash flow: strongest Q1 cash flow performance in three years, supporting full-year free cash flow guidance of £25m–£50m.
  • Middle East exposure: affected regions represent <1.5% of FY 2025 Group revenue, weighted towards Beauty.
  • Beauty operational: UK orders +7% with growth in LTM active customer base; US retail delivered revenue growth/market share expansion driven by +10% new customers.
  • Dermstore partnered with Flex to enable customers to use HSA/FSA at checkout (positioned to support affordability, convenience, and AOV).
  • Category/brand momentum: K-Beauty revenue more than doubled YoY; six new brands launched YTD; SkinCeuticals and MAC launches on Cult Beauty “exceeding expectations”.
  • Retail footprint: new Lookfantastic Bristol store outperforming initial flagship, with a “localised halo effect” on awareness and acquisition.
  • Beauty manufacturing/owned brands: new retail/spa listings for Ameliorate and ESPA; manufacturing growth driven by strategic new business wins + existing customer growth.
  • Nutrition: growth +8.8%; +12.1% ex-Asia, with Asia declining due to a deliberate pivot to licensing with local manufacturing/distribution partners (management expects growth to resume once transition annualised).
  • Nutrition category mix/pricing: expansion into higher-margin categories (activewear, creatine, hydration, collagen) plus pricing/product optimisation to offset “record” whey costs; reformulation and strategic pricing in core powder range; launch of MARS® Impact Whey Protein.
  • Activewear penetration: c.15% of active customers purchased activewear (+230bps YoY); orders including activewear had c.31% higher AOV; activewear annualised run-rate sales “fast approaching” £100m.
  • Licensed-out retail: >200% growth in units sold into retail YoY; Greencore food-on-the-go launch and Müller range expansion; Jimmy’s Iced Coffee partnership at c.20% share of protein coffee sub-category.
  • Retail expansion initiatives: Tesco branded bays nationwide; entry into Canada/Latin America with new distribution partners + marketplace presence; US launch in 1,200 Kroger stores in June; expanded listings with GNC (1,500 stores) and Vitamin Shoppe (Myprotein Gummies first-to-market).
  • CEO Matthew Moulding (verbatim): “While the geopolitical backdrop remains uncertain, we enter Q2 with confidence after a better-than-expected Q1, giving us a stronger base against any unforeseen risks later in the year.”

time_period: Chair succession timeline (12 May 2026 to 22 July 2026 AGM)
body:
• Adam Crozier to join the Board as independent Non-Executive Director and Chair Designate from 12 May 2026.
  • Crozier to succeed Mike Rogers as Chair after the AGM on 22 July 2026; Rogers retiring as Director and Chair at the conclusion of that AGM (served 9 years on Board; 7 years as Chair).
  • Crozier committee roles: will serve on Remuneration and Nomination & Corporate Governance committees; to chair Nomination & Corporate Governance committee following the AGM.
  • Crozier profile: current Chair of BT Group plc (also chairs Nomination Committee; responsibility for security oversight) and Chair of Kantar Group; former FTSE 100 CEO; previously Chair of Whitbread and ASOS; former NED of Sony Group Corporation.
  • Alison Brittain (Senior Independent Director) (verbatim): “Following a comprehensive and rigorous search process, the Board is delighted to appoint Adam as Chair… Adam's strategic judgement, extensive Chair experience and measured approach position him well…”
  • Brian Cassin (CEO) (verbatim): “We are very pleased to welcome Adam to the Board… his insight will be valuable as we continue to execute our strategy and deliver long-term growth.”
  • Adam Crozier (verbatim): “I am delighted to be joining Experian at an important stage in its development… well positioned to capitalise on the opportunities ahead.”
  • Company confirmation: aside from the announcement, no further disclosures required under UK Listing Rule 6.4.8R (1)–(6) regarding Crozier’s appointment.

time_period: Board appointment (effective close of AGM on 19 May 2026)
body:
• Appointment of John-Paul Savant as independent Non-Executive Director, effective close of the AGM on 19 May 2026.
  • Current role: CEO of Auction Technology Group plc (ATG) since 2016.
  • Prior experience: ~10 years at eBay/PayPal, latterly PayPal VP of Product, Experience, and Consumer Engagement (EMEA); then CEO of Think Finance UK.
  • Background: began career at J.P. Morgan (New York); Georgetown University graduate; MBA (University of Chicago).
  • Chair Zillah Byng-Thorne (verbatim): “We are delighted to welcome John-Paul to the Trustpilot Board. He brings deep expertise in scaling digital marketplace businesses and a strong track record of driving value through technology-led transformation…”
  • John-Paul Savant (verbatim): “In a world where AI-generated content is increasingly prevalent, genuine human reviews and transparent trust signals matter more than ever… I am delighted to be joining the Board at such an exciting moment in Trustpilot's journey…”

• Portfolio company First Light Fusion Ltd announced a £25m successful first close of its latest funding round.
  • Round led by East X Ventures via its fusion fund Starmaker One, with a significant strategic investment from the UK Atomic Energy Authority (UKAEA).
  • Also supported by existing shareholders including IP Group and its Hostplus managed fund.
  • Post-completion holdings: IP Group has an undiluted 28.3% beneficial holding in First Light, plus 17.3% held via its managed funds.
  • Proceeds to accelerate commercial development of FLARE Fusion Energy concept — described as a “novel, reactor-compatible approach to Inertial Fusion Energy” aimed at a simpler, lower-cost pathway to fusion.
  • Greg Smith (IP Group CEO) (verbatim): “First Light Fusion continues to make strong progress as it advances a highly differentiated and commercially focused approach to inertial fusion energy. The participation of specialist fusion investors alongside the UK Atomic Energy Authority is a strong endorsement of both the company's technology and its strategic relevance to the UK's long-term energy ambitions… We continue to support the company as it accelerates development of its FLARE concept towards scalable, reactor-compatible fusion.”

• Brazilian Federal Regional Court of the 6th Region monitoring report indicates continued progress under the Brazilian Agreement for reparations following the 2015 Samarco tailings dam collapse.
  • Report tracks actions by Samarco Mineracao SA and public authorities; Samarco is a JV between BHP Brasil and Vale SA.
  • 323,000+ approvals issued during May–Oct 2025, directing BRL 14.1bn (~US$2.85bn) in compensation payments and legal fees.
  • Progress across programmes: individual compensation schemes, income-support initiatives for affected workers, and payments to agricultural producers and traditional communities.
  • Resettlement: collective works in Bento Rodrigues and Paracatu de Baixo completed; additional support for families whose individual projects had not begun when the agreement was approved.
  • Governance actions: further contributions to the Rio Doce fund; launch of a unified public portal to centralise remediation information.
  • Long-term investments: agreement provides for health and basic sanitation investments; new funding directed to hospital and reference-centre projects.
  • BHP statement (verbatim): “The company actively participates in the process and supports Samarco in fulfilling the obligations established by the Brazilian Agreement, prioritizing transparency and dialogue with communities and authorities. This stance aligns with the company's vision of contributing to responsible mining and solutions that promote sustainable development,” BHP said.

• Pilbara iron ore output 67.8Mt (+13% YoY); Pilbara sales 61.2Mt (+0.5% YoY).
  • Global iron ore production 82.8Mt (+12% YoY); global iron ore sales 75.7Mt (+2.1% YoY).
  • Bauxite production 13.3Mt (-11% YoY).
  • Alumina output 2.0Mt (+6.1% YoY); aluminium production 840kt (+0.7% YoY).
  • Consolidated copper production 228.6kt (+8.9% YoY).
  • CEO Simon Trott: “Operating excellence drove 9% YoY copper equivalent production growth… as the Oyu Tolgoi copper mine continues to ramp up as planned…”
  • Trott: “The unmatchable mix and scale of our portfolio has ensured growth and supply chain resilience… as we continue to closely monitor the evolving situation in the Middle East.”
  • Exploration & evaluation spend $180m (vs $141m a year earlier; ~+28%), driven by Resolution Copper (Arizona) and higher copper product group activity; copper “just over half” of total spend.
  • Productivity: initiatives delivering $650m in annualised productivity gains; further throughput/cost-efficiency measures planned.
  • Guidance reaffirmed: Pilbara iron sales 323–338Mt; copper 800–870kt; bauxite 58–61Mt; alumina 7.6–7.8Mt; aluminium 3.25–3.45Mt.

• Record steel shipments of 3.6m tons.
  • Net sales $5.2bn; operating income $538m; net income $403m ($2.78/diluted share).
  • Net income up ~52% QoQ (vs $266m in Q4 2025) and ~86% YoY (vs $217m in Q1 2025).
  • Adjusted EBITDA $700m; operating cash flow $148m after $120m annual retirement profit-sharing distribution.
  • Cash dividend increased 6% (Q1 2026).
  • CEO Mark D. Millett: “The teams executed well… with operating income increasing $228 million, or 73 percent… driven by record steel shipments combined with higher steel prices.”
  • CEO: “Underlying steel demand strengthened… customer orders rebounded and backlogs increased… value-added flat rolled steel margins expanded… We are seeing an improved steel market environment…”
  • Steel ops: operating income $557m, +73% vs Q4; average external selling price $1,193/ton (+$86 sequentially); ferrous scrap cost $396/ton melted (+$22).
  • Metals recycling: operating income $47m, +155% vs Q4; shipments “marginally lower” due to winter weather impacting scrap flows.
  • Steel fabrication: operating income $90m (steady QoQ); backlog >38% higher YoY, extending “through the third quarter and into October 2026.”
  • Aluminum ops: operating loss $65m ($17m worse QoQ); finished shipments 22,500 mt vs 14,600 mt in Q4 (~+54%). Startup issues led to a temporary pause and inventory write-offs; company expects shipments and earnings to increase sharply in Q2 2026.
  • Capital allocation/liquidity: capex $138m; dividends paid $72m; share repurchases $115m; liquidity $2.0bn at 31 March 2026.
  • Outlook quote: “We remain constructive that market conditions are in place for domestic steel and aluminum consumption to be strong through 2026…”
  • Aluminum ramp milestones: third cold mill expected to commission Q3 2026; second CASH line expected to commission Q3 2026; first CASH line now operational and producing qualification material for automotive customers.

time_period: Director dealing (trade date: Monday; exact date not provided)
body:
• Chair Rob Woodward bought 18,319 shares at 353p (total consideration £64,666).
  • Post-transaction, Woodward’s total holding is 23,407 shares (including holdings by persons closely associated).

time_period: Director dealing (trade date: “Friday last week”; exact date not provided)
body:
• Chair Keith Spencer sold 1.0m shares at an average 157.38p (gross proceeds ~£1.6m).
  • Post-transaction, Spencer retains a direct holding of 2.0m shares.

• Will Wilkins appointed Group Chief Financial Officer and Executive Director, expected to join on 1 August 2026.
  • Will joins from Mpac Group plc, where he has been CFO since 2018; previously held senior finance roles at BSH Home Appliances and began his career at Grant Thornton.
  • No other external appointments disclosed for Will Wilkins.
  • Appointment followed a formal CFO search led by the Nomination Committee, supported by external advisers.
  • Simon Bedford to remain Interim CFO until Wilkins starts (Bedford has been interim since October 2025).
  • CEO Joe Hudson quote: "I am delighted to welcome Will to Ibstock to support the continued delivery of our strategy… [He] will be a great complement to our Board and Executive team." Also: "I would also like to thank Simon for his important contribution as Interim CFO since his appointment in October 2025."
  • Will Wilkins quote: "My focus will be on maintaining strong financial discipline and ensuring the Group is well positioned for long-term growth and value creation."

time_period: Strategic update (demerger process targeted before end of calendar 2027)
body:
• Board decision: proceed with demerger of Primark (Retail) from Food business (“FoodCo”); ABF shareholders to receive shares in both listed entities.
  • Largest shareholder: review conducted with Wittington Investments; Wittington supports demerger and intends to maintain majority ownership of both Primark and FoodCo.
  • Separation economics: expected ongoing dis-synergies <£45m; one-off separation/transaction costs ~£75m.
  • Listing intention: both Primark and FoodCo to list on LSE Equity Shares (Commercial Companies) category; expected both to be FTSE 100 constituents (scale-dependent).
  • Post-demerger naming: FoodCo will retain the Associated British Foods plc name.
  • Target timeline/process: intention for demerger to be effective before end-2027; subject to approvals and tax clearances; expected to be effected via dividend demerger; investor sessions planned for both entities ahead of completion.
  • Governance/management intentions: Chair Michael McLintock asked to remain Chair until completion; intended CEOs: George Weston (FoodCo) and Eoin Tonge (Primark).
  • Regulatory: expected to be a Significant Transaction under UK Listing Rules; additional disclosures to follow once details finalised.
  • Chair Michael McLintock: “...concluded that a demerger of Primark is the best way to maximise long-term returns for shareholders…”
  • CEO George Weston: “...For our Food business… the only FTSE100 pure play food producer. For Primark… appropriate governance to maximise… Primark’s powerful brand…”

• Transact net inflows £1.3bn (+8% YoY) (Q2 FY25: £1.2bn), equal to 6.9% of opening FUD annualised.
  • Gross inflows £3.1bn (+15% YoY) (Q2 FY25: £2.7bn); outflows £1.818bn (Q2 FY25: £1.483bn) → net flows £1.327bn.
  • Platform scale: average daily FUD £78.9bn (+18% YoY); quarter-end FUD £77.8bn (+18% YoY) despite volatility; Q2 market/other movements -£0.78bn.
  • Clients: platform clients 254,667 vs 241,197 (+13,470; +5.6% YoY).
  • H1 FY26: net flows £2.362bn (H1 FY25: £2.129bn; +10.9%); revenue guidance: H1 total Group revenue expected up 11% to ~£85.8m (H1 FY25: £77.2m).
  • April-to-date: average FUD increased with equity market recovery and continued net inflows; monitoring market impact from Middle East conflict; notes diversified client assets dampen index-volatility impact on FUD/revenue.
  • Cost guidance reiterated (from Dec 2025): underlying admin expense growth expected to slow to ~3% in each of FY26 and FY27; FY26 slowing weighted to H2.
  • CEO Alex Scott: “...net inflows… over £1.3bn, and a new record for quarterly gross inflows of over £3.1bn… AI can augment adviser capabilities… we expect to deliver strong, sustainable earnings growth.”

time_period: Trading update since AGM statement (25 March 2026); FY ending October 2026
body:
• Macro backdrop: conflict-driven uncertainty cited as increasing risk of “more prolonged higher interest rate environment”, renewed cost pressures and weaker consumer confidence.
  • Sales: open market reservations “in line” with improved levels since mid-January; strength in Midlands/South-West/Eastern; continued softness in South; no material increase in discounting/incentives/cancellations, but new enquiries and visitor levels down.
  • Volume guidance cut to 1,400–1,500 units (from 1,550–1,700), implying c. 11% cut at the midpoint (1,625 → 1,450); FY order book 1,106 units.
  • Land sales: sentiment “markedly” softened; land sale revenue now ~£40m (from £75m–£100m); expects no material profit on disposals for remainder of FY under revised assumptions.
  • Cash/working capital actions: prioritising cash and balance sheet; faster reduction of finished plots (notably completed apartments); tighter WIP controls.
  • Build costs: assumption of higher build costs in remainder of FY due to higher energy costs.
  • Earnings / leverage: now expects FY EBIT ~£5m–£15m; interest costs ~£15m; year-end net debt £100m–£120m.
  • Banking: seeking temporary covenant relaxation; lender discussions commenced; further update to follow.
  • Fire remediation: no material change to provision; on track to start 80% of affected sites by end-July; FY cash spend now £75m–£80m (slightly lower than planned) and pursuing recovery prospects.
  • CEO Martyn Clark: “...the right and prudent course of action is to adapt quickly… and focus on prioritising cash generation and optimising our balance sheet position.”

• FY26 like-for-like net rental growth 6% (ahead of guidance), including 12% in campuses.
  • Expect FY26 Underlying EPS 28.9p (FY25: 28.5p) and Underlying Profit £294m (+5.4% YoY) (FY25: £279m) — EPS “ahead of guidance.”
  • Upgrading FY27 Underlying EPS guidance to at least 30.5p (from 30.2p) following Life Science REIT acquisition (completed 20 April 2026) and cited ~1% FY27 EPS accretion.
  • CEO Simon Carter: “This is expected to deliver Underlying EPS of 28.9p… and… drive an 8.1% total accounting return… we are confident in our earnings growth outlook for FY27 and beyond.”
  • FY26 portfolio: values +2.3% (campuses +2.0%, retail parks +3.3%); ERV growth +4.9% (campuses +6.5%, retail parks +4.4%); NEY down 4bps to 6.0%; EPRA NTA 590p (FY25: 567p).
  • Balance sheet: LTV 39.2% (FY25: 38.1%); net debt / EBITDA ~7.7x (FY25: 8.0x).
  • Leasing: 3.8m sq ft signed, 7.2% ahead of ERV and 7.6% ahead of previous passing rent; 1.1m sq ft under offer, 12.9% ahead of ERV.
  • Campuses: 1.7m sq ft leasing, 20.0% ahead of previous passing rent; occupancy 95% (ex. last-12-month completions); Q4 deals 834k sq ft (~50% of FY by area). Record campus rents referenced at Broadgate (1 Appold Street).
  • AI/innovation demand highlighted: signed Anthropic for 158k sq ft at One Triton Square; One Triton Square now 78% let and 16% under offer (including all lab space).
  • Retail parks: portfolio 99% occupancy; leasing 3.4% ahead of passing rents for FY (H2: 6.3%); like-for-like net rental growth in retail & London urban logistics 2%.
  • Outlook reiterated: 3–6% p.a. EPS growth medium term; ERV growth guidance 3–5% p.a. across portfolio.

• CEO Simon Trott: “The tragic loss of two colleagues this year, at Simandou and Kennecott…”
  • Copper equivalent production +9% YoY across portfolio; copper production (consolidated) 229kt (+9% YoY); 2026 copper guidance unchanged (800–870kt).
  • Oyu Tolgoi ramp-up continues “as planned”; Oyu Tolgoi metal in concentrates +56% YoY to 102kt; ramp-up remains on track for ~500ktpa (100% basis, recoverable metal) from 2028 to 2036.
  • Kennecott refined copper 34kt (-20% YoY) (unplanned smelter maintenance → low anode inventory; geotechnical constraints); staged restart of Kennecott underground project commenced 16 April after March fatality.
  • Pilbara: production 78.8Mt (+13% YoY) (second-highest Q1 since 2018); Pilbara sales 72.4Mt (+2% YoY); cyclones impacted shipments by ~8Mt, with ~50% expected to be recovered (effects into early Q2).
  • Simandou: first full SimFer shipment delivered to China; first sales realised in April; Q1 crushed ore stockpile at mine gate 2.1Mt (100%); permanent crushing facilities still expected H2 2026; 30-month ramp to full production rates expected during H2 2028.
  • IOC (Canada): production -13% YoY (weather + mine/asset health challenges); sales -3% YoY.
  • Aluminium value chain resilient: bauxite 13.3Mt (-11% YoY) (Weipa/Gove weather + Cyclone Narelle), alumina +6% YoY (QAL now included at 100%), aluminium 0.84Mt (+1% YoY).
  • US tariffs: total RTA shipments to US destination 273kt (Q4: 303kt); total RTA tariff cost $362m (Q4: $416m); average realised tariff cost $1,327/t (Q4: $1,371/t).
  • Lithium: LCE production 12.7kt (YoY not directly comparable due to Arcadium acquisition timing; Mt Cattlin now in care & maintenance); Fenix 1B and Sal de Vida mechanically complete with first production on track for H2 2026.
  • 2026 production/sales and unit cost guidance unchanged, including Pilbara FOB unit cash costs $23.5–$25.0/wmt and copper C1 net unit costs 65–75c/lb.
  • Exploration & evaluation expense $180m (Q1 2025: $141m), including spend linked to Resolution land exchange.
  • Productivity: $650m of annualised benefits fully implemented by March; “substantially more underway”.
  • Resolution Copper: “historic land exchange” completed; drilling now underway; planning to invest ~$500m over two years (Rio share $275m) for enabling works/community funding/infrastructure/initial underground development and ~100 jobs.
  • Portfolio actions: actively “testing the market” for Borates business and evaluating options for Rio Tinto Iron & Titanium.
  • Capital projects: AP60 low-carbon smelter achieved first hot metal in March 2026; gross project costs up ~$122m to ~$1.5bn (construction productivity issues). Nemaska (Bécancour) construction pace slowed during 2026 (optimisation), while stating “no major changes” to overall timeline anticipated.
  • Middle East conflict: “direct impacts… limited” so far; higher input prices (incl. diesel) noted; “limited visibility” on supply-chain impacts in H2; Pilbara unit cost sensitivity from May: each $10/bbl oil move → ~$0.15/t impact (subject to FX/inflation).

• PHP noted that Richard Howell (PHP CFO) has resigned as an independent non-executive director of Life Science REIT plc, effective 20 April 2026.
  • The resignation followed the conclusion of the Scheme of Arrangement between Life Science REIT plc and British Land plc (announced earlier the same day).

• CEO Paul Bassi bought 204,035 shares at an average 30.38p, for GBP61,976.
  • Post-purchase holding: Bassi now holds 18.4m shares, a 10.7% stake.

• Chief Risk Officer Chris Knight sold 200,000 shares at GBP2.55 for GBP490,800 (1 April).
  • Knight sold an additional 82,612 shares at GBP2.61 for GBP215,535 (9 April).
  • The announcement also notes Knight sold 100,000 shares in late March.

• Sales & Marketing Director James Roper sold 30,000 shares at 344p, for GBP103,200.
  • Post-sale holding: Roper retains 994,035 shares (reported as a 0.0% stake).

• Barclays downgraded Hunting to ‘underweight’ (from equal weight).
  • Barclays raised its price target to 600p (from 550p), a +9.1% increase.
  • Rationale: Hunting viewed as one of the “highest-rated stocks in our coverage universe” after Barclays cut its earnings outlook.
  • Forecast changes: Barclays cut 2026–2028 EBITDA forecasts by 3%–5%.
  • Valuation point: Barclays notes Hunting is around 16x 2026E P/E, making it among the highest-rated names in its coverage.
  • Performance noted: stock up 30%+ YTD, broadly in line with the European, Middle East & Africa Oil & Gas Index (per Barclays).
  • Barclays’ positives despite the downgrade: “Its Middle East exposure is relatively limited and it could be a beneficiary of a US rebound if that occurs. In addition, its Subsea business appears to be going well and the early signs of adoption for its Organic Oil Recovery product are encouraging.”
  • Valuation method detail: Barclays bases valuation on 15x 2027E P/E and notes this requires lifting the terminal margin in its reverse DCF to 2025–2026 levels versus 2023–2024 levels previously used.

time_period: FY 2026 (year ending September 2026); trading update includes H1 FY26
body:
• CEO succession: CEO John-Paul Savant to stand down after a “short orderly handover period”; successor search at an advanced stage; further announcement “in due course”.
  • Chair Scott Forbes said the company is looking for a new CEO “with a strong commercial and operational track record as well as considerable marketplace, e-commerce and public company leadership experience”.
  • Chair said company confident of maintaining “good strategic and trading momentum” during transition.
  • Outlook reaffirmed for FY 2026: pro forma constant-currency revenue growth 4%–5%.
  • FY 2026 margin guidance reaffirmed: adjusted EBITDA margin 34.5%–35.5%.
  • Phasing: revenue growth expected to be more weighted to the first half.
  • H1 revenue expectation: expects to report USD125m revenue in H1; Q2 trading remains positive after “good momentum” in Q1.
  • H1 pro forma constant-currency revenue growth expected to be close to 8%.
  • H1 adjusted EBITDA said to be in line with expectations.
  • Cost actions: further cost cuts in H1 to provide a small benefit to margins in H2 and improve margins in FY 2027.
  • Reiterated FY 2026 expectations: adjusted EBITDA USD83.1m–USD85.6m on revenue USD239.8m–USD244.9m.
  • Implied growth vs FY 2025: FY 2026 revenue guidance implies +26.1% to +28.8% vs FY 2025 revenue of USD190.2m; EBITDA guidance implies +8.2% to +11.5% vs FY 2025 adjusted EBITDA of USD76.8m.
  • CEO quote: “We continue to focus on our strategy for extending our leading position in the curated second-hand goods market. ATG has delivered good trading and pro forma growth in H1 26, and I'm particularly pleased to report our progress on driving [arts & antiques gross merchandise value] which has been a focus area,”.
  • Share price move: shares down 4.7% to 373.4p; stock down 34% over the last 12 months.
  • Takeover context: in February, FitzWalter Capital decided against a firm offer after its 400p per share proposal was rejected by the board.

• Q1 2026 revenue rose 18% YoY to USD242.1m (Q1 2025: USD205.8m).
  • Performance attributed to “strong customer activity across both over-the-counter and non-OTC businesses, amid heightened market volatility”.
  • Q1 2026 EBITDA rose 2.0% YoY to USD95.7m (USD93.8m).
  • New customers increased 48% YoY.
  • Active customers increased 21% YoY.
  • Average revenue per customer declined 2.7% YoY.
  • Customer income rose 53% YoY to a five-year record high of USD270.6m (USD176.3m).
  • Guidance: expects 2026 revenue and EBITDA to be ahead of current market expectations, cited as USD779.3m revenue and USD360.4m EBITDA.
  • Context: FY 2025 reported USD792.4m revenue and USD348.1m EBITDA.
  • Share price reaction: shares fell 0.9% to 4,416p; shares up 47% over the last 12 months.
  • CEO David Zruia said: “The group delivered an excellent performance in the quarter, with strong growth across key financial and operational metrics.”
  • Expects to announce additional strategic partnerships across its B2B futures and prediction markets businesses in the short to medium term.
  • Product launch: launched a US B2C prediction markets product in February.

• In accordance with UK Listing Rule 6.4.9, the company confirmed that independent non-executive director Martyn Coffey will be appointed Independent Non-Executive Director and Chair Designate of Stelrad Group plc.
  • Appointment is effective from 1 May 2026.

• Blanket Mine gold production: 14,767 oz vs 18,671 oz in Q1 2025 (-20.9% YoY).
  • Lower quarter was anticipated due to mining sequence and expected access constraints to higher grade / higher volume areas.
  • Operational impacts in the quarter: equipment availability issues and challenging ground conditions (constraining access to planned ore sources).
  • New mine shift system being implemented to move production from 6 days/week to 7 days/week.
  • Contractor appointed to accelerate access to higher-grade ore sources.
  • Milling capacity expected to increase following commissioning of an additional ball mill in Q2 2026.
  • Management remains comfortable with full-year production guidance of 72,000–76,500 oz; production expected to be weighted to H2 2026.
  • Processing performance: 202,217 tonnes milled with “good operational availability across the processing circuit”.
  • CEO Mark Learmonth comment (full quote): “Gold production in Q1 2026 was below our expectations, reflecting the lower grades mined during the Quarter; however, this is consistent with our guidance of production being weighted towards the second half of the year. Pleasingly, plant performance remained strong, with 202,217 tonnes milled and good operational availability across the processing circuit. This is an important reflection of our continued investment into the future of the processing facility. "The Quarter also included several typical operating challenges associated with deep, mature mining assets, including sequencing considerations, equipment downtime and difficult ground conditions in certain areas. These factors temporarily constrained access to some planned ore sources. "We are actively addressing these challenges through several practical measures, including the acceleration of mine development, supported by the appointment of a contractor, to improve access to planned ore sources. "Importantly, we will be introducing a revised shift system later in the year, moving the operation to a seven-day working week (up from six), which we expect will both reduce worker fatigue and support increased ore production. "Alongside these mining initiatives, we are progressing further plant improvements, including an increase in milling capacity following the commissioning of an additional ball mill. Taken together, these actions are expected to support a stronger production profile in the second half of the year and into 2027. "The challenges experienced in the first quarter do not reflect the underlying quality of the orebody or the long-term fundamentals of the operation. Blanket remains a resilient, cash-generative asset, supported by a capable and committed workforce and a processing plant that continues to perform reliably and where we are growing capacity. Management's focus is on stabilising mining areas, improving equipment availability and restoring access to planned ore sources as we progress through the year.”

time_period: Announced 20 April 2026 (vessel sale expected later April 2026; funding runway guidance through end-Aug 2026)
body:
• Agreed sale of owned transhipping vessel “Iron Destiny” for US$7.7m net cash consideration (subject to delivery and customary closing conditions; expected to complete later this month).
  • Rationale for disposal: constrained ability to use vessel since start of the war; continued monthly maintenance and sustaining capex to keep seaworthy; uncertainty on timing of end of war; vessel approaching end of useful life; sale provides immediate cash injection and removes ongoing maintenance / future capex burden.
  • Net accessible cash runway: including expected proceeds from Iron Destiny sale, Ferrexpo estimates runway extended from at least end-June 2026 to approximately end-August 2026 (subject to iron ore price volatility, opex including energy costs, operating conditions, potential actions by the insolvency manager at FPM, and outcomes of ongoing proceedings).
  • Funding options: Board says an equity capital raise is currently the most viable solution within the required timeframe (after discussions with existing and certain potential new institutional investors).
  • Possible Fundraise: likely structured as a conditional placing of new shares to institutional investors to raise a minimum of US$100m to support working capital and short-term operational requirements while operating at a reduced level for the next 18 months.
  • Fundraise conditionality: would require shareholder resolutions at a general meeting to be convened “as early as possible” following launch.
  • Prospectus: company does not intend to publish a prospectus in connection with any Possible Fundraise.
  • Largest shareholder engagement: Fevamotinico S.a.r.l. (holding 49.32% of ordinary shares in issue, excluding treasury shares) is in discussions regarding supporting resolutions and potential participation, given dilution considerations; discussions ongoing.
  • Uncertainty statement: “there can be no certainty” the group will progress a fundraise or an alternative funding solution within a suitable timeframe, or at all.

time_period: Scheme effective 20 April 2026 (record time 17 April 2026; admission/cancellation 21 April 2026; settlement by 4 May 2026)
body:
• The Scheme of Arrangement to acquire Life Science REIT by British Land became Effective on 20 April 2026 following registration of the Court Order; British Land now owns the entire issued and to be issued share capital of Life Science REIT.
  • Life Science REIT intended to be re-registered as a private limited company shortly after the announcement date.
  • Scheme Record Time: 6:00 p.m. on 17 April 2026.
  • Consideration per Scheme Share (for holders on the register at the Scheme Record Time): 0.07 New British Land Shares + 14.1 pence cash.
  • New British Land Shares issuance: application for admission of 24,500,000 New British Land Shares; expected admission to trading 8:00 a.m. on 21 April 2026.
  • CREST settlement timing: New British Land Shares expected to be credited on or as soon as possible after 8:00 a.m. on 21 April 2026, but no later than 4 May 2026.
